title: Taking offence and incivility in the Omani society
abstract:
This study investigated what causes individuals in Omani society to take offence, and the norms that govern the perception of incivility in Oman. Previous research on (im)politeness has predominantly focused on the speaker’s role in causing, often overlooking the hearer’s evaluative perspective in taking offence. Moreover, recent research that has focused on the hearer’s perspective has investigated taking offence through a moral lens. While this approach captures an important evaluative dimension, not all instances of offence are morally anchored. Therefore, this study argues that offences also arise from perceived violations of sociocultural norms and expectations.

While the notion of taking offence has recently gained attention, incivility remains relatively underexplored within (im)politeness research. Most existing work on incivility has emerged from fields such as sociology, political science, criminology, and organisational psychology, where the focus has been on identifying and classifying uncivil behaviours and examining their consequences. These studies prioritise the act itself rather than exploring the evaluative processes through which lay individuals interpret certain behaviours as uncivil. However, more recent work in sociology and (im)politeness, particularly by Horgan (2020, 2024) has shifted the focus towards how ordinary people evaluate mundane behaviours through a moral lens, framing incivility as a form of moral transgression. This idea is beginning to have a greater impact on (im)politeness theory per se, as reflected in the works of Haugh ( 2022) and Parvaresh ( 2022). While this framing captures an important aspect of judgement, this study argues that incivility, just like taking offence, is best understood in the Omani context as a perceived transgression of sociocultural norms and expectations.

To investigate how taking offence and incivility are evaluated as perceived transgression of sociocultural norms and expectations, this study adopted an explanatory sequential mixed-methods design consisting of two phases. In the first phase, data was collected from X (formerly known as Twitter) to gain an initial understanding of the two phenomena using specific hashtags and keywords in the Arabic language, such as # offended and # uncivil. The data was first analysed using corpus analysis, which focused on identifying linguistic structures associated with the expression of taking offence and incivility using collocations, n-grams, and frequency analysis. The second phase focused on capturing individuals’ personal experiences of taking offence and incivility to deepen the understanding of the phenomena.

The findings revealed that both offence and incivility in Omani society are shaped by violations of deeply embedded sociocultural norms, with evaluation rooted in the perception of unmet expectations. Notably, offence as expressed in the X data was less about individual hurt and more about collective disapproval. The corpus analysis revealed that offence was articulated using collective terms such as ‘we’, ‘Omanis’, ‘our’, and ‘people’, indicating that individuals positioned themselves as speaking on behalf of a broader social group rather than from a purely individual perspective, thereby framing offence as a shared concern. The main concern underlying these expressions was the perceived violation of social identity face, particularly through acts that were seen to disrespect and misrepresent Omanis. Diary report data, on the other hand, highlighted offence as a more personal and relational experience, rooted in failures to meet expectations of maʿrūf (favour), iḥtirām (deference) based on social role, and equity rights.

Incivility in both X and diary reports, in contrast, was judged as a violation of sociocultural norms and those governing public behaviour, with evaluations most prominently anchored in expectations tied to social identity face, even when the acts themselves varied, ranging from littering and immodest dress to mistreating expatriates. At the same time, the analysis highlighted that a single behaviour could give rise to multiple judgements depending on the normative lens through which it was assessed. Littering; for example, was viewed as a violation of religious norms, a threat to social identity face, and a failure to uphold communal responsibility.

This study contributes to (im)politeness research and sociological research by highlighting how offence and incivility are evaluated through sociocultural norms and expectations, drawing on both online discourse and lived experiences within Omani society.

ABSTRACT 
 
This study investigated what causes individuals in Omani society to take offence, and the 
norms that govern the perception of incivility in Oman. Previous research on (im)politeness 
has predominantly focused on the speaker’s role in causing, often overlooking the hearer’s 
evaluative perspective in taking offence. Moreover, recent research that has focused on 
the hearer’s perspective has investigated taking offence through a moral lens. While this 
approach captures an important evaluative dimension, not all instances of offence are 
morally anchored. Therefore, this study argues that offences also arise from perceived 
violations of sociocultural norms and expectations. 
While the notion of taking offence has recently gained attention, incivility remains relatively 
underexplored within (im)politeness research. Most existing work on incivility has emerged 
from fields such as sociology, political science, criminology, and organisational psychology, 
where the focus has been on identifying and classifying uncivil behaviours and examining 
their consequences. These studies prioritise the act itself rather than exploring the 
evaluative processes through which lay individuals interpret certain behaviours as uncivil. 
However, more recent work in sociology and (im)politeness, particularly by Horgan (2020, 
2024) has shifted the focus towards how ordinary people evaluate mundane behaviours 
through a moral lens, framing incivility as a form of moral transgression. This idea is 
beginning to have a greater impact on (im)politeness theory per se, as reflected in the 
works of Haugh ( 2022) and Parvaresh ( 2022). While this framing captures an important 
aspect of judgement, this study argues that incivility, just like taking offence, is best 
understood in the Omani context as a perceived transgression of sociocultural norms and 
expectations. 
To investigate how taking offence and incivility are evaluated as perceived transgression 
of sociocultural norms and expectations, this study adopted an explanatory sequential 
mixed-methods design consisting of two phases. In the first phase, data was collected 
from X (formerly known as Twitter) to gain an initial understanding of the two phenomena 
using specific hashtags and keywords in the Arabic language, such as # offended and # 
uncivil. The data was first analysed using corpus analysis, which focused on identifying 
linguistic structures associated with the expression of taking offence and incivility using 
collocations, n-grams, and frequency analysis. The second phase focused on capturing 
individuals’ personal experiences of taking offence and incivility to deepen the 
understanding of the phenomena. 
The findings revealed that both offence and incivility in Omani society are shaped by 
violations of deeply embedded sociocultural norms, with evaluation rooted in the 
perception of unmet expectations. Notably, offence as expressed in the X data was less 
about individual hurt and more about collective disapproval. The corpus analysis revealed 
that offence was articulated using collective terms such as ‘we’, ‘Omanis’, ‘our’, and 
‘people’, indicating that individuals positioned themselves as speaking on behalf of a 
broader social group rather than from a purely individual perspective, thereby framing 
offence as a shared concern. The main concern underlying these expressions was the 
perceived violation of social identity face, particularly through acts that were seen to 
disrespect and misrepresent Omanis. Diary report data, on the other hand, highlighted 
iii 
 
offence as a more personal and relational experience, rooted in failures to meet 
expectations of maʿrūf (favour), iḥtirām (deference) based on social role, and equity rights. 
Incivility in both X and diary reports, in contrast, was judged as a violation of sociocultural 
norms and those governing public behaviour, with evaluations most prominently anchored 
in expectations tied to social identity face, even when the acts themselves varied, ranging 
from littering and immodest dress to mistreating expatriates. At the same time, the analysis 
highlighted that a single behaviour could give rise to multiple judgements depending on 
the normative lens through which it was assessed. Littering; for example, was viewed as 
a violation of religious norms, a threat to social identity face, and a failure to uphold 
communal responsibility. 
This study contributes to (im)politeness research and sociological research by highlighting 
how offence and incivility are evaluated through sociocultural norms and expectations, 
drawing on both online discourse and lived experiences within Omani society. 
Keywords: taking offence, incivility, norms and expectations, inappropriate, uncivil, offensive

1 
 
CHAPTER 1: INTRODUCTION 
 
 
1.1 Introduction 
The purpose of this thesis is to investigate taking offence and incivility in the Omani society, focusing 
on the underlying sociocultural norms and expectations that shape individuals’ judgements. To 
situate this research, Section 1.2 offers both background and conceptual grounding for the study of 
taking offence; it reviews key literature and also identifies the existing gaps, as well as how this 
research seeks to address them. Section 1.3, in a similar manner, provides background to the study 
of incivility, outlining relevant interdisciplinary perspectives. It also identifies the existing gaps in the 
literature and explains how the present study contributes to filling the current gaps. After introducing 
the two main research focuses, the gaps associated with them in the current literature, and how the 
present research aims to address these gaps, the chapter proceeds to outline the research questions 
and their objectives in section (1.4). Then, Section 1.5 outlines the methodological approach 
employed, followed by the research contribution in Section 1.6. Lastly, Section 1.7 provides an 
overview of the thesis structure. 
1.2 Background to the Study of Taking Offence 
In the recent decade, attention has been drawn to the notions of causing and taking offence (see 
Haugh, 2015; Parvaresh & Tayebi 2018, 2020; Tayebi, 2016). Nevertheless, greater attention has 
been given to the former than the latter despite its evident importance for (im)politeness research. 
The notion of offence, in fact, is situated at the “core of the present models of (im)politeness” (Haugh, 
2015, p.2). However, many studies have focused on the act of causing offence, i.e., the speaker, whose 
conduct is framed as a transgression and regarded as potentially offensive to the other party, thereby 
leaving the role of the hearer out of the bigger picture. It is recognised that being (im)polite does not 
always mean that one has caused offence, nor does it mean that the person has taken offence 
(Haugh, 2010). The fact that offence might occur whether the language used is polite or (im)polite 
leads us to question what makes people take offence in the first place and what is considered offensive. 
Weckert (2007) maintains that offence is “some sort of hurt or pain, displeasure” (p.26). Barrow (2005, 
as cited in Evans, Jeffries, O’Driscoll, 2019 ) refers to the word offensive as an action or talk that is 
irritating, annoying or anger-provoking. While these definitions highlight the emotional dimension of 
taking offence, Haugh (2015) and Culpeper (2011) expanded the understanding of offence to include 
an evaluative aspect whereby offence involves a judgement of perceived transgression. Building on 
2 
 
this broader view, Parvaresh (2022) definess offence as an “emotionally bad feeling created by what 
is perceived to be a transgression of (social) expectation and (moral) norms” (p.62), drawing on the 
work of Haugh (2015) and Culpeper (2011). In this study, however, this definition of offence is 
adapted to focus specifically on sociocultural norms and expectations, that is, the socially and 
culturally shared understanding of acceptable conduct. Therefore, taking offence as a whole is 
defined as the experience of feeling emotionally bad when an individual perceives that their 
sociocultural norms and expectations have been transgressed. The role of “moral norms” in the 
evaluation of perceived transgression, as emphasised in Parvaresh’s (2022, p.62) definition, is 
recognised in this study but is approached differently; this point will be addressed later in this chapter, 
specifically when discussing the evaluative judgments. 
 Building on this definition, taking offence usually involves two key acts: registering and sanctioning 
offence (Haugh, 2015). When registering offence, the person takes an affective stance which 
encompasses negative emotions like annoyance, hurt or anger (Culpeper, 2011). Sanctioning 
offence, on the other hand, involves taking a moral stance towards an act perceived as transgressive 
or an affront. In this sense, offence is more than ‘feeling bad’; it is a process of evaluating and grounding 
one’s moral claims that a particular action is offensive, often drawing on a range of norms, some 
rooted in social and cultural practices, and others anchored in morality (Tayebi, 2016; Parvaresh, 
2022; Haugh, 2015). The moral claims are shaped by what is referred to as the moral order as Davis 
(2008) defines as “any system of obligations that defines and organises the proper-good, right, 
virtuous- relations among individuals and groups in a community”. Domenici and Littlejohn (2006, 
cited in Culpeper, 2011, p.38) similarly describe the moral order as a “socially constructed set of 
understandings we carry with us from situation to situation’’. From a sociological perspective, 
Garfinkel (1964, p.225) describes the moral order as “the rule-governed activities of everyday life” 
including the familiar, taken for granted. The moral order thus provides the normative background 
against which evaluations of offence are made. However, how this moral order is instantiated can 
vary not only across different social and cultural groups but also within the same group, resulting in 
variability in perceptions of offence (Spencer-Oatey and Kádár, 2016). This variability in interpretation 
contributes to the inherently subjective nature of taking offence (Barrow, 2005, as cited in Evans, 
Jeffries, & O’Driscoll, 2019; Tayebi, 2016; Haugh, 2015). Barrow (2005) argues that feeling offended 
is a subjective experience, as individuals may react differently to the same act, even within the same 
society. As Tayebi (2016) notes, such differences can stem from varying schemata and lived 
 
3 
 
 experiences, which shape how individuals perceive and evaluate behaviour. Building on this, 
Spencer-Oatey and Kádár (2021) explain that when individuals in interactions have significantly 
different cognitive schemata, formed through their unique backgrounds and experiences, there is a 
higher likelihood of mismatched expectations. This mismatch can influence how people behave and 
how they evaluate the behaviour of others, contributing further to the variability in the judgement of 
what is offensive. Nevertheless, this variability does not negate the presence of a shared meaning 
system (Fischer and Schwartz, 2011). Through socialisation and repeated exposure, individuals 
develop a sense of what constitutes normal or acceptable behaviour in particular contexts and then 
develop expectations around it (Spencer-Oatey & Žegarac, 2018). As such, while individuals may 
differ in how they evaluate a given behaviour, their evaluations are still shaped by broader shared 
patterns of meaning (Spencer-Oatey & Kádár, 2021). 
Given this understanding that evaluation of offence is both inherently subjective and yet shaped by 
a broader system of shared meaning, a key question that has emerged in recent research is: What 
moral grounds do individuals invoke when evaluating certain behaviour as offensive? Several studies 
have sought to address this by examining perceived breaches in encounters, by focusing on 
interpersonal concerns such as face and sociality rights. Culpeper et al. (2011), for instance, 
examined how people from different countries evaluate (im)politeness in social interaction. 
Nonetheless, despite drawing from several countries, they did not delve into culturally specific 
interpretations of offence, and their focus remained limited to verbal interaction. Tayebi (2016), on 
the other hand, examined how interpersonal expectations shape evaluations of offence among 
Persian speakers, however, her work remains limited to one sociocultural context. More research is 
needed to understand how offence is evaluated in other Middle Eastern societies, including Oman.  
While earlier studies investigated taking offence through the lens of different types of face and 
sociality rights and interpersonal expectations ( see Culpeper et al. 2011, Tayebi, 2016), a more 
recent shift observed within impoliteness studies has foregrounded the role of morality in evaluative 
judgements. Xia (2018, p.206) refers to this shift as a “ moral turn”, which refers to a growing scholarly 
focus on how individuals ground their evaluations in “higher-level” moral norms to frame and 
legitimise their judgements (Haidt & Joseph, 2004; Gouldner, 1960, p.170). More recently, research 
on taking offence has begun to adopt this moral perspective, emphasising how individuals appeal to 
shared moral values when evaluating perceived transgressions. For instance, Blitvich (2022, p.70) 
investigates how moral emotions underpin responses to offensive behaviour in the context of online  
 
4 
 
public shaming. Drawing on the concept of “ good moral panics”, she argues that public acts of 
shaming on digital platforms are often framed as morally justified, particularly in cases involving 
racism and mistreatment of children. Her study highlights how individuals appeal to broader moral 
frameworks such as fairness, harm, and care to legitimise their aggressive responses. Morality is 
thus positioned as a central organising principle in the evaluation of perceived offences. Similarly, 
Parvaresh (2022) examined how individuals sanction acts they perceive as offensive by appealing 
to broader moral norms. Drawing on X posts featuring the hashtag #offended, he demonstrates that 
taking offence often involves attributing blame to others for violating higher moral-order values such 
as honesty and fairness. In all of these studies, the focus is on how individuals frame perceived 
offences through appeals to moral values, positioning morality as a key driver in evaluative judgement. 
As Haugh, Kádár, and Reiter (2022) highlight the importance of this line of work, they maintain that 
“offence strongly correlates with understandings of morality and the morally loaded feelings of the 
participants and observers of such incidents” (p.119). Nevertheless, while the moral turn has 
undoubtedly contributed to our understanding of how the evaluation of offence can be framed through 
a moral lens, this research argues here that when morality is invoked, it is not always tied to the so-
called higher level moral norms. To explain, even when individuals appeal to moral values such as 
fairness in their responses to perceived offence, their evaluation is not necessarily grounded in 
universal moral principles. Instead, morality can be invoked as part of sociocultural embedded norms 
and expectations. In this way, morality operates less as an appeal to universal moral values and more 
as a reflection of locally shared values. Therefore, investigating offence primarily through the moral 
norms invoked may risk overlooking how the evaluation of offence can also be shaped by broader 
sociocultural embedded norms and expectations. Nevertheless, it is important to acknowledge that 
the data itself can guide the researcher in determining which evaluative grounds, whether appeals to 
moral norms, sociocultural norms, or both are most salient in participants evaluation of offence. 
 
In summary, although interest in taking offence has emerged in recent years, it remains relatively 
new within (im)politeness research, with earlier studies focusing predominantly on the act of causing 
offence rather than on taking offence. Moreover, although recent studies on taking offence have 
provided valuable insights, there remains a need for further research. To explain, since taking offence 
is a subjective experience, shaped by individuals’ unique backgrounds, lived experiences, and 
cognitive schemata (Barrow, 2005), there is still a need to explore how different communities 
experience and evaluate perceived offence , while also recognising that such evaluations are situated 
within broader shared meaning systems (Spencer-Oatey & Žegarac, 2018; Fischer & Schwartz, 
5 
 
2011). To date, much of the existing research has been conducted within Western contexts, with 
some exceptions, such as Tayebi’s (2016) study  on Persian speakers. However, there remains a 
lack of research examining offence evaluation within Arab settings, highlighting the need for more 
culturally diverse investigations. Additionally, the recent emphasis on morality in taking offence 
research, while offering important insights, has also contributed to widening the gap by directing 
attention primarily towards appeals to morality. As noted earlier, these risks overlooking the influence 
of locally embedded sociocultural norms and expectations in shaping evaluative judgement. 
Furthermore, beyond the above gaps identified in the existing literature, there is also a 
methodological gap. Studies on taking offence have relied either on online discourse or diary reports, 
with more recent research predominantly relying on online data. This separation may restrict the 
ability to capture the full range of how offence is experienced, both as publicly communicated in 
social media and as privately narrated in diary reports. Therefore, there is a need for research that 
adopts a more integrative approach combining naturally occurring public expression of offence with 
personal reflective accounts to provide a comprehensive understanding of the phenomenon. 
In order to address these gaps, the present study investigates how offence is evaluated within Omani 
society, with particular attention to the role of sociocultural norms and expectations in shaping the 
evaluative judgements. The study draws on two distinct sources of data, social media posts from X 
and diary report forms, to examine how individuals in Omani society evaluate perceived offence. By 
focusing on the evaluation of offence within an Arab, non-Western context, this study expands 
research on taking offence beyond the predominantly Western settings. Moreover, this study brings 
renewed attention to an earlier focus within (im)politeness research on socioculturally embedded 
norms and expectations, which has been underexplored in more recent studies on taking offence 
following the moral turn that emphasises higher level moral norms.  While acknowledging the role of 
morality in shaping individuals’ judgements, this research positions appeals to morality as part of the 
broader sociocultural meaning system rather than as appeals to universal moral norms. Furthermore, 
this study moves beyond the single-method approaches commonly used in previous studies on taking 
offence to collect data by combining naturally occurring online discourse with personal reflective 
accounts. In doing so, it offers a multidimensional perspective on how offence is experienced and 
evaluated in public and private contexts. Having established the background to the study of taking 
offence and outlined the key gaps addressed in this research, the following section turns to the 
phenomenon of incivility, which forms another key focus of this study. 
 
6 
 
1.3 Background to the Study of Incivility 
Incivility, both as a concept and as a social phenomenon, has emerged as another important, yet 
often overlooked, area of inquiry. While it has received increasing attention in fields such as political 
science (Papacharissi, 2004; Bormann et al., 2022), sociology (Phillips & Smith, 2006), and 
organisational psychology (Andersson & Pearson, 1999; Cortina, 2008), its relevance within 
(im)politeness research remains comparatively underexplored. Sifianou (2019) draws attention to 
this gap, noting that the relationship between incivility and impoliteness remains a neglected area 
despite their conceptual overlap. Although the concept of (in)civility has not been a central focus in 
(im)politeness research, a small number of scholars have engaged with it over the past two decades. 
Lakoff (2005) describes civility as “acting according to general societal tenets of appropriateness” 
(p.26), while Incivility reflects a disregard for those tenets. For Lakoff, incivility reflects a breakdown 
in shared expectations, often surfacing during periods of cultural or political change when traditional 
restraints no longer hold. Her account suggests that what is labelled as “uncivil” frequently indicates 
a deeper discomfort with those who disrupt the status quo or challenge dominant norms. Mills (2009, 
2017b) refers to Lakoff’s work as addressing “impoliteness at the cultural or societal level” arguing 
that contemporary judgements about (im)politeness in public discourse tends to be ideological rather 
than analytical (2009, p.1049). She maintains that the notion of unified “cultural norms” is in many 
ways “mythical” (p.1058), given the diversity of norms across communities of practice and the 
influence of power in shaping which norms are treated as dominant. More recently, Terkourafi et al. 
(2018) and Sifianou (2019) further developed this direction by examining incivility in online contexts, 
framing it as a discursive breach of expected communicative norms. Terkourafi et al. (2018) analyse 
a series of controversial tweets, demonstrating how implicated meanings can be misinterpreted or 
lost due to context collapse on social media. They argue that tweets, while often pragmatically 
complex, are evaluated in fast-paced public discourse according to surface-level cues, which 
increases the potential for them to be read as uncivil. Sifianou (2019), on the other hand, draws on 
an online Greek newspaper article and its associated reader comments to illustrate how incivility is 
constructed and evaluated in everyday discourse. While her analysis is brief, it highlights the public 
negotiation of what counts as rude or uncivil behaviour, and how such evaluations are shaped by 
cultural expectations, tone, and ideology. In her work, she underscores the need for more systematic 
investigation of incivility within (im)politeness research, particularly given its complex and context- 
sensitive nature. 
Yet, for all its complexity, a few common denominators unite different strands of incivility research  
7 
 
across disciplines: scholars largely agree that incivility is mostly approached as a violation of norms, 
whether these are social, cultural, political, communicative or organisational in nature (e.g., 
Muddiman, 2017 , Papacharissi, 2004; Lakoff, 2005; Phillips & Smith, 2006; Cortina, 2008; Sifianou, 
2019; Bormann et al., 2022). Moreover, the widely acknowledged commonality is that incivility is 
highly subjective, shaped by what individuals perceive as uncivil, meaning that incivility is not an 
objective quality of an utterance or action but an evaluation (e.g., Phillips & Smith, 2006, Andersson & 
Pearson, 1999; Stryker et al., 2021; Bormann et al., 2022). 
Despite these shared insights, what remains largely absent from the literature is detailed empirical 
research on how individuals themselves evaluate an act as uncivil, whether in online discourse, which 
has received the bulk of recent scholarly attention, or in mundane everyday encounters where such 
judgements also frequently occur (Phillips & Smith, 2006). In response to this gap, and echoing the 
recent moral turn in (im)politeness research, a similar evaluative shift has emerged in the field of 
urban sociology. Horgan (2020, 2024), for instance, explores how ordinary people interpret and 
morally judge breaches of expected conduct in public life, drawing on interview data about 
encounters with rude strangers in public spaces. He conceptualises these judgements as occurring 
within a broader “moral landscape” understood as “culturally available meanings organised around 
a basic sacred/profane binary” p.128. While Horgan’s account provides a valuable lens for 
understanding how incivility is evaluated in the public sphere, his notion of moral landscape tends to 
foreground the moral framework that individuals draw upon when evaluating something as uncivil. It 
highlights how participants often appeal to shared, seemingly universal moral values such as 
kindness and respect when making their judgements. However, the emphasis on morality may 
overlook the extent to which evaluations of incivility are shaped by sociocultural norms and context-
specific expectations. 
 
Nonetheless, despite Horgan’s (2020, 2024) emphasis on the moral dimension of the evaluative 
judgement, his work makes an important contribution by shifting the understanding of incivility 
towards the evaluative grounds upon which individuals draw to make sense of breaches in public 
conduct. However, while this study offers a valuable starting point, further research is needed to 
explore the full range of evaluative grounds individuals may draw upon to legitimise their judgements 
including the role of sociocultural embedded norms and expectations in shaping the judgement. 
 
Focusing on the grounds that participants allude to goes beyond simply identifying and categorising 
what individuals label as uncivil or which behaviours they perceive as inappropriate, which was the 
8 
 
case in previous research on incivility across the disciplines (e.g. Phillips & Smith, 2006). Instead, it 
involves examining the normative assumptions and interpretive frameworks (Garfinkel, 1967; 
Goffman, 1974; Spencer-Oatey & Žegarac, 2018) that shape how individuals perceive, interpret, and 
evaluate social behaviour as uncivil. These frameworks are grounded in schemata, which are 
internalised structures of knowledge and experience that guide individuals in interpreting behaviour. 
They help individuals anticipate how people ought to act in a given context, and when these 
expectations are breached, such behaviours may be perceived as uncivil or inappropriate. In this 
way, evaluations of incivility are closely tied to what Garfinkel (1967) refers to as the moral order, the 
background of shared “taken-for-granted” norms that underpin everyday interaction. When these 
norms are disrupted, participants draw upon both personal and culturally shaped frameworks to 
assess the severity and meaning of the act. Understanding incivility, thus, requires paying attention 
to the underlying social norms, cultural norms and expectations, and in some cases, moral norms, 
that shape how individuals judge certain behaviours as uncivil. 
 
To sum up what has been discussed so far, incivility has been largely neglected within the 
(im)politeness field. Moreover, much of the existing literature on incivility has focused on 
organisational or institutional settings particularly workplaces (e.g., Andersson & Pearson, 1999; 
Cortina, 2008) and political discourse (e.g., Papacharissi, 2004; Muddiman, 2017). These studies 
have tended to prioritise either the identification and classification of uncivil behaviours or the 
analysis of their broader implications. What remains underexplored is how ordinary individuals 
outside of institutional frameworks make sense of and evaluate incivility in everyday contexts. 
Nonetheless, earlier studies that did address everyday incivility, such as Phillips and Smith (2006), 
tended to focus on classifying behaviours considered uncivil. More recent research, however, has 
begun to explore the evaluative nature of incivility, shifting attention towards how individuals justify 
their judgements about what counts as uncivil. Yet, this body of work has largely focused on morality 
anchored grounds and has been situated within Western contexts. Further research is needed to 
uncover the broader range of evaluative grounds that shape these judgements across cultural 
settings. 
In response to these gaps, the present study investigates how incivility is evaluated within Omani 
society, an underrepresented context in (im)politeness research. It explores the broader evaluative 
grounds individuals draw upon, including sociocultural norms and context-specific expectations, 
when judging behaviour as uncivil. In order to do that, it draws on two different sources of data, X 
posts and personal diary reports, to capture how incivility is evaluated in public online discourse and 
9 
 
in private. By employing two forms of data collection, the study offers a multidimensional 
understanding of how incivility is perceived and evaluated in different contexts. 
Building on the discussion of the key phenomena introduced in this chapter, taking offence and 
incivility, the following section presents the research questions and objectives that guide these 
investigations. 
1.4 Research Questions and Objectives 
The aim of this study is to uncover the sociocultural norms and expectations that shape how 
individuals in Omani society judge behaviour as offensive and uncivil. To achieve this aim, the study 
is guided by two research questions, each addressing one of the core phenomena introduced in this 
chapter. To address the two questions, the study draws on two sources of data for each research 
question: data from X and diary reports. This combination enables an exploration of both public 
expressions and private reflections, allowing for a richer understanding of the evaluative grounds 
underpinning offence and incivility in the Omani context. The following sections introduce each 
question along with its focus: 
1.4.1 What causes the Omani people to take offence? 
This question is addressed in chapters 4 and 5. The aim is to investigate incidents that lead 
individuals in Omani society to feel offended and to understand the underlying norms and expectations 
that shape such responses. Offence is approached as a socially and culturally informed judgement 
rooted in a shared understanding of what is considered acceptable (Culpeper, 2011). By exploring 
both public discourse and personal accounts, the research seeks to uncover the ways in which 
individuals draw on these shared expectations when interpreting and responding to potentially 
offensive acts. In doing so, it highlights the dynamic relationship between individual experience and 
broader social norms in constructing and expressing offence. 
1.4.2 What norms govern perceptions of (in)civility in the Omani society? 
This question is explored in Chapters 6 and 7. The aim is to understand the norms and expectations 
that individuals in Omani society draw upon when judging certain behaviour as uncivil. Incivility, in 
this study, is approached as a perceived transgression of sociocultural norms that govern appropriate 
conduct in public places, often marked by emotionally charged reactions. This definition builds on the 
view that incivility is a socially and culturally constructed evaluation, shaped by widely held beliefs 
about how people ought to behave in shared spaces (Locher & Watts, 2005; Kádár & Haugh, 2013). 
The study examines how people describe and interpret uncivil encounters, paying attention to how 
10 
 
these evaluations reflect collective understandings of public conduct. While individuals may differ in 
what they consider uncivil, their interpretations often draw on shared cultural standards that define 
acceptable behaviour within a given social setting (Spencer-Oatey, 2008). By analysing both digital 
expressions and lived experiences of incivility, this study sheds light on how certain norms are 
invoked, negotiated, and sometimes contested in everyday life, revealing the dynamic and context-
sensitive nature of individuals’ judgements. 
1.5 Methodology 
To address the two research questions, this study adopts a two-phase mixed methods design. Each 
phase investigates taking offence and incivility separately but in parallel, using the same 
methodological structure for both phenomena. Phase one starts with collecting naturally occurring 
data from X using a set of hashtags in Arabic language that are relevant for each phenomenon such 
as # مسيء (offensive) for taking offence, and #الئق  غير (inappropriate) for incivility. It is then analysed first 
quantitatively and then qualitatively. In the quantitative analysis, the study uses corpus-based 
techniques to identify linguistic patterns and recurring themes, which then inform the qualitative 
thematic analysis. The thematic analysis in Phase one examines how the identified patterns are 
interpreted and evaluated within their specific social and cultural contexts. In preparation for Phase 
Two, the findings from Phase One play a crucial role in shaping the design of this phase, particularly 
by guiding the wording and sequencing of the diary questions, as well as the depth of responses 
sought. 
In Phase Two, the study focuses on the lived experiences of Omanis by collecting qualitative data 
through diary reports. Participants are invited to recount situations in which they, or someone they 
know, felt offended or witnessed behaviour they perceived as uncivil. By incorporating this phase, 
this study extends the inquiry from public discourse into private accounts. The collected narratives 
are then analysed thematically to further explore the grounds that individuals draw upon when 
perceiving something as offensive or uncivil and how their evaluations reflect broader sociocultural 
norms within Omani society. A more detailed account of the research design, data collection 
procedures, and analytical approaches is provided in Chapter 3. 
1.6 Thesis contribution 
This thesis contributes to the field of (im)politeness studies by addressing two key phenomena: taking 
offence and incivility. While taking offence has received some attention within the field of 
(im)politeness, incivility remains comparatively underexplored in this field. In fact, it has been more 
extensively examined in disciplines such as sociology, political science, organisational psychology 
11 
 
and criminology. Thus, this research addresses a significant gap in the (im)politeness research by 
foregrounding incivility as a noteworthy phenomenon and a concept that deserves equal scholarly 
attention alongside other well-established concepts in the field, such as offence, aggression, and 
rudeness. 
Moreover, a key contribution of this study is its departure from the recent trend in (im)politeness 
research, particularly the moral turn, by refocusing the analytical attention on sociocultural norms 
and expectations that participants draw upon when judging behaviour as offensive or uncivil. While 
this study does not deny the role of morality in shaping evaluative judgements, it situates morality 
within a broader sociocultural understanding that guides how individuals evaluate behaviour. This 
perspective highlights how judgements of offence and incivility are socially and culturally mediated, 
grounded in locally shared norms rather than higher-level moral norms. 
Furthermore, this research contributes to (im)politeness research by investigating the two 
phenomena from a Middle Eastern perspective, specifically Omani society, a regional context that 
remains significantly underrepresented in (im)politeness research. By investigating how individuals 
in Oman interpret and evaluate offence and incivility, this study broadens existing findings and offers 
new culturally situated insights to (im)politeness research. 
Methodologically, this study also contributes to (im)politeness research by building an integrated, two 
phases mixed- methods design that can serve as a model for future studies. By combining corpus 
analysis and thematic analysis within the same dataset in Phase One and complementing this with 
thematic analysis of diary reports in Phase Two, the study demonstrates a systematic approach that 
can be applied to investigate similar phenomena of an evaluative nature. 
1.7 Structure of the Thesis 
This thesis is structured into nine chapters, each contributing to a comprehensive examination of 
taking offence and incivility in Oman. The current chapter, Chapter 1, introduces the study by outlining 
its scope and focus. It presents the gaps of the current research and how these gaps will be 
addressed, followed by the research questions and objectives. Additionally, it provides an overview 
of the methodology and highlights the potential contributions of this research. 
Chapter 2 reviews the key literature and concepts underpinning this study. It begins by tracing early 
ideas of proper conduct in various cultural and philosophical traditions and then moves to discussing 
how politeness has been theorised across different waves. The chapter then examines the concept 
of (im)politeness in relation to offence and incivility, followed by a discussion of norms. It concludes 
12 
 
with a review of existing studies on taking offence and incivility. 
Chapter 3 presents the methodological framework of the study. It begins by restating the research 
questions and outlining the philosophical underpinnings of the research. The chapter then explains 
the use of an explanatory sequential mixed-methods approach, combining quantitative and 
qualitative methods across two phases. It highlights that Phase One involves both corpus-based and 
thematic analyses of social media data from X, while Phase Two focuses on qualitative diary reports 
that capture personal narratives of offence and incivility. The chapter also critically details the data 
collection methods and analysis procedures for each phase, including the use of Sketch Engine and 
NVivo. It also discusses the strategies employed to recruit participants. It also presents the theoretical 
framework that guided the thematic analysis and discusses the translation process and ethical 
considerations associated with collecting data.  
Chapter 4 answers the first research question in this research: What causes Omani people to take 
offence? This chapter focuses on the findings from Phase One, which involves the analysis of 
naturally occurring data from X. The analysis in this chapter was conducted in two layers. The first 
layer employed quantitative corpus-based techniques using Sketch Engine to identify frequent lexical 
patterns, collocations, and n-grams in relation to the hashtag. The findings were then used to inform 
the second layer, which was a qualitative thematic analysis. This analysis explored the specific 
grounds that participants allude to when evaluating behaviour as offensive. This chapter then ends 
with a summary of the findings from the corpus analysis and thematic analysis. 
Chapter 5 continues to answer the first research question by focusing on diary reports narratives. 
The findings are analysed thematically providing an insight into the grounds they allude to when 
evaluating something as offensive. 
Chapter 6 addresses the second research question: What norms govern perceptions of (in)civility in 
Omani society? This chapter focuses on public evaluations of incivility as expressed in digital 
discourse on X. Following a similar two-layered approach to that used in Chapter 4, the analysis 
begins with a corpus-based analysis of lexical patterns, collocations, and frequently occurring 
references to behaviours perceived as uncivil. These trends are then explored through thematic 
analysis, revealing how users frame and interpret acts of incivility in an online context. Most 
importantly, it highlights the grounds that participants allude to legitimise their judgements. 
Chapter 7 continues the exploration of the second research question by presenting findings from the 
diary report data that focus on incivility. These personal narratives provide insight into how individuals 
13 
 
in Omani society perceive, interpret uncivil behaviour in everyday contexts. Through thematic 
analysis, the chapter uncovers the sociocultural norms and expectations that were violated and the 
underlying grounds they allude to in order to legitimise their judgements. 
Chapter 8 provides a comprehensive discussion of the key research findings by interpreting them in 
relation to previous literature. 
Chapter 9 synthesises the key findings in relation to the two research questions. It then highlights 
how this study contributed to the field theoretically, empirically and methodologically. Finally, it 
highlights the limitations of the study and provides recommendations for future research. 
14 
 
CHAPTER 2: LITERATURE REVIEW 
 
 
2.1 Introduction 
This chapter reviews the key theoretical and empirical literature and concepts that inform the present 
study. It begins with a historical overview (Section 2.2), tracing early ideas of proper conduct in 
ancient China, Greece, and Renaissance Europe. Section 2.3 then outlines earlier theories and key 
developments in (im)politeness research, including the shift from traditional models to discursive and 
post-discursive approaches. Section 2.4, then connects (im)politeness to taking offence and incivility.  
Sections 2.5 to 2.7 then explore the role of norms and review empirical studies on offence and 
incivility. 
2.2  Tracing Cultural and Philosophical views of Proper Conduct 
The interest in appropriate conduct has long been embedded in sociocultural and philosophical 
discussions of social behaviour, dating back to ancient civilizations such as those in Ancient Egypt, 
Greece, and China, which existed more than 2,500 years ago (Terkourafi, 2011; Pan & Kadar, 2011). 
Although the specific terminology varied across these cultures, the idea of behaving in ways deemed 
‘proper’ or ‘appropriate’ permeated their social systems, expressed through concepts that captured 
the essence of expected social conduct.  
In ancient China, the concept of ‘Li’ or “ritual propriety”, outlined by Confucius, provided a 
comprehensive framework for behaviour that spanned both grand ceremonies and daily interactions. 
Described as “patterns of ritualised, ceremonially proper conduct for various social roles in different 
social situations, ranging from mundane everyday contexts… to major events” ( Fraser, 2023, p100), 
Li encapsulated a broad spectrum of rituals. These rituals, also referred to as norms of propriety and 
duty, specify what should be done and how it should be carried out, including the manner of speech, 
dress, facial expression, gesture, and posture. They included both clearly defined rules and more 
nuanced, unwritten expectations that subtly guided interpersonal dynamics. For example, formal 
rituals might dictate specific ceremonial actions, while informal norms shape routine social 
interactions, such as personal space management in public settings. This system of norms served 
to place “measures and bounds” on individuals’ conduct based on their role in society (Yan, 2019, p. 
78), thereby aiming to prevent conflicts among people. If everyone adheres to the norms of propriety 
and duty associated with their place in society, i.e. collective conformity, a sustainable balance is 
achieved. While ancient China emphasized Li as a framework for societal harmony, Ancient Greece 
15 
 
also developed its own distinct concept. The Greeks valued 'sophrosyne,' a concept that describes a 
sound mind and an excellent character, a combination of self-control and temperance ( Rademaker, 
2017). Philosophers like Socrates and Plato emphasised sophrosyne as a foundational element of 
the individual and, by extension, a harmonious society  as noted by Annas (2013). 
As time progressed, concepts related to 'proper' behaviour continued to evolve and took on new 
meanings in different cultural contexts. During the Renaissance in Europe, these evolving norms 
became closely linked with civility, a term that encapsulated the refined behaviour and manners 
expected in both courtly and urban life. The term ‘civility’ was used to characterise the behaviours 
and manners typical of a refined society during the Renaissance; it represented a mode of 
demonstrating one’s social refinement and commitment to the emerging codes of conduct that 
defined that era. Furthermore, civility served as a crucial marker of social distinction, delineating 
between different social classes and groups. This distinction was evident not only in public behaviour, 
but also in private life highlighting the comprehensive influence of civility (Elias, Jephcott, Dunning, 
Goudsblom, and Mennell, 2000). 
As the sixteenth century unfolded, these established notions of civility became more pronounced. 
This era was marked by a more rigid social hierarchy, partly due to the emergence of a new 
aristocracy and an increasingly diverse upper class. This diversification intensified the need for 
unified behavioural norms as a means of affirming one's social status and navigating the complexities 
of this evolving landscape. During this period, the publication of etiquette books and manuals saw a 
rise, such as The Book of the Courtier ( Castiglione, 1528), and The Rules of Polite Behaviour (Casa, 
1558). According to Elias et al “the code of behaviour became stricter, and the degree of 
consideration expected of others became greater. The sense of what to do and what not to do in 
order not to offend or shock others became more binding” (2000, p.69). Consequently, proper 
behaviour’ transitioned from being merely about personal virtue to becoming essential for maintaining 
one's social standing. These books quickly became indispensable references for anyone aspiring to 
secure or elevate their status within society, marking the onset of the formal study of etiquette that 
has continued to evolve through the centuries. 
However, these books and manuals neglected the everyday manners and norms of the broader 
population, it only emphasised norms that underpinned aristocratic life, thereby sidelining the 
everyday practices and manners of the broader population. The focus was so narrow that it offered 
little insight into the social conduct of the lower classes, who undoubtedly maintained their own sets 
of norms and behaviours. This oversight not only underscores a historical bias towards the affluent 
16 
 
but also highlights a significant gap in our understanding of past social norms across all levels of 
society, as these accounts fail to capture the full spectrum of societal interactions (Elias et al, 2000). 
Although concerns with appropriate conduct have long existed and have been discussed in social 
and philosophical thought, it was only in the twentieth century that such concerns began to be 
examined systematically through the lens of language and interaction (Culpeper et al., 2017). The 
following section explores this shift towards theorising (im)politeness.  
2.3 Theorising politeness  
The twentieth century marked the emergence of politeness as a focus of systematic inquiry. Scholars 
began to examine how it is constructed, negotiated, and interpreted in context, paving the way for a 
more dynamic understanding of social interaction. This development aligned with a broader disciplinary 
shift toward studying language in its social context, and by the mid-twentieth century, as sociolinguistics 
emerged as a distinct field, politeness came to be examined not only as a matter of linguistic form (e.g., 
honorifics, indirectness) but as a way of negotiating relationships, managing face, and navigating 
power dynamics. A key figure in this shift was Goffman (1967), whose framework of facework laid the 
conceptual foundation for later theories of politeness by highlighting the interpersonal dynamics 
involved in the management of face during social interaction. Central to this framework is the concept 
of face, which he defines as “the positive social value a person effectively claims for himself by the 
line others assume he has taken during a particular contact (p.213). In other words, it is the image 
individuals present to others and wish to have accepted and upheld in interaction. When an individual 
projects a particular self-image in social situations, they also feel a strong need to maintain and live 
up to that image (Treviño, 2003). Goffman’s work emphasised that much of everyday interaction 
involves efforts to preserve both one’s own face and that of others. 
Building on Goffman’s sociological conception of face (1967), Brown and Levinson (1978, 1987) 
developed a systematic theory of politeness by extending the concept of face into a model of linguistic 
interaction. They redefined the concept of face as “the want to be unimpeded and the want to be 
approved of in certain respects” (p.63), distinguishing between negative face, the desire for autonomy 
and freedom from imposition, and positive face, the desire to be liked, appreciated, and approved of 
by others. Brown and Levinson’s (1978, 1987) argued that these two face needs are universal 
aspects of human interaction that shape how people manage communication across different social 
and cultural contexts. Their model also takes a speaker- oriented perspective, assuming that 
speakers strategically manage face-threatening acts by selecting from four politeness strategies 
based on their assessment of power, distance, and imposition. 
17 
 
While Brown and Levinson’s (1978, 1987) politeness theory has been highly influential, it has also 
faced growing criticism in the decades that followed, particularly for its limited cross-cultural 
applicability. Matsumoto (1988) and Gu (1990), for example, argued that their model centres around 
the individuals’ face needs and thus is more suited to individualistic cultures, such as those found in 
many Western societies, where personal goals, self-expression, and independence are prioritised 
(Hofstede, 2001; Triandis, 1995; Ting-Toomey, 2005, 2017). In such contexts, negative face, the 
desire for freedom from imposition, is often central to communication (Brown and Levinson, 1978, 
1987). In contrast, in collectivist cultures, such as those in East Asian and Middle Eastern countries, 
group needs take precedence over individual needs and maintaining interpersonal relationships and 
fulfilling social obligations are often more important than asserting personal autonomy or individual 
face (Ting-Toomey, 1994 & Al Zidjaly, 2012). Additionally, critics have pointed out that their framework 
overlooks relational or interpersonal aspects of the face often referred to as face concerns which are 
especially salient in some cultures (Mao, 1994; Haugh, 2007). 
Moreover, a number of scholars have identified further conceptual limitations in Brown and 
Levinson’s model. They argue that their approach to face is overly individualistic and overlooks its 
relational nature (Bargiela-Chiappini, 2003, 2006; Haugh, 2005a; Ide, 1989; Lim, 2004; Mao, 1994; 
Matsumoto, 1988). Building on this relational view, Locher and Watts (2005) and Haugh (2007) argue 
that politeness is not something performed solely by the speaker but jointly constructed and 
interpreted in context. As a result, Brown and Levinson’s (1978, 1987) model may not fully capture 
the diverse ways in which politeness is conceptualised and negotiated in interaction. These critiques 
subsequently led scholars to reconceptualise politeness as a more relational and interactionally 
grounded phenomenon, a shift explored further in the following section: 
2.3.1 The Discursive approach to (Im)politeness 
In response to the limitations of Brown and Levinson’s model (1978, 1987), a speaker- oriented and 
strategy-based model, the discursive approach to politeness emerged as a significant shift in how 
politeness is conceptualised and studied. It moves away from predefined politeness strategies and 
universality. Instead, it reconceptualises politeness as a relational and context-sensitive 
phenomenon that emerges from participants’ situated evaluations of communicative behaviour, 
emphasising a bottom-up, first-order perspective that prioritises how politeness is understood, 
negotiated, and talked about by lay interactants, rather than imposed through universal or analyst- 
driven frameworks (Eelen, 2001; Mill,2003 ; Watts 2003; Locher & Watts, 2005).  
Eelen’s (2001) critique of Politeness Theories marked a turning point in politeness research (Mills, 
18 
 
2011). Although Eelen (2001) did not propose a new theory of politeness but instead offered a self-
reflexive critique of previous models that encouraged researchers to go beyond Brown and 
Levinson’s theory.  By highlighting issues and unanswered questions that persisted as long as 
traditional models were repeated without change, his work opened the way for more critical and 
reflective approaches to politeness.   
Eelen (2001) argued that traditional politeness theories were overly normative and analyst-driven, 
often imposing external judgements on what counts as polite or impolite. He pointed to the gap 
between theoretical models of politeness and everyday understandings of politeness by language 
users, laying the foundation for a more participant-focused approach centered on how politeness is 
experienced and evaluated in actual interaction.  In this context, he called for a clear distinction 
between politeness1, participants’ own understanding of what constitutes polite behaviour, and 
politeness2, theorists’ analytical and academic conceptualisation of politeness. He argued that in 
practice, analysts frequently risked mixing the two perspectives within their analyses.  
Following Eelen’s (2001) argument, Watts (2003) argues that a theory of politeness must be able to 
“locate possible realisations of polite or impolite behaviour and offer a way of assessing how 
members themselves may have evaluated that behaviour” (pp.19-20). This position challenges the 
premise, central to Brown and Levinson’s framework, that politeness can be identified through 
predetermined linguistic strategies. For Watts, the key task for researchers is not to ascribe 
politeness to linguistic forms but to understand how participants interpret and evaluate behaviour as 
(im)polite within situated interaction. To support his view, Watts draws on Sperber and Wilson’s 
(1995) Relevance theory to develop a form of analysis which sees politeness as something worked 
out by the participants in context. He emphasises that evaluation of politeness is not encoded in 
linguistic forms but is inferred from participants as interactions unfold. This focus on participants’ 
interpretive work led Watts and Locher (2005) to distinguish between first-order (im)politeness and 
second-order (im) politeness. First-order politeness refers to the everyday, common-sense 
understanding of what counts as polite within a community; second-order politeness is the theoretical 
construct used by researchers. They argue that politeness research should prioritise first-order 
evaluations, making participants’ metapragmatic assessments the basis of analysis.  The distinction, 
however, has been widely debated. Haugh (2007), for instance, questions whether analysts can fully 
avoid imposing their own judgements.  
Alongside the distinction, Locher and Watts (2005, 2008) propose the broader concept of relational 
work, which refers to the ongoing negotiation of social relations during communication through 
19 
 
language. It encompasses a continuum of behaviours “from direct, impolite, rude, aggressive 
interaction to polite interaction, encompassing both appropriate and inappropriate forms of social 
behaviour” (Locher and Watts, 2005 p.95). They argue that evaluation of such behaviour depends 
on how it aligns with or deviates from the norms and expectations that individuals have developed 
and internalised through categorising their own past experiences in similar past situations, as well 
as through inferences drawn from the experiences of others (LPRG, 2011).  
While Locher and Watts (2005, 2008) offer a broad discursive perspective on relational work, 
Spencer-Oatey (2000, 2008) contributes to this relational turn in politeness theory by proposing a 
more systematic and multi-dimensional account of how interpersonal relationships are managed in 
communication. Although her Rapport Management framework is not situated strictly within the 
discursive tradition, it shares with it a central concern for the contextual and evaluative nature of 
communicative behaviour. A key strength of Spencer-Oatey’s model lies in its delineation of three 
interrelated dimensions that collectively shape interpersonal rapport: the management of face, 
sociality rights and obligations, and interactional goals (Spencer-Oatey, 2008). Rather than treating 
these as isolated elements, the framework highlights their interdependence in shaping how relational 
meaning is constructed and interpreted in context. Moreover, her conceptualisation of face, unlike 
that of Brown and Levinson (1987), is not limited to the individual self. Spencer-Oatey (2007, 2008) 
broadens the concept to account for the self as an individual, as relationally positioned in interaction 
with others, and as a member of groups. This tripartite view allows for a more nuanced understanding 
of identity concerns in interaction, recognising that face may be tied not only to personal attributes 
such as competence or autonomy, but also to one’s role in a relationship and one’s status within a 
collective. As such, face becomes a socially embedded construct, shaped by cultural norms and 
interpersonal dynamics, rather than a static, universally defined notion. This expanded definition 
allows for a better understanding of how face is negotiated across diverse interactional settings. In 
this study, the rapport management framework is drawn upon to aid in understanding the evaluative 
grounds individuals invoke when judging an act as uncivil or offensive, particularly through examining 
how face concerns and sociality rights and obligations inform their judgement. Further details on the 
application of this framework are provided in Section 3.7 of the methodology chapter. 
Another important contribution to the discursive approach comes from Mills (2003), who also frames 
politeness as something evaluated by participants rather than encoded in linguistic forms. She 
proposes a community of practice (CoP) framework for examining people’s social behaviour. She 
argues that “communities of practice rather than individuals negotiate and arbitrate over whether 
speech acts are considered polite or impolite”, noting that while gendered stereotypes may influence 
20 
 
these judgements, individuals can still “use such stereotypes strategically to their own advantage” 
(Mills, 2002, p.1). She further maintains that politeness should be analysed at discourse level rather 
than at the level of isolated utterances. It is important to note that Mills view of CoP is based on 
Wenger’s (1998)  social perspective who maintains that “a community of practice consists of a loosely 
defined group of people who are mutually engaged in a particular task and who have a shared 
repertoire of negotiable resources accumulated over time ( Wenger, 1998, cited in Mills, p.30).  This 
grounding is significant because it positions politeness as socially learned and collectively negotiated 
practice. In other words, what counts as polite or impolite emerges from shared histories, 
expectations, and norms within a community.  
Having discussed  some of the main scholars that contributed to the development of the discursive 
approach, namely Eelen (2001), Watts (2003), Locher and Watts ( 2005, 2008), Spencer-Oatey 
2008, and Mills ( 2003), and outlined their respective views, it is now time to consider some of  key 
limitations that have been raised in relation to this approach. One of the limitations of the discursive 
approach is its lack of theoretical grounding. To explain, it does not propose a coherent framework 
for what constitutes impoliteness beyond the fact that it is recognised as such by interactants at the 
moment of interaction. In this regard, Culpeper (2011) maintains that without engagement with 
higher-order abstractions, discursive studies can result in fragmented accounts that lack explanatory 
scope beyond the immediate context of talk-in-interaction, making the approach analytically unstable 
and difficult to generalise. Moreover, a related concern raised by Haugh (2007) is that by highlighting 
that only participants’ evaluations count and excluding the role of analyst, the discursive approach 
risks blurring the boundary between participant and analyst perspective. Additionally, this leaves the 
analyst with very limited scope to offer interpretation or explanation, and makes it unclear who is 
actually establishing that an evaluation of impoliteness has occurred. A further limitation of the 
discursive approach concerns how it deals with social norms. To explain, norms are central to 
understanding how people judge behaviour as polite or impolite in interaction, yet the discursive 
approach does not provide a systematic method for determining where these norms come from, how 
they operate, how they are invoked by participants, or how they can be analytically captured or 
compared across contexts (Haugh, 2007).  
To sum up, the discursive approach’s strength lies in its sensitivity to participants’ evaluative 
judgements, capturing how they interpret, negotiate and respond to perceived impoliteness within 
specific interactional contexts. Nonetheless, this very strength becomes a weakness when the 
approach fails to offer a clear conceptual structure. While it shows how people negotiate impoliteness 
in context, it does not adequately theorise what impoliteness is, or how it can be systematically 
21 
 
identified. As a result, several scholars have called for more integrated models that combine 
discursive sensitivity with theoretical rigor, allowing researchers to remain context-aware without 
sacrificing analytical clarity (Culpeper, 2011; Haugh, 2013).  
 
2.3.2 Third- wave (Im)politeness research 
In response to the previous limitations, a more recent line of research is commonly referred to as 
third-wave politeness or a post-discursive approach has developed, it refers to those who have 
moved beyond the discursive view. This approach emerged as a more integrative framework that 
bridges the gap between participants’ perspectives and the theoretical grounding. Scholars such as 
Culpeper (2007, 2008, 2013), Haugh (2007, 2008, 2013, 2023, 2024), and Kádár and Haugh (2013) 
have been instrumental in shaping this movement and their ideas provide key conceptual resources 
for the analytical approach adopted in this study. Given the scope of the present study, it is not 
possible to review all post-discursive developments in detail. The discussion therefore concentrates 
on strands of work most relevant to the aims of this research.  
 Within this paradigm, Haugh’s (2013) work offers a significant theoretical advancement by 
conceptualising (im)politeness as “ social practices that are occasioned through particular social 
actions and pragmatic meanings in situated, talk-in-interaction” (p.24). Haugh emphasises that 
evaluations of (im)politeness are not simply interpretive responses but should be understood as 
social practices embedded within a broader moral order. To explain, he argues that people do not 
judge something as polite, impolite or even offensive, or uncivil based on what was said, the linguistic 
form of the act, or even what was done, the behaviour itself. Instead, the judgement stems from the 
social action, that is, what participants perceive the speaker to be doing through their words and 
embodied conduct, and the meaning that is interpreted as arising from the interaction. Haugh 
maintains that such evaluations are rooted in a broader moral order, which Davis defines as “any 
system of obligations that defines and organises the proper-good, right, virtuous- relations among 
individuals and groups in a community” (2008, p.204). It is one that is often “ taken for granted” ( 
Kádár & Haugh, p.13) and built upon the ‘‘familiar scenes of everyday affairs’’ (Garfinkel, 1967, p. 
35). The moral order functions as a shared framework through which participants make sense of 
others’ behaviour and evaluate it as appropriate, inappropriate, polite, impolite or offensive. 
Nonetheless, as Haugh (2013, p.57) points out, the moral order is “multi-faceted and multi-layered”, 
meaning it does not operate as a single fixed set of rules. Thus, as Haugh (2013, p.59) maintains, 
“members who identify with different groups, or even members who identify with the same group, do 
22 
 
not necessarily always constitute the moral order in the same way”. This variability means that the 
evaluations of taking offence or incivility are not fixed or universally agreed upon but are shaped by 
participants’ interpretations of what they perceive to be at stake in a given moment. Thus, what one 
individual may view as offensive or inappropriate may be considered acceptable or unproblematic by 
another, depending on how each individual orients to the moral order in that specific context. In this 
sense, the moral order does not function as a static background for interpretation, but is actively 
drawn upon, negotiated and at times contested through interaction. 
In contrast to earlier perspectives on politeness, whether traditional or discursive approaches, which 
tend to prioritise either the speaker or the hearer, the perspective of (im)politeness as a social 
practice does not limit itself to the evaluation of one party. Instead, it encompasses the judgements 
made by all participants involved in a given interaction, as well as any potential meta-participant as 
shown in Kádár and Haugh (2013) and Haugh (2024). Haugh (2013) argues that understanding 
impoliteness requires more careful theorisation of the notion of the participant. He emphasises that 
analysts must avoid presenting their own interpretations as if they were the participants and must 
remain attentive to the multiple footings from which participants themselves evaluate behaviour. For 
Haugh, these footings are instantiated through the moment-by-moment participation order and 
tracing them enables the analyst to avoid confounding their own footing as a meta-recipient with the 
evaluative positions adopted by participants.  
Central to Kádár and Haugh’s (2013) and Haugh’s (2024) perspective is the view that understanding 
(Im)politeness requires drawing on multiple loci of understanding rather than relying on a single lens. 
To account for this plurality, Haugh (2024) proposes a prismatic model of (im)politeness, which 
extends beyond the traditional first-order and second-order distinction. The model identifies four loci 
through which understanding can be rotated or foregrounded depending on the analytic stance 
adopted. 1These loci include the emic/etic perspective, participant/ multiparticipant perspective, 
analyst/ lay observer, and folk theoretical/ scientific.  Each of these loci offers a distinctive point of 
insight on how evaluation of impoliteness arises (Haugh, 2024). While not all loci are relevant in every 
instance, they can overlap depending on the nature of interaction and the research question being 
asked. Consequently, the analyst task is not to select one correct perspective but to recognise how 
multiple understandings may co-exist, intersect or become foregrounded in particular moments. This 
theoretical perspective is particularly valuable for the present study, which explores the grounds that 
 
1 An explanation of their role and relevance to the present study is provided in the methodology chapter, Section 3.8.  
23 
 
participants allude to when making judgements about what they consider offensive and uncivil. 
2.4 (Im)politeness in relation to taking offence and incivility. 
(Im)politeness plays a central role in understanding the phenomena of taking offence and incivility. 
In this study, impoliteness is conceptualised as “an umbrella term” ( Spencer-Oatey, 2005, p.97) that 
offers a theoretical foundation for exploring how individuals perceive and evaluate behaviour that is 
seen to violate social norms and expectations (Culpeper, 2011; Mills, 2003; Sifianou, 2019). Both 
taking offence and incivility are fundamentally subjective phenomena that share an evaluative core: 
each arises when behaviour is perceived to be out of alignment with what is expected or considered 
acceptable in a particular context ( Locher & Watts, 2005; Culpeper et al., 2017). Although they may 
involve different kinds of perceived norm violations, with offense often, but not necessarily, arising in 
response to interpersonal transgressions (Tayebi, 2016), and incivility commonly linked to 
inappropriate conduct in public or shared spaces, both are shaped by individuals’ judgements of what 
they consider acceptable (Locher & Watts, 2005; Culpeper et al., 2017). These judgements are 
grounded in what Haugh (2013) describes as the moral order as explained earlier. This moral order 
plays a key role in determining whether behaviour is interpreted as offensive, or uncivil, and 
underpins the evaluative judgement involved in both taking offence and incivility. 
This view aligns with Culpeper’s (2011, p. 24) definition of impoliteness as “a negative attitude towards 
specific behaviour occurring in a specific context”. This definition captures the evaluative and context- 
sensitive nature of impoliteness, emphasising that it is not an inherent property of a behaviour but a 
judgement made by individuals based on their expectations. As Culpeper et al. (2017) explain, 
behaviours are considered “impolite when they conflict with how one expects them to be, how one 
wants them to be, and/ or how one thinks they ought to be” (p.204). This emphasis on expectations, 
context, and judgement provides a useful lens for understanding how participants in this study interpret 
and respond to both offence and incivility. 
By considering (im)politeness as the overarching concept, this study examines how people interpret 
both offensive and uncivil behaviour through the lens of perceived norms and expectation violations. 
In the case of taking offence, it is maintained that offence involves an affective stance, expressed 
through emotional reactions such as anger, or hurt, annoyance, and may also involve a moral stance, 
whereby the individual grounds their moral claim (Haugh, 2015). However, not all responses 
necessarily involve a moral stance; some may remain at the level of emotional discomfort i.e. negative 
emotions without progressing to sanctioning. This includes drawing on various sociocultural norms 
and expectations ( Tayebi, 2016) in order to justify and legitimise one’s evaluative claims (Haugh, 
24 
 
2015). While Haugh’s (2015) model of registering and sanctioning offence specifically outlines these 
stances in the context of taking offence, this distinction is also relevant to responses to incivility. 
Incivility, too, can evoke affective reactions such as irritation, disappointment, or frustration, and may 
similarly lead to moral judgements when behaviour is perceived to breach what is considered 
acceptable in public places. 
2.5 Norms  
In this study, norms are understood as the shared sociocultural standards that give rise to the 
expectations people rely on when evaluating behaviour as offensive or uncivil. In this study, norms 
are treated as active, socially constructed frameworks through which behaviour is judged as uncivil 
or offensive. This understanding aligns with a long standing view in (im)politeness studies that 
judging a person or their behaviour as polite or impolite is not intrinsic to the act itself, but rather a 
normative assessment grounded in socially shared expectations about what is fitting in a given 
context (Locher and Watts, 2005; Culpeper, 2011, Culpeper & Haugh, 2021). 
Evaluating behaviour as offensive or uncivil therefore involves making a normative judgement. Such 
judgements may be grounded in perceived breaches of what people ought to do that may be 
grounded known as moral or injunctive norms, or in deviations from what people typically do in a 
given context known as descriptive norms (Cialdini, Kallgren and Reno, 1991; Lapinski and Rimal, 
2005; Kallgren, Reno and Cialdini, 2000). While breaches of injunctive norms are more likely to 
trigger a valenced negative judgement, breaches of descriptive norms tend to be perceived as 
surprising rather than problematic (Burgoon and Ebesu- Hubbard, 2005; Spencer-Oatey and Kádár, 
2021). 
Injunctive norms operate through evaluative tendencies that arise when individuals reflect on what 
ought to have been done in a particular situation. As Kádár (2020) explains that injunctive norms are 
inherently abstract, and they only become visible when a breach prompts people to signal how things 
ought to be. These evaluations draw on the moral order, the shared sense of what counts as 
appropriate or acceptable in that situation. Thus, the presence of an injunctive norm can be 
pragmatically identified when it is “talked into being” through recurrent patterns of morally loaded 
disapproval that follow a perceived instance of inappropriateness (p.2). These post-incident 
reactions, what Kádár terms meta-reflective evaluation, highlight the injunctive norms at play. 
Nonetheless, people may evaluate the same incident differently, as they each draw on and prioritise 
different elements of the moral order when making their judgements. Accordingly, within this study 
evaluation of offence and incivility are viewed as responses to perceived violations of injunctive 
25 
 
norms that underpin the moral order of everyday life in Oman. The emphasis is therefore placed on 
injunctive norms, what is ought to rather than descriptive norms, which capture what people typically 
do and are therefore of secondary importance in sensitive evaluative contexts. Having said that, 
descriptive norms can still play a role in shaping the moral order; to explain, when certain behavioural 
patterns are repeated frequently enough, descriptive norms may gradually acquire social or moral 
significance, eventually giving rise to expectations about what people ought to do (Culpeper, 2011a; 
Spencer-Oatey and Kádár, 2021; Terkourafi, 2005a). As Terkourafi (2019) points out “ what is 
frequent to our experience is automatically positively evaluated simply because that’s the way we’ve
