always done it and seen it done and it didn’t occur to do it in any other way” (p.12). She further explains 
that early exposure to conventionalised behaviour shapes our sense of what is normal, making any 
deviation from it feel less natural and worthy of attention. This experiential familiarity, in turn, can 
contribute to the emergence of injunctive norms. 
This point is reinforced by Spencer-Oatey and Kádár (2021), Spencer-Oatey (2008b), and Culpeper 
(2011a) who argue that when certain behaviour happens often enough a descriptive norm may 
gradually evolve into an injunctive norm, as repeated patterns of behaviour begin to carry 
expectations of what ought to be done. This gradual shift from what is frequent to what is expected 
illustrates how behavioural regularities (Burgoon and Ebesu, 2005), can become part of the moral 
order. That is, behaviours that are repeatedly observed may no longer be seen as merely typical; 
instead, they come to represent what is understood as proper, right or expected within a given 
context. In this way, the deviation is not simply noticed as uncommon but interpreted as a breach of 
shared norms and may elicit social judgement, that is an evaluative assessment. 
However, not all norm violations trigger evaluation. As Spencer-Oatey and Kádár (2021) argue, 
individuals operate within subjective “normalcy zones” that is context-specific ranges of behaviour 
they perceive as acceptable (p.118). It is only when individuals perceive that these thresholds have 
been crossed and that the conduct falls outside the acceptability zone, that evaluative processes are 
likely to be triggered. Minor deviations, by contrast, often go unnoticed or are dismissed as 
insignificant. This reinforces the idea that (im)politeness evaluations, and by extension offence and 
incivility, are highly context-dependent and subjectively experienced. This is particularly relevant to 
the present study, which explores how individuals in Omani society judge certain behaviours as 
offensive or uncivil. Such judgements are not triggered by every instance of norm violation but tend 
to arise when certain behaviour is perceived to cross a threshold of propriety, one shaped by a locali 
localised understanding of what is acceptable, and morally binding. 
26 
 
The following sections examine each of these phenomena in turn. They begin with a review of recent 
studies on taking offence and followed in Section 2.7 by a critical exploration of incivility through a 
cross-disciplinary lens, drawing on insights from organisational psychology, political science, 
criminology, urban sociology, and a limited number of studies within (im)politeness studies.  
2.6 Studies On Taking offence 
The notion of offence is a relatively recent addition to the field of (im)politeness and has not yet 
received much attention. While the majority of existing studies have focused on the act of causing 
offence, particularly examining the linguistic strategies that speakers use to cause offence and the 
intentions behind their acts (Culpeper, 1996), the notion of taking offence, that how the hearers 
perceive and evaluate certain behaviour as offensive, has been comparatively underexplored. 
Nevertheless, a growing body of research has begun to shift this focus, placing greater emphasis on 
the hearer’s perspective. This shift has paved the way for a deeper investigation into how offence is 
perceived and how it is evaluated in context. 
Culpeper et al. (2011), drew on Spencer-Oatey’s (2005) rapport management framework to examine 
the perceptions of (im)politeness in social interactions reported by university students across 
England, China, Finland, Germany, and Turkey. Their study quantified the loci on which participants 
based their evaluations, identifying breaches of quality face, equity rights and association rights as 
the most frequently cited grounds. However, although they compared the distribution of these loci 
across different countries, they did not engage in a detailed analysis of culturally specific 
interpretations or the variations in the perception of offence within these loci. Moreover, in their data 
collection, participants were explicitly instructed to narrate incidents involving something that was 
said to them, thus limiting the scope of the study to verbal interaction and excluding non-verbal 
behaviour, even though taking offence is not confined to spoken language alone. Nonetheless, this 
study provides an important foundation for understanding the evaluative dimensions of offence by 
showing the interpersonal concerns that people draw on when making judgements.  
 
Haugh’s (2015) seminal work offers an important contribution to the study of taking offence by 
examining how speakers of American and Australian English navigate moments of potential offence 
in initial interactions. Drawing on naturally occurring conversational data, Haugh examines how 
participants respond to possible transgression as a social action embedded within the interaction 
flow. His analysis included cases where individuals withheld expressing that they had taken offence 
as well as cases where offence was explicitly signalled through pauses, hesitation, or subtle shifts in 
tone without being explicitly verbalised. Haugh argues that taking offence is a pragmatic act shaped 
27 
 
by both the affordances and constraints of the interaction. To explain, in initial interaction, for 
instance, there is often a preference for maintaining agreeability, thus people tend to avoid 
confrontational or disruptive behaviour such as openly expressing that someone has taken offence. 
As he noted, “pointing out someone’s transgression or impropriety could itself be constructed as 
impolite” (p.56). Thus, while the opportunity to take offence may be present, individuals may carefully 
assess whether or not to express it based on the dynamics of the interaction. 
 
Another study done by Taybei (2016) explores the expectations underlying people’s evaluation of an 
act as offensive in the Persian language, and the various social and cultural factors that inform these 
expectations. The analysis of the data showed that participants feel offended when their 
“interpersonal, deference, reciprocity or ritual based expectations” are broken (p.4). She also 
reported that not all members of the community share the same expectations, and she supported 
her argument by referring to cognitive and relational bases of expectations. To explain, cognitively, 
individuals form expectations based on the past experiences they have internalised. Relationally, 
expectations are shaped by the specific interpersonal histories between individuals, meaning that 
offense may be taken differently depending on the relationship’s context and history ( Locher and 
Watts, 2008). As expectations and norms vary in different languages and cultures, further research 
is required to investigate this phenomenon. 
 
Moreover, in a recent study by Parvaresh & Tayebi (2020), the authors attempt to explain how and 
why the hearer assigns offensive meaning to an utterance. They aim to elucidate the pragmatic 
processes at play when the hearer either assigns a particularly impolite or offensive meaning to 
an utterance during an interaction or adjusts the interpretation of the entire interaction in ways that 
are not necessarily intended by the speaker. Based on the analysis of the data taken from a reality 
TV show and a movie, the study found that the hearer frequently engages in an active evaluation 
process, a top- down process, where they assign offensive or impolite meaning to utterances beyond 
the immediate linguistic content. The analysis also suggested that the hearer engages in a 
predominantly top-down process of meaning making where they impose their own interpretation on 
the interaction, often overriding the immediate contextual information. While these study insights are 
valuable, the reliance on the data from TV shows and movies means that they may not capture 
everyday natural interactions, as these media often involve scripted or exaggerated behaviour. 
 
In another recent study Zhao and Ran ( 2022) investigated how offence is taken and rationalised by 
third parties drawing on data from the mediation of conflicts between mothers-in-law and daughters 
28 
 
in law. Specifically, they explore how mediators justify impoliteness when daughters in law take 
offence at their mother-in-law’s impolite behaviour. The analysis of the mediation discourse reveals 
that the mediator frequently uses “vicarious accounts” in their efforts to resolve conflicts, allowing 
them to minimise the accountability of mothers-in-law for their offensive actions. The mediators’ 
justification of mothers-in-law’s behaviour is influenced by two culture-specific moral principles in 
Chinese society namely filial piety” and “harmony is paramount” (p.76). The former emphasises the 
subordination, respect and care the younger generation owes to their elders, while the latter refers 
to preserving harmony, as it is seen as a key to success and fulfilment. Therefore, the mediators’ 
rationalisation of offensive behaviour through these moral codes suggests that offensive acts are not 
just viewed through the lens of the individuals who have taken offence, but  are filtered through 
broader social expectations and moral obligations. The mediators in these contexts serve to maintain 
social stability even if it involves downplaying or justifying offensive acts. This sheds light on how 
impoliteness is managed not just through direct interactions between the person who caused the 
offence and the person who has taken offence, but also through the intervention of others who help 
to interpret and rationalise the offence within a broader cultural framework. 
2.7 Studies on Incivility 
As outlined in the introduction chapter (see Chapter 1, section 1.2), incivility has received growing 
attention in fields such as political science, organisational psychology, and sociology. However, within 
(im)politeness research, its treatment remains limited and often under-theorised (Sifianou, 2019). To 
develop a more comprehensive understanding of incivility, impoliteness studies can benefit from 
engaging with cross-disciplinary research, where the concept has been more extensively theorised 
and empirically examined. However, such engagement requires a critical approach, as each 
discipline conceptualises incivility through the lens of its own priorities in their field. In what follows, 
I examine several seminal works from these disciplines and identify the common insights among 
them, particularly those related to how incivility is evaluated, understood and framed that can inform 
the study of incivility in this research. 
The concept of incivility carries a wide range of meanings across academic fields, encompassing 
everything from minor breaches of etiquette to professional misconduct, civil unrest, and even 
concerns about the erosion of moral values (e.g., Carter, 1998; Johnson, 1988; Roberts, 1985). While 
the Oxford English Dictionary defines incivility as “ rude, uncivil, or uncourteous behaviour towards 
others; want of civility or politeness; discourtesy, rudeness, and uncivil as “not civil or courteous, 
impolite; rough or lacking manners”, these definitions suggest that incivility implies rudeness and 
disregard for others in violation of norms for respect in interpersonal relations ( Brown & Levinson, 
29 
 
1987; Morris, 1996). In criminology literature, incivility has been described as “ low-level breaches of 
community standards that signal an erosion of conventionally accepted norms and values” ( 
LaGrange, Ferraro, and Supancic, 1992), often manifested in physical signs such as graffiti, 
vandalism, and deteriorating buildings, as well as in social behaviours like antisocial conduct and 
begging (LaGrange et al., 1992; Perkins, Meeks & Taylor, 1992). Their interest in incivility has largely 
been shaped by its perceived link to crime and the potential escalation of criminal activity. These 
studies typically categorise incivility into “physical incivility” involving visible signs of disorder in the 
environment (e.g., littering, vandalism), and “social incivility”, referring to behaviours deemed 
threatening or undesirable in public spaces, such as loitering, public intoxication, or verbal 
altercations (Sampson & Raudenbush, 1999). However, as Phillips and Smith (2006) point out, this 
approach often targets specific social groups, particularly young people, marginalised communities, 
and minorities, who are more likely to be associated with these acts. Second, it tends to treat incivility 
as a decontextualised, observable condition, overlooking how judgements of incivility are socially 
constructed. Finally, criminological models frequently ignore interpersonal incivilities that emerge in 
routine face-to-face interactions, moments that while not illegal, may still deeply disrupt individuals’ 
experience of public life. 
A different approach can be seen in organisational psychology, one of the most influential strands of 
incivility research, where Andersson and Pearson’s (1999) seminal work defines incivility in relation 
to workplace norms. They define it as “ low-intensity deviant workplace behaviour with an ambiguous 
intent to harm the target, in violation of workplace for mutual respect,… it is characteristically rude 
and discourteous, displaying a lack of regard for others” (p. 457). Their definition highlights several 
characteristics. First, it is proposed to be ambiguous in its intent to harm meaning it is often unclear 
to those involved whether the act was deliberate. This focus on ambiguity of intent contrasts with 
second-wave (im)politeness approaches (e.g., Culpeper, 2011; Locher & Watts, 2005), which move 
beyond the intention of the speaker and instead emphasise how behaviour is interpreted by the 
recipient. Secondly, incivility is theorised to cause harm to the recipient because uncivil behaviour 
conveys a lack of concern for others, leading to negative emotions. Third, uncivil behaviour is 
considered to be deviant in that it violates norms of respect and courtesy in organisations. To account 
for how such exchanges unfold, Andersson and Pearson (1999) adopt a social interactionist 
perspective, emphasising the interpersonal and situational dynamics that shape uncivil interactions. 
In this view, the uncivil act is not viewed as an isolated event but as an ongoing process shaped by 
contextual constraints such as power relations, prior interactions, and workplace-specific norms of 
respect. They observe that the starting point of incivility often lies in the perception that a norm has 
30 
 
been violated, which gives rise to negative feelings specifically a sense of injustice that might escalate 
the interaction into a cycle of uncivil behaviours. Their work provides valuable insights for this study 
by identifying key elements of incivility: perceived norm violations, the emotional responses they 
evoke such as a sense of injustice and its processual nature, whereby incivility unfolds through 
ongoing interaction rather than as a discrete event. However, their framework places a heavy emphasis 
on a singular normative foundation mutual respect norm, which while relevant, risks oversimplifying 
the complexity of normative expectations even within workplace settings. Moreover, it offers limited 
engagement with the interpretive processes through which individuals evaluate behaviour, especially 
in relation to the culturally and socially embedded norms that vary across contexts and shape 
perceptions of incivility. 
Moving from workplace incivility to the public sphere, Papacharissi (2004) explores incivility in 
political discourse, where it is understood as a “negative collective face; that is, disrespect for the 
collective traditions of democracy” (p. 267). Drawing on Brown and Levinson’s (1987) concept of 
face, she extends it beyond the individual to frame incivility as a threat to civil identity and shared 
democratic values. By contrast, scholars such as Chen and Lu ( 2017) and Chen and Ng (2017) 
conceptualise incivility as a threat to an individual’s public identity in contexts of online political 
discourse using face and politeness theories (Brown & Levinson, 1987; Goffman, 1959; Oetzel & 
Ting-Toomey, 2003). In contrast to face-based models, which emphasise identity threats either at an 
individual level or collective level, Dishon and Ben-Porath (2018) offer a justice-oriented account of 
incivility. They conceptualise incivility as conduct that undermines others’ equal status within a shared 
community, whether through exclusion from participation or the reinforcement of social hierarchies. 
In their view, incivility cannot be judged by “a predominant code of conduct”; instead, it is evaluated 
in relation to the social context, specifically, whether a given behaviour undermines fairness, 
inclusion, or equal participation within a shared community (Dishon & Ben-Porath, 2018, p. 439). 
Sociological approaches to incivility offer a distinct lens, emphasising the phenomenon as socially 
constructed and embedded in the routines of everyday public life (Phillips and Smith, 2006 & 2003; 
Smith, Phillips, and King, 2010). In this strand of work, Phillips and Smith (2006) examine how 
incivility surfaces through seemingly mundane encounters in public spaces, such as on public 
transport or in commercial areas. They focus on what participants themselves consider to be uncivil. 
However, their emphasis remains largely on categorising behaviour that people find uncivil. Although 
their study stresses the importance of understanding incivility as it is “defined by ordinary social 
actors” (Phillips & Smith, 2008, p. 88), it does not fully theorise the evaluative frameworks through 
31 
 
which such judgements are made. As a result, while their work provides a grounded account of 
everyday incivility, it does not engage with how norms are constructed, negotiated, or contested in 
the process of those evaluations.   
To address this conceptual gap, Horgan (2020, 2024) provides a more explicitly evaluative account 
of everyday incivility, grounding it in how ordinary people interpret and morally judge breaches of 
expected conduct in public life. Horgan (2024, p.06) frames breaches as "ruptures in interactional 
norms that draw attention to the more broad-scale production of social order" suggesting that such 
moments are not merely local disruptions but also windows into the shared moral frameworks 
underpinning public life. He argues that the identification of incivility occurs through “in situ lay 
classification of conduct”, guided by “culturally available meanings organised around a basic 
sacred/profane binary” (2020, p.128), where civility is treated as sacred and its violation as profane. 
This evaluative process takes place within what he describes as a moral landscape, which is marked 
by the coexistence of multiple, and sometimes contradictory, moral justifications. In making sense of 
uncivil encounters, individuals foreground certain moral meanings while backgrounding others, 
working to render their interpretations intelligible. Although these evaluations are shaped by specific 
interactions, they are not wholly determined by them; he argues that the moral landscape operates 
at a broader level, existing independently of any single episode and offering a shared, though flexible, 
framework for moral judgement in everyday public life. 
While Horgan provides an account of how ordinary people morally evaluate incivility, his notion of the 
“moral landscape” tends to foreground a universalist orientation to morality, such as fairness and 
respect as key interpretive anchors (2024, p.06). While this is valid for the data he examines, his 
analysis underplays the extent to which social and specific factors shape. Although he acknowledges 
that participants on a few occasions temper universal claims by referencing personal circumstances, 
this complexity is not fully explored. That is, while he notes that contextual factors can influence moral 
evaluation, he does not develop a sustained theoretical account of how these personal and 
situational elements systematically shape judgements of incivility. As a result, the analysis may risk 
presenting incivility evaluations as grounded solely in shared moral principles, rather than as socially 
embedded, negotiated, and responsive to the complexities of everyday interaction. What underpins 
participants’ evaluations, then, may go beyond moral principles alone, drawing also on social norms 
and context-specific expectations that shape how incivility is interpreted in real life encounters. 
Moving to incivility within (im)politeness research, there have been some recent efforts to bring the 
concept more clearly into the field, although engagement remains limited. As noted in Chapter 1 
32 
 
(Section 1.2), a small number of scholars have touched on the phenomenon of incivility often in 
connection with broader discussions of public discourse, normative expectations, and evaluations of 
(im)polite behaviour (e.g., Lakoff, 2005; Mills, 2009, 2017b; O’Driscoll, 2013). While incivility is 
referenced in some of these works, it is not positioned as a central analytical category. However, 
these studies contribute to opening up space for thinking about how behaviour is judged in relation 
to shared norms. More explicit treatments of incivility appear in the work of Terkourafi et al. (2018) 
and Sifianou (2019), and Haugh (2022), all of whom examine how incivility is constructed and 
evaluated in online discourse. Terkourafi et al. (2018) treat incivility as a discursively constructed 
judgement that emerges in public responses to controversial tweets. Drawing on theories of 
(im)politeness and hate speech, they show how implicated meanings are reinterpreted by diverse 
audiences, and how certain utterances come to be framed as offensive or uncivil through re-
contextualisation processes. 
Sifianou (2019), drawing on data from reader comments on a Greek news article, highlights how lay 
evaluations of impoliteness often extend beyond verbal interaction to include behaviours such as 
pushing, littering, or failing to queue, which are judged against broader social and moral expectations. 
Her study foregrounds the significance of folk understandings in shaping perceptions of im/politeness 
and argues that these evaluations frequently align more closely with what she describes as “the 
rather broader concept of incivility” (p. 49). However, while the paper draws attention to the 
conceptual overlap between impoliteness and incivility, it does not provide a systematic framework 
for distinguishing the two, noting instead that “their exact relationship and their points of overlap and 
divergence remain rather unclear” (p. 49). The study is insightful in exposing the moral and 
ideological dimensions embedded in public evaluations, yet it remains largely descriptive and 
exploratory. It stops short of theorising how incivility might be analytically integrated into 
(im)politeness research, or what methodological tools might be necessary to capture its distinct 
features. 
A more recent study, Haugh (2022), examines how conduct described as 'public incivilities' is framed 
or construed as offensive in online denunciations. Drawing on a dataset of viral social media posts 
in Australia and New Zealand, the study focuses on interactions between strangers in public settings 
that are treated as morally transgressive when shared and evaluated online. While the term 'public 
incivility' is used to describe such behaviour, the study does not aim to define or theorise incivility 
itself. Instead, it focuses on how such behaviour is discursively framed as denounceable through 
what Haugh calls “moral indignation devices” such as ought-statements, imperatives, assessments, 
33 
 
sarcasm, and negatively valenced descriptions (p. 53). These elements work together to express 
disapproval and position the conduct as morally blameworthy. As Haugh explains, “online public 
denunciations of public incivilities do not simply involve claims that the public conduct in question is 
transgressive, but involve discursively framing that transgression as one warranting public 
condemnation” (p. 55). Haugh’s (2022) analysis is particularly relevant to the present study in its 
attention to how ordinary behaviours become the target of public moral judgement through specific 
linguistic and discursive practices. However, this exclusive focus on moral framing limits the 
analytical scope of the study, as it does not consider how evaluations of incivility might also stem 
from context-specific breaches of social or cultural expectations that are not necessarily viewed 
through a moral lens. 
Extending the focus on how public conduct is evaluated, pragmatic research has also examined 
situations where responses to norm violations become far more intensified and ritualised. In this 
regard, Kádár and Ning’s (2019) study on ritual public civil humiliation (RPCH) examines extramarital 
cases in which individuals who are believed to have violated a significant moral norm are subjected 
to public shaming. Their analysis across many different cases of RPCH shows that people repeatedly 
use similar interpersonal pragmatic features such as making derogatory moral verdicts, addressing 
the victim with derogatory terms, and appealing to public attention in order to secure ratification of 
punishment. These features demonstrate that the impoliteness involved in RPCH is ritual rather than 
ad hoc, organised around rights and obligations serving to publicly affirm the moral judgement and 
reinforce the underlying social norm. Unlike the mundane form of public incivility discussed earlier 
such as those examined by Haugh (2022), where moral appeal may be implicit or explicit depending 
on the context, RPCH tends to operate with explicit moral appeals as an integral part of the punitive 
act. Nonetheless, RPCH represents an extreme and punitive response compared to everyday 
incivility explored in this thesis. It highlights that evaluation of norm violation can take on a ritualised 
and moralising character, foregrounding the social authority that underpins judgements of 
inappropriate conduct.   
In conclusion, the review of incivility across disciplines reveals that while the concept is interpreted 
in diverse ways, each field offers valuable insights that can inform a more comprehensive, norm- 
based evaluative framework. Criminological approaches, despite their tendency to treat incivility as 
an observable condition, contribute useful distinctions between physical and social forms of incivility 
and draw attention to how visible disorder can shape perceptions of social decline. Organisational 
psychology, particularly the work of Andersson and Pearson (1999), offers an interactional model of 
34 
 
incivility as a dynamic process grounded in perceived norm violations and emotional responses, 
providing a foundation for understanding how uncivil behaviour unfolds over time. Political science 
approaches, such as those by Papacharissi (2004) and Dishon and Ben-Porath (2018), extend the 
notion of incivility into the civic realm, where it is understood as a threat to either collective identity or 
equal participation, highlighting the political and justice-oriented implications of uncivil conduct. 
Sociological perspectives, especially those of Phillips and Smith (2006), emphasise how incivility is 
constructed in everyday public encounters, drawing attention to how ordinary people identify and 
respond to norm violations in routine contexts. Horgan’s (2020, 2024) contribution lies in 
foregrounding the interpretive processes involved in these evaluations, showing how judgements of 
incivility are shaped by broader moral frameworks what he terms the ‘moral landscape’—while also 
revealing the layered and sometimes conflicting meanings behind those judgements. 
 
Within (im)politeness research, engagement with incivility remains relatively limited, though recent 
work by Terkourafi et al. (2018), Sifianou (2019), and Haugh (2022) signals an important shift. These 
studies highlight how incivility is discursively framed in online discourse and how folk evaluations 
reflect broader normative and ideological concerns. However, many of these contributions either 
remain exploratory or implicitly moral, without offering sustained theoretical engagement with how 
incivility is judged in relation to context-specific norms. The value of this cross-disciplinary review lies 
in demonstrating the need for an approach that combines the interpersonal, discursive, and socially 
grounded dimensions of incivility. The present study builds on these insights by proposing a 
framework that treats incivility as an evaluative act anchored in locally shared norms and 
expectations, where morality may be evoked but part of broader sociocultural framework. 
2.8 Conclusion 
This chapter has traced the development of politeness from its early historical roots in ancient moral 
and social codes to contemporary theories of (im)politeness, and it has argued that impoliteness offers 
a valuable overarching framework for understanding how individuals evaluate behaviour as offensive 
or uncivil. Both taking offence and incivility were shown to involve subjective judgements grounded in 
perceived violations of norms and expectations, shaped by orientations to a broader moral order. 
This conceptual foundation informs the study’s focus on how such evaluations are experienced and 
articulated in everyday interactions. This chapter also reviewed recent studies on taking offence and 
incivility and identified gaps in the literature in greater depth. 
35 
 
CHAPTER 3: RESEARCH METHODOLOGY 
 
3.1 Introduction 
This chapter provides a comprehensive account of the methodological framework employed to 
investigate the phenomena of taking offence and incivility within Omani society. It begins by restating 
the research questions that underpin this study, establishing a clear focus for the methodological 
approaches that follow. The chapter then delves into the philosophical foundation of the research, 
positioning phenomenology as the central perspective. It highlights how meaning is co-constructed 
through the dynamic interplay between individual subjective experiences and the social-objective 
dimension of reality, shaping the understanding of taking offence and incivility. 
Building on this foundation, the research design and methodological execution are detailed, with a 
particular emphasis on the explanatory sequential mixed-methods design, followed by an exploratory 
qualitative layer. It combines quantitative and qualitative methodologies to achieve a multidimension 
understanding of the phenomena under investigation. Phase One integrates quantitative corpus 
analysis and qualitative thematic analysis of X data, while Phase Two employs qualitative diary 
reports to capture detailed personal narratives. The transition from mixed methods in Phase One to 
the qualitative focus in Phase Two is discussed, demonstrating how these phases complement each 
other to provide a comprehensive exploration of the phenomena under investigation. 
The chapter elaborates on the specific data collection methods utilised in each phase. For Phase 
One, the process of collecting X data is explained, followed by an account of its analysis, which 
involved two layers: quantitative corpus analysis using Sketch Engine and qualitative thematic 
analysis. For Phase Two, the focus shifts to diary reports as the data source, detailing the 
construction and distribution methods with versions tailored to examine “taking offence” and the 
“incivility” respectively. The processes of participant selection and the dual distribution strategies, 
online via WhatsApp and paper-based distribution in public spaces, are also examined along with 
the preparation and cleaning of the diary report. Subsequently, the data analysis methods for each 
phase are discussed in depth, with particular attention given to the application of thematic analysis 
and the theoretical framework that guided this process. 
This chapter also details important elements of the research process such as data translation and 
concludes with a discussion of the ethical measures taken to ensure participant confidentiality. 
36 
 
3.2 Research Questions 
The methodological approach outlined in this chapter is guided by two central research questions 
which reflect the broader aim of the study: is to explore the grounds that Omani individuals invoke to 
legitimise their judgements of offence and incivility. These questions are as follows: 
 
1. What makes Omani people take offence? 
2. What are the norms that govern the perception of incivility in the Omani society? 
 
Building on the research questions, the next section introduces the philosophical foundation that 
shapes the study’s approach and analytical framework. 
3.4 Overview of Research Design 
This study adopts a two-phase sequential research design, beginning with a preliminary mixed 
methods phase followed by a qualitative phase. Phase One draws on data from X collected using 
hashtags associated with offence and incivility, and applies quantitative corpus analysis first, followed 
by a qualitative thematic analysis of the same dataset.  Insights gained from the quantitative analysis 
inform the subsequent qualitative strand, allowing the study to first identify linguistic patterns in how 
offence and incivility are expressed online, and then explore the meanings underlying these patterns.  
Phase Two builds on the insights developed in Phase One by employing diary reports to capture 
individuals lived experiences of offence and incivility. The diary report questions were informed by 
the communicative patterns identified in Phase One, allowing this phase to offer depth and detail that 
could not be obtained from X data alone.    
This section provides a brief outline of the overall design; a detailed account of research procedures 
is presented in Section 3.5. The next section discusses the philosophical foundation that guides this 
study.   
3.5 Philosophical Foundation of Research 
This research adopts a phenomenological epistemological stance, which positions offence and 
incivility as phenomena that are understood through the ways people experience them and make 
sense of them in daily life. Underlying this stance is a social constructionist ontological view in which 
offence and incivility are not treated as objective properties inherent in behaviour, but as socially and 
culturally mediated interpretations that emerge through individuals’ lived experiences with others. In 
this sense, reality is viewed as intersubjective and shaped by the meaning people attribute to events 
within their social world.  
37 
 
This stance directs attention to how individuals interpret behaviour within the context of their lived 
realities. Husserl’s (1970) account of the lifeworld informs this approach by emphasising that people 
come to understand the world through the lived experiences that shape how meaning is formed. In 
addition, Schutz’s (1945) view of the lifeworld as a social realm, a world that is “self-evident, “pre-
scientific”, “taken-for-granted”, “public” and the “common to all of us” (p. 534), highlights that these 
interpretations are shaped by shared assumptions. In other words, as also viewed in this research, 
people do not make sense of events in a vacuum; rather, participants’ evaluations of offensive or 
uncivil behaviour are shaped by what they assume to be shared understandings within their social 
world.  
These taken- for-granted understanding that guide these evaluations are drawn from individuals’ 
“stock of knowledge” (Schutz, 1945 p.137). This stock is an accumulated reservoir of experiences 
that individuals have acquired throughout their lives; it includes cultural information, personal past 
experiences and general expectations about the world. Thus, the knowledge generated in this study 
emerges from the ways participants draw on this reservoir when interpreting and evaluating 
behaviours as offensive or uncivil, relying on what they already know, expect, and assume to be 
shared within their sociocultural environment. Drawing on this stock, individuals develop typifications 
by identifying recurring patterns in their interactions and organising them into social categories. 
 This notion of typifications offers a useful lens for understanding this process in this research, as 
typifications act as interpretive tools that allow individuals to quickly classify situations, judge their 
meaning, and anticipate how others are likely to behave. In doing so, they operate as a common 
frame of reference and provide a foundation for mutual understanding within social groups. However, 
when they are challenged, such as by events or behaviours that fail to align with the established 
patterns of meaning, individuals may experience disruption, prompting them to revisit their taken-for-
granted knowledge and reassess their existing assumptions.  In this study, these moments of 
disruption, when a behaviour no longer fits an expected typification, are precisely where participants 
articulate why an action is experienced as offensive or uncivil, making visible the interpretive 
resources through which knowledge is formed. 
This philosophical position aligns directly with the methodological design of the research. It draws on 
data from X in Phase One and diary reports in Phase Two to access how participants themselves 
interpret and evaluate these moments of disruption. The accounts gathered in this research enable 
the study to trace the underlying assumptions that guide participants’ judgements of offence and 
incivility. In doing so, the methodological approach reflects the study’s phenomenological foundation, 
38 
 
ensuring that the knowledge produced remains grounded in participants’ own meaning-making and 
socioculturally situated evaluations. 
As a researcher, my positionality forms an important part of this philosophical stance. I hold insider 
knowledge in this research, as an Oman researcher familiar with the social environment from which 
participants’ accounts emerge. At the same time, I also occupy an analyst perspective, applying 
theoretical frameworks and systematic analytical procedures to interpret the data. This dual position 
requires ongoing reflexivity to ensure that the analysis remains grounded in participants’ accounts.  
3.6 Research Design and Methodological Execution 
Drawing on the phenomenological foundations outlined in Section 3.3, particularly Schutz’s concept 
of typifications and Vargas’s dynamic view of intersubjectivity, the research design is structured to 
explore how individuals in Omani society draw on the shared sociocultural norms to interpret and 
evaluate behaviour they consider offensive or uncivil. Having said that, this study is divided into two 
primary sections: one exploring the phenomenon of taking offence and the other exploring incivility. 
Both sections follow an identical methodological process to address their research questions and 
involve two phases. 
This study employed an adapted explanatory sequential mixed-methods design followed by a 
qualitative layer (Creswell & Plano Clark, 2018), structured to explore the norms and expectations 
underlying offence and incivility in Omani society. Creswell and Plano Clark (2007) describe an 
explanatory sequential mixed-methods design as a method where researchers first collect and 
analyse quantitative data, followed by qualitative data collection and analysis to provide deeper 
insights and explanations for the quantitative findings. 
While the general structure of the explanatory sequential mixed methods design, beginning with a 
quantitative phase followed by a qualitative phase remains consistent with Creswell and Plano 
Clark’s (2007) design, this study introduced key adaptations (see Figure 3.1). 
39 
 
 
 
Figure 3.1: An adapted explanatory sequential mixed-methods 
design with an additional qualitative layer 
 
Rather than following the conventional approach of collecting a new qualitative dataset in the second 
strand of Phase One, the analysis instead incorporated the same dataset used in the first strand. 
The second strand (qualitative data analysis) was directly informed by the patterns and insights 
identified in the quantitative findings. This approach enabled a deeper and more integrated 
exploration of the same dataset, offering richer insights into the identified patterns. By combining 
these methods in this way, the study retained the sequential nature of the design while adapting it to 
provide a richer understanding of offence and incivility (see Section Chapter 4 section 4.3). 
In Phase Two, the study adopted an additional qualitative phase to further explore the phenomena 
of taking offence and incivility. As I noted in chapter One ( see Section 1.2 & 1.3 ), previous research 
in both taking offence and incivility has often relied on a single method of data collection, which limits 
the depth and does not capture the whole perspective. It is important to note that Phase One informed 
the design of Phase Two, as indicated by the directional flow in Figure 3.1. Specifically, insights from 
the initial phase helped shape the development of the second, particularly by identifying how offence 
and incivility were discussed, including the specific wording, sequencing and framing used by 
individuals when commenting online. This will be discussed in detail in Section 3.6.2. Phase Two 
complemented the findings from Phase One by providing a more detailed and a personal account of 
how individuals interpret and evaluate behaviours in their social world. It must be highlighted that 
relying only on Phase One would have provided a limited perspective as it primarily captured online 
public expression of offence and incivility. Phase Two was therefore essential for gaining 
multidimensional insights and deeper understanding of the phenomena. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
40 
 
The following sections briefly discuss each strand within the two phases of study, along with the 
rationale underpinning each strand, illustrating how these strands are systematically applied to 
uncover the layers of meaning behind taking offence and incivility within Omani society. 
3.6.1 Phase 1: Quantitative and Qualitative strands 
The quantitative strand was used in the first part of the preliminary study to generate a broad 
understanding of the phenomenon. Therefore, a large number of tweets were analysed to identify 
statistical patterns that recurred across many users, offering insights into the general trends of what 
Omani people find offensive and uncivil in Oman. For instance, analysing the frequency of specific 
words such as “country” in the context of taking offence, or “rubbish” in discussions of incivility, 
revealed how certain topics and behaviours consistently trigger offence or are deemed inappropriate 
by Omani society. This method offered a bird’s eye view of how offence and incivility manifest across 
a wide cross-section of the Omani population on X, highlighting common triggers and behaviours 
perceived as offensive or uncivil. 
While this strand offered valuable insight into general trends, it lacked the depth required to fully 
understand the underlying norms and expectations that shape perceptions of offence and incivility. 
As Creswell and Tashakkori (2007) noted, quantitative methods often fall short of capturing the depth 
that is sometimes needed for a fuller understanding. To address the limitations of quantitative 
analysis, a qualitative strand was incorporated into the preliminary study. This strand involved 
conducting a thematic analysis on the same dataset, which had been analysed quantitatively, 
building upon the patterns identified during the quantitative analysis. The intent of this qualitative 
strand was to provide a deeper understanding and contextual insights into the patterns and trends 
revealed by the quantitative analysis. It uncovered the expectations that were infringed that led to 
taking offence, and the norms that were violated that made the X users consider an act as uncivil. 
By integrating these two strands, quantitative strand to identify trends and qualitative strand to 
explore the underlying meaning, a comprehensive initial understanding of the dynamics of taking 
offence and incivility was uncovered within the context of Omani X users. 
3.6.2 Phase 2: Qualitative Strand 
Building on the findings from Phase One, which provided an initial understanding of the dynamics of 
taking offence and incivility within the context of Omani X users, Phase Two was designed to deepen 
this understanding by capturing personal accounts of these phenomena. It was built on the 
developmental role of Phase One. In other words, insights from both quantitative and qualitative 
41 
 
analysis of X data in Phase one informed the design and formulation of the diary reports in Phase 
Two. As Greene et al. (1989, p. 259) note, "results from one method help develop or inform the other 
method”. This sequential integration enabled Phase Two to build on the broader understanding 
gained from Phase One, transitioning from a broad overview to an in-depth investigation of 
individuals’ experiences. 
While the X data provided valuable initial insights into the perceptions of taking offence and incivility, 
it only captured a specific demographic group, primarily X users, who are males. Although the exact 
figure on X users in Oman from the period when data was retrieved (2011-2020) does not exist, recent 
data indicates that as of early 2024, 62% of X users in Oman were male, while 37.5% were females, 
reflecting a broader trend of male-dominated X usage in the country (DataReportal, 2024). This 
gender imbalance means that the data predominantly reflects male perspectives, limiting the 
representation of female voices on offence and incivility. Additionally, the use of X data primarily 
reflects the perspectives of platform users, which may not fully capture the experiences of those who 
do not engage with X. While this provides valuable insights into public discussions on offence and 
incivility, it is important to acknowledge that perspectives from individuals who do not use X might be 
unrepresented. 
Additionally, the character limit on X posts during the period from which the data was retrieved 
imposed constraints on the depth of users’ expressions. To explain, until 2017, posts were restricted 
to 140 characters per post, and although this limit was later expanded to 280 characters, the brevity 
still limited users from providing detailed accounts and sufficient context ( Rosen & Ihara, 2017). 
These constraints, coupled with demographic limitations of X users, underscored the necessity of 
incorporating additional data collection methods to achieve a more comprehensive understanding of 
these phenomena. 
Thus, Phase Two served a dual purpose: addressing the inherent limitations of Phase One while 
capturing the personal narratives and contextual insights necessary for a holistic understanding of 
offence and incivility in Omani society. Together, the two phases created a layered and 
comprehensive exploration, integrating the quantitative and qualitative insights from Phase One, 
where broad patterns in language use and themes were identified, with the detailed, context-rich 
accounts captured in Phase Two. 
The next sections outline the methods employed in Phase One, detailing the data collection tools, the 
process of gathering the data, and the subsequent data cleaning procedures. This is followed by a 
discussion of analysis process, starting with quantitative corpus analysis then transitioning to 
42 
 
qualitative thematic analysis. 
3.7 Phase One: Data Collection and Analysis 
3.7.1 Date Collection: X as Data Source 
In Phase One of the study, X was used to locate written texts that reflect acts of taking offence and 
incivility. As a widely used online platform, X allows users to share their thoughts and opinions in short 
posts limited to 280 characters. The nature of the text content that the users post may include 
personal updates, thoughts and opinions, and it may include images, links and hashtags to participate 
in broader conversations or categorise content effectively (Weller et al., 2014). 
X was selected as a data source for several compelling reasons. One of these  is that unlike 
traditional surveys where responses are guided, X offers a naturally occurring dataset that captures 
users’ spontaneous behaviour and unfiltered thoughts. This aligns with McCormick, Lee, Cesare, 
Shojaie, and Spiro’s (2017, p.96) observation that “this source of organically created and 
automatically archived human data allow researchers to see what people are doing, what they are 
saying, and how they feel about a particular issue as these actions and thoughts arise”. This highlights 
the value of X data as authentic data source for capturing individuals’ perspectives. 
What is more, X is considered a “locative platform” as it allows users to attach location data as well 
as timestamps to their tweets. This optional feature significantly enhances the platform’s value for 
research purposes by enabling the identification of geographical locations and timeframes, allowing 
researchers to find data based on where and when events occur (Weller, Bruns, Burgess, Mahrt, and 
Puschmann, 2014, p.155). 
It is important to note, however, that X posts do not represent natural, real-time interaction. To 
explain, posts are written after an event has occurred, meaning that the platform captures people’s 
recalled perceptions rather than the full unfolding of the situation itself. However, X posts are 
considered spontaneous, unprompted expressions produced without researcher influence, allowing 
individuals to articulate their evaluations freely. Thus, they provide valuable insights into how offence 
and incivility are publicly expressed which aligns with the aims of this study.   
Having established the rationale behind using X as a  data collection platform, hashtags and 
keywords were therefore employed to efficiently locate relevant tweets related to taking offence and 
incivility within Oman. As Weller et al. (2014, p.74) note, X users use hashtags to “explicitly mark 
tweets as relevant to a specific theme”, making them easily researchable. A hashtag is created by 
placing the symbol # directly before a keyword, enabling the categorisation of content and facilitating 
43 
 
its discovery. Keywords, on the other hand, are words or phrases that can appear anywhere within 
the text of a tweet and are used to identify content related to a particular topic. They are used in 
research queries to locate relevant material without requiring the use of a specific symbol. 
Having said that, hashtags were employed as a primary method for identifying tweets explicitly 
tagged with specific terms such as مسي # ( translated as # offensive) and or حضاري غير# (translated as 
# uncivil). These hashtags enabled efficient organisation of posts and conversations directly linked to 
the topic of interest ( Efron, 2011). Nevertheless, relying solely on hashtags to locate relevant posts 
posed several limitations. To explain, “hashtags datasets do not contain all relevant tweets, but only 
those whose authors knew of and felt motivated enough to include the hashtag in the tweet”. 
Furthermore, not all relevant posts are “marked with an appropriate hashtag” (p.24). In other words, 
some people might talk about taking offence or incivility without using hashtag. This means that 
relevant content could remain hidden within posts not explicitly tagged with the # symbol, making it 
more challenging to collect or identify through hashtag searches alone. 
Therefore, to address this limitation, keyword searches were employed alongside hashtag searches. 
This combined approach ensured that relevant but untagged posts could also be captured, thereby 
enhancing the comprehensiveness and accuracy of the data collection process. 
3.7.2 The Process of Collecting X Data: 
To locate written texts involving the act of taking offence and incivility, the selection of hashtags and 
keywords was a crucial step in the data collection process. This process was informed by 
consultations with subject matter experts, input from Omani friends with firsthand knowledge of the 
country social and cultural context, and review of existing research on taking offence and incivility. 
The subject matter experts provided valuable academic insights, using their academic expertise to 
identify commonly used words and phrases relevant to discussions of taking offence and incivility. 
Their input ensured that the selected terms were theoretically grounded and aligned with broader 
research on these topics. Complementing this academic perspective, Omani friends offered crucial 
local knowledge, including nuances in language used on the platform. Their contribution included 
identifying slang, idiomatic expressions, and culturally specific terms that might not be easily 
recognised by those unfamiliar with the local context. 
Additionally, reviewing previous studies that utilised hashtags to investigate taking offence and 
incivility such as Tayebi (2016) and others, contributed to informing the selection of hashtags by 
shedding light on common terminology used to discuss these phenomena. While these studies were 
44 
 
conducted in different contexts, they highlighted specific terms and phrases frequently associated 
with taking offence and incivility. This provided clear guidance on the language and expressions to 
consider when identifying appropriate hashtags for this research. 
By the combining the academic expertise, local insight from Omani friends, and a review of previous 
studies, the selection of hashtags was thoughtfully crafted to ensure it was both culturally relevant 
and academically grounded. This collaborative approach resulted in a carefully crafted list of 
hashtags and keywords in Arabic that best represent the terms associated with the discussion of 
taking offence and incivility within Omani X community. 
The following tables present this comprehensive and representative list of terms tailored to the 
context of this research. Table 3.1 focuses on terms associated with taking offence, while Table 
3.2 highlights terms related to incivility. Each table includes the Arabic hashtags and keywords which 
were originally used in the search, their English translations, and phonetic transcriptions for 
accessibility to non-Arabic speakers. Together, these tables form the foundation for identifying the 
relevant posts.

45 
 
After identifying relevant hashtags and keywords, additional sampling criteria were applied to ensure 
that the dataset was geographically and culturally specific to the Omani society. The first sampling 
criterion was geolocation; given that this study focuses on the Omani society, it was vital to ensure 
that the tweets were generated from within Oman. To achieve this, a geolocation filter was applied, 
targeting tweets were explicitly tied to an Omani geographical location. While “most tweets are not 
associated with geographic location” (Davis et al., 2011, p.739), meaning they do not explicitly 
indicate the location from which they were posted, their geographic origin can often be determined 
through the metadata embedded within them. To find the embedded location, geoparsing was 
employed; a process described as “automated place-name recognition within text strings which 
converts these names into longitude/latitude coordinates” (Janowicz & Kebler, 2008, p.1129). 
However, clear location cues are not always present in tweets due to the use of textual abbreviations 
and non-standard vocabulary (Cheng et al, 2010). This limitation can result in compiling a dataset 
that may not accurately reflect the intended geolocation. This issue was specifically addressed and 
resolved during the data cleaning process. 
The second sampling criterion applied was language. Since this research focuses on Omani X users, 
the dataset was filtered to include tweets written in the Arabic language. This decision was based on 
the fact that Arabic is the primary language of communication in Oman. This criterion is particularly 
significant because language is not merely a means of communication but also a carrier of cultural 
expectations and norms as will be demonstrated in the results chapters (4,5,6 and 7). As Schieffelin, 
Woolard, and Kroskrity (1998) highlight, language plays a crucial role in shaping and reflecting 
societal values, making the decision to filter for Arabic essential to accurately represent Omanis. 
To retrieve tweets using the predefined hashtags and keywords listed above (see table 3.1 & table 
3.2 ), along with geolocation and language criteria, Vicinitas was employed . Vicinitas is an online 
analytics tool designed for tracking hashtags, keywords, accounts and websites. The request to 
Vicinities was submitted on 16 October 2020 to collect tweets spanning from 2011 to 2020, capturing 
nearly a decade of discussion on offence and incivility within Omani society. The retrieved data was 
provided in a single Excel file containing links to the original tweets, along with metadata such as 
timestamps and geolocations (see figure 3.2). The dataset included approximately 1,500 tweets 
under hashtags related to taking offence and around 400 tweets under hashtags relevant to incivility. 
These tweets were organised in reverse chronological order, reflecting their timeline. While the 
dataset was extensive, it required a thorough cleaning process to ensure accuracy and relevance.  
46 
 
 
Figure 3.2: Snapshot of retrieved X data with metadata 
                 The following section details the steps taken to prepare the data for analysis process. 
 
3.7.3 X Data Cleaning 
The first version of the file obtained from Vicintas was not ideal for use immediately in building the 
corpora; thus, additional steps had to be followed to clean, refine, and standardise the data. Firstly, 
once confronted with the raw data, information about the users’ accounts and names was removed 
from the dataset to protect their identity, aligning with ethical guidelines for research involving social 
media data (Townsend & Wallace, 2016). Furthermore, unwanted metadata such as HTML tags, 
links, and images were removed, as these elements do not contribute to text analysis and can 
interfere with the tools used for quantitative analysis. 
Moreover, standardising the spelling of keywords and hashtags used in the tweets was an essential 
step in preparing the corpus for the quantitative analysis. Terms associated with taking offence (e.g., 
 إساءة#  ،إهانة  ،مسيئة# ،مستاء #) and incivility (e.g.,  الئق  غير#  ،حضاري  غير#) were carefully standardised to ensure 
consistency. Spelling variations, such as the difference between “إساءة” and “ اساءة ”, which were 
prevalent in the data received, could result in inaccurate analysis outcomes, especially when using 
text analysis tools such as Sketch Engine. For example, frequency counts might treat these 
variations as entirely different words, despite them being the same but simply misspelled. This 
discrepancy would reduce the reliability of frequency data and impede pattern recognition (Baroni & 
Bernardini, 2004). Furthermore, beyond word frequency, analytical methods like collocation and 
47 
 
concordance rely on consistent word forms to identify accurate patterns. Without standardised 
spelling, the analysis tools may fail to detect significant collocations, resulting in incomplete insights. 
After standardising the spelling, the data was separated into six different Word documents, with each 
document containing tweets corresponding to one of the six keywords. This organisation ensured 
that all tweets related to specific keywords were grouped together, making the data easier to manage. 
Upon closer inspection of the data, decisions had to be made regarding which tweets to include in 
the corpus. While retaining all contributions might initially seem ideal, it soon became apparent that 
some contributions were problematic. One major challenge was that many of the original tweets 
associated with the hashtags had been deleted, rendering them unavailable and making it difficult to 
fully understand the context of certain tweets that were linked to the hashtag. Additionally, some of 
the tweets linked to the hashtags were irrelevant to the research; for example, some contained 
poems that included the keywords but were unrelated to the subjects being studied. These tweets 
were excluded to maintain the relevance and focus of the corpus. 
Moreover, despite applying geolocation filters designed to capture tweets originating from Oman, 
some tweets from neighbouring countries, such as the UAE and Saudi Arabia, were included due to 
the proximity of locations. A significant amount of time was spent manually identifying and removing 
these tweets to ensure the dataset’s accuracy. Additionally, some of the tweets containing the 
keywords were unrelated to Oman and were written by non-Omanis, despite having an Omani 
geolocation tag; these tweets were also excluded to maintain the relevance of the corpus. 
Furthermore, many tweets in the dataset were duplicated, either as identical posts or retweets shared 
multiple times by different users. Retweets, which are simply reposts of existing content, were 
deemed of no value for this research as their frequency does not add any additional insight to the 
content itself; therefore, they were removed. With the dataset now refined and organised, the next 
section will outline the data analysis process. 
3.7.4 Data Analysis in Phase One 
The data analysis in Phase One for both Taking Offence and Incivility, involved two integrated layers 
of analysis: quantitative and qualitative approaches. The first layer employed quantitative corpus 
analysis conducted with the help of Sketch Engine, which offered key linguistic insights and patterns. 
These findings informed the second layer, a qualitative thematic analysis. These two layers were 
designed to complement each other, with the findings from the quantitative analysis shaping and 
guiding the focus of the qualitative analysis. 
48 
 
This integrated approach ensured a more comprehensive understanding of the data by combining 
the strengths of both methodologies. 
3.7.4.1 First Layer: Quantitative Corpus Analysis Using Sketch Engine 
The first layer of analysis was conducted with the help of Sketch Engine on the cleaned and 
organised corpus. Sketch Engine is an advanced corpus analysis tool specifically designed for 
processing and analysing large volumes of textual data. 
It offers a range of features for exploring linguistic patterns, including word frequency, collocations, 
concordances, and N-grams (Kilgarriff, Baisa, Bušta, Jakubíček, Kovář, Michelfeit, Rychlý and 
Suchomel, 2014). These tools were instrumental in analysing both Taking Offence and Incivility 
corpora. Each hashtag corpus was analysed individually to allow a focused exploration of the specific 
linguistic patterns and features associated with each hashtag or keyword. This individualised 
approach ensured that the distinct contexts and characteristics of each corpus were thoroughly 
examined, providing a nuanced understanding of the linguistic structure, recurring patterns, and 
themes linked to each hashtag. 
Starting with Taking Offence corpora, two types of analysis were conducted: N-gram analysis and 
frequency analysis. N-gram refers to a sequence of words where n signifies the number of words in 
the sequence. For instance, a 1-gram consists of a single word, and a 2- gram consists of two 
consecutive words, and a 3-gram consists of three consecutive words. In this research, N-gram 
analysis was employed to identify words that appear before and /or after the hashtag. This focus 
helped reveal how Omani people construct narratives when discussing taking offence, offering a 
detailed perspective on the underlying linguistic structures. For instance, the keyword “إهانة” (“insult”) 
was consistently followed by “ل” (“to”), indicating that the expression typically introduces the recipient 
of the insult. In the corpus associated with #insult, analysing bigrams (combinations of 1-grams and 
2-grams) provided insights into the common targets of offence in Omani society. 
Moreover, frequency analysis was also employed in this corpus. Frequency analysis examines how 
often a particular word or phrase appears within a text or a collection of texts. In the taking offence 
corpora, the frequency of N-grams was explored in relation to the base word, which served as the 
anchor for the analysis. The base word refers to the keyword or hashtag around which N-grams were 
analysed and acted as the starting point for this exploration. 
For example, consider the bigram “insult to Oman”, where the base word is “insult”, the 1-gram is 
“to”, and the 2-gram is “Oman”. By investigating the frequency with which this bigram appeared in 
49 
 
the corpus, it became evident that Omani X users often discussed insults in connection with the 
nation. 
Moving to the analysis of Incivility corpora, N-gram and frequency analysis were also employed along 
with concordance analysis. These methods allowed for the identification of dominant patterns as well 
as the contextual nuances in how incivility was described, offering valuable insights into the linguistic 
and the dynamics of incivility reflected in the corpora. 
The analysis of incivility began with a 1-gram analysis, focusing on identifying what comes before 
the base word, which in this case is the keyword. This step helped uncover linguistic patterns that 
provide context to the base word, shedding light on how users frame discussions of uncivil behaviour. 
To illustrate this, in the phrase “  الئق غير منظر”, (“inappropriate view”), the 1-gram “ منظر”, (“view”) 
precedes the base word “ الئق غير”, (“inappropriate”) (see figure 3.3). This pattern highlights that the 
discussions of incivility that often emphasise visual elements, suggesting that societal concerns 
around appearance play a significant role in defining what is deemed inappropriate. 
 
 
Figure 3.3 : Highlighted 1-gram pattern for “  الئق  غير  منظر” (“inappropriate view”) 
 
To further support this N-gram findings, the frequency of the 1-gram was examined. Counting the 
frequency of “منظر” preceding “ الئق غير” provided additional evidence of the prominence of visual 
references in the discourse. By quantifying how often this 1-gram appears, the analysis validated the 
observed linguistic pattern and reinforces the conclusion that concerns related to visual 
appropriateness are central to discussions of incivility. 
50 
 
This systematic approach ensures that the N-gram findings are not only identified but also supported 
by measurable data, offering a more robust understanding of themes emerging from the corpora. In 
addition to n-gram analysis and its frequency, further analysis was conducted to delve deeper into 
the incivility corpora. This included running a concordance analysis to examine the textual context 
surrounding the keywords. Concordance, as defined by Kübler (2014, p.197), refers to “a list 
displaying all occurrences of a word in a text with its context”. 
The decision to utilise concordance analysis, rather than relying solely on N-gram analysis as was 
done with the Taking offence corpora, was driven by the limitations of N-gram analysis. N-gram is 
restricted to examining short sequences of words (e.g., 1-gram, 2-gram, or 3-gram), which proved 
inadequate for capturing the broader context of what X users considered uncivil. During the initial 
review of the incivility data, it was observed that users often articulated their perceptions in extended 
sequences, frequently exceeding 6-grams. This limitation made concordance analysis essential to 
uncover what the users perceived as uncivil behaviour ( see figure 3.4). 
 
Figure 3.4: Sample concordance lines generated for the keyword “ غير حضاري” 
(“uncivil”) from incivility corpora 
51 
 
After generating the concordance list, the analysis focused on extracting specific segments that were 
directly related to what the users considered حضاري  غير (uncivil), that is the phrase highlighted in red in 
Figure 3.4 rather than analysing the entire posts. The concordance list provided a detailed view of the 
contextual usage of the keywords. This selective approach ensured that the focus remained on 
elements most relevant to understanding what X users considered uncivil. By isolating these key 
elements, the analysis prioritised capturing the instances within the concordance lines where uncivil 
acts or behaviours were described. These lines served to highlight examples of incivility, which were 
later explored in greater depth during the thematic analysis phase to understand the underlying 
norms associated with the behaviour. 
One of these segments was extracted from the concordance list; the analysis proceeded by counting 
the occurrence of these segments. This additional step allowed for the identification of patterns in 
the frequency and distribution of uncivil acts or behaviours as described in the data. The counting 
process involved systematically reviewing the extracted concordance segments to determine how 
often particular phrases or descriptions of uncivil acts appeared within the data. This process was 
conducted manually because the phrasing used to describe uncivil behaviour varied across the 
dataset. Users expressed similar ideas in different ways, making it essential to account for semantic 
variations, where conceptually similar acts were described using different words or expressions. 
The automated frequency count in Sketch Engine was not used because such tools rely on exact 
word matches and are limited in their ability to identify semantic variations or account for linguistic 
nuances. Conducting the counting manually ensured that all meaningful expressions of incivility, 
regardless of how they were phrased, were captured. Moreover, single-occurrence instances where 
a description appeared only once were also included in the analysis. While these segments may 
seem insignificant at first glance, many of them reflected the same underlying semantic meaning as 
more frequent patterns but were expressed using different phrases. Less common expressions of 
incivility were also included to ensure that the analysis captured the full range of ways in which users 
perceived and described behaviour as uncivil. This approach allowed the analysis to account for both 
common patterns and less frequent, yet equally relevant instances of what is considered uncivil, 
providing a more comprehensive understanding of the data. Once the concordance frequencies were 
identified, further contextual details were explored. 
Recent research in incivility has emphasised that understanding the situational environment in which 
the act occurs is important, and overlooking it can lead to misinterpretation of communicative 
behaviour (Litvinenko, 2023). In light of this argument, identifying the setting in which the 
52 
 
described acts occurred was a necessary step toward understanding perceptions of incivility. This 
process involved a manual review of the concordance result to look for locations connected with the 
previously extracted segments. 
To summarise the first layer of analysis, quantitative methods, including N-gram, frequency, and 
concordance analysis, were employed to systematically examine the X corpus. These methods were 
instrumental in identifying key linguistic patterns, and recurring themes, and the triggers associated 
with discussions of offence and incivility. While this quantitative analysis provided valuable insights 
into prominent patterns, it laid the groundwork for a deeper exploration of the phenomena. 
Building on this foundation, the next section introduces the second layer of analysis, which employed 
thematic analysis to delve deeper into the results highlighted by the quantitative analysis. The 
transition from quantitative-driven analysis to qualitative analysis was not merely a methodological 
shift but a progression necessary to achieve a comprehensive understanding of taking offence and 
incivility. The themes explored in the second layer through thematic analysis were grounded in the 
language and discourse previously analysed. 
3.7.4.2 Second Layer: Qualitative Thematic Analysis 
The second layer of the analysis involved a qualitative thematic analysis of the X data, informed by 
the patterns identified during the first layer of quantitative analysis. This phase aimed to delve deeper 
into the expectations and norms that were violated by closely examining tweets and uncovering their 
underlying meaning within their specific contexts. 
Thematic analysis as defined by Braun and Clarke (2006, p.8) is a method for “identifying, analysing 
and reporting patterns (themes) within data”. It goes beyond simply counting explicit words or 
phrases and instead focuses on identifying and interpreting both explicit and implicit ideas within the 
data, referred to as themes (Guest, MacQueen, and Namey, 2012). The explicit ideas are examined 
at the semantic level, which involves analysing the surface content of the data. This includes 
interpreting the direct language used in the tweets, specific words, phrases, and expressions to 
understand what is overtly communicated. By focusing on the semantic content, the study identifies 
how users articulate their experiences in relation to taking offence and incivility. 
In contrast, the implicit ideas are explored at the latent level, where analysis goes beyond the 
participant-expressed meanings to uncover the underlying norms and expectations that inform the 
semantic expression. At this level, the analysis “tends to bring analyst’s framework to bear on the data 
and are built around concepts that help explain the data” (Willig & Rogers, 2017, p.23). 
53 
 
3.7.4.3 Conducting thematic analysis 
To analyse the qualitative X data, this study adapted Braun and Clarke’s (2006) six-step framework 
for thematic analysis, ensuring a rigorous and systematic approach. Given that quantitative analysis 
had already been conducted on the same X dataset during Phase One, the findings from Phase One 
provided a solid foundation for guiding the thematic analysis. These insights informed the coding 
process and guided the themes’ identification, enabling a more focused and comprehensive 
exploration of the data. 
While the X dataset contained a rich and extensive body of data, its volume became manageable after 
the quantitative analysis. Consequently, manual coding was chosen for the analysis, as the patterns 
identified in Phase One made this approach both feasible and effective. The following table 3.3 
summarises all the steps undertaken, and each step involved:
