Following the completion of the thematic analysis in Phase One, which provided foundational insight 
into the expectations and norms surrounding Taking Offence and Incivility, this study transitions to 
Phase Two in the following section. Phase Two begins with introducing diary reports as a data source 
and discussing how they were constructed to explore the two phenomena separately. It then 
discusses the participant selection, methods of diary report distribution and the process of cleaning 
the data to prepare it for analysis. Finally, the thematic analysis of the diary report data is discussed 
using Braun and Clarke’s six-step framework to identify key themes and patterns. 
3.8 Phase two: Data Collection and Analysis 
3.8.1 Diary Report as a Source of Data 
In Phase Two of the study, a diary report was used to collect written accounts of individuals narrating 
incidents where they felt offended or encountered behaviour they considered uncivil in public spaces. 
Designed to capture the “little experiences of everyday life” (Wheeler & Reis, 1991, 
56 
 
p.340), the diary report allowed participants to document these moments as they recalled them, 
providing deeper insight into how they interpreted and evaluated such occurrences. 
The fundamental benefit of using a diary in this research is that it allows the investigation of narrated 
events and experiences in their natural and spontaneous context (Reis, 1994). While the participants 
were guided by carefully formulated questions, they still had the freedom and flexibility to describe 
incidents in their own words, emphasising aspects they found most relevant, striking, or emotionally 
significant. Additionally, it facilitates the collection of “reliable person-level information” (Bolger, Davis 
and Rafaeli, 2003, p.581). This reliability stems from the fact that the participants are directly 
recounting incidents they experienced or witnessed, providing a firsthand or closely observed 
account of how offence and incivility are perceived in real-life contexts. By capturing these narratives, 
the diary report provided rich, personal accounts that reflect individuals’ interpretations and 
evaluations. Therefore, the diary report was employed as a key data collection tool in Phase Two to 
gather detailed, personal narratives from the participants about their experiences with taking offence 
and incivility. 
It is important to acknowledge that although diary reports offer valuable access to participants’ 
evaluations of offence and incivility, they do not capture events as they occurred in the moment. 
Ideally, phenomena such as offence and incivility are best studied as they unfold in real life. However, 
this was not feasible, as these incidents usually occur unexpectedly sometimes in private or even in 
sensitive settings, and even when they happen in public, observing enough relevant cases would 
require a considerable amount of time. For this reason, the diary report relies on participants’ 
retrospective accounts rather than real-time observation. Nevertheless, participants were given the 
freedom to describe incidents in their own words and highlight what they found meaningful or 
significant, providing rich insight into how they understood and evaluated the incidents.  
3.8.2 The Construction of Diary Reports 
In this research, two diary reports were constructed based on respectively based on the broader 
understanding of offence and incivility developed in Phase One (see Appendix A). The findings from 
Phase One played a crucial role in guiding the design of the diary report. They guided the wording of 
the questions, their sequence, and the depth of responses required. 
The analysis of X data provided insights into how offence and incivility were discussed, including the 
language people used and how they framed their experiences. This ensured that the diary report 
questions were phrased in a way that matched how people naturally talked about these experiences.  
57 
 
Instead of using general theoretical wording such as “what makes you take offence,” which focuses 
on an abstract concept and may lead to generalised or opinion-based responses, the diary report 
questions were designed to elicit experience-driven responses (see section 3.6.2). 
Additionally, the sequence of questions in the diary report were guided by patterns observed in Phase 
One. The X data often showed that when discussing offence, people first described the event itself 
before reflecting, sometimes on why they found it offensive. This informed the logical progression of 
the diary report questions, which first asked participants to recount the incidents, then encouraged 
them to reflect on their feelings and reasoning. 
Beyond the wording and sequence of the questions, the findings from Phase One also influenced 
how much detail participants were encouraged to provide in their responses. The analysis of X data 
revealed that discussions of offence and incivility was often brief and lacked personal and contextual 
depth, highlighting a need for more elaborate and reflective accounts. This insight directly informed 
the diary report by shaping how responses were elicited, ensuring that participants were encouraged 
to expand on their experiences rather than providing brief answers. 
While both reports followed a similar structure, they were tailored to address distinct research 
questions. Each question was carefully crafted to guide participants in narrating incidents, ensuring 
the collection of meaningful and detailed responses. The two diary reports consisted of two parts to 
facilitate the collection of both structured and open-ended responses. The following section outlines 
the construction of each diary report, beginning with the Taking Offence report and followed by the 
Incivility report. 
3.8.2.1 Diary Report 1: Taking offence 
Part one consisted of a simple Yes/No question: ‘Have you ever been offended, or has anyone you 
know been offended?’ This question served multiple purposes. Firstly, it aimed to establish whether 
the participant had relevant experiences to share. Secondly, it was designed to prompt reflection on 
either personal experiences or second-hand accounts of offence, fostering a sense of openness. 
By giving the participants, the option to narrate either their own experience or someone else’s, the 
question fulfilled two key purposes. First, it reduced the pressure participants might feel when sharing 
personal and sensitive experiences. Second, it allowed for the collection of diverse perspectives on 
offence, whether participants were directly involved in the event or recounting the experience of 
someone they know. 
Part two included two open-ended questions for participants who answered ‘Yes’ in Part One. The 
58 
 
first prompt asked, “tell us more about it (where did it happen or the person you know? and what 
exactly happened)”? This question invited participants to reconstruct the incident in detail, allowing 
for an in-depth exploration of the context and the circumstances surrounding the act. By asking ‘where’ 
and ‘what happened’, the question aimed to gather critical details that contribute to the understanding 
of the perceived offence. To explain in detail, ‘Where’ provided insight into the setting which could 
influence social norms and expectations, while ‘what happened’ captured the specific actions or 
words that triggered the offence revealing the behaviour that was evaluated as offensive. 
The final question in the diary report was “How did this situation make you feel, and why?” This 
question aimed to explore both the emotional and interpretative aspects of the participant’s 
experience. By asking “how,” it examined the participants’ emotional reactions, shedding light on the 
nature of their feelings, such as anger, shame or disappointment. These emotions are significant 
because individuals tend to react strongly when something they care deeply about is violated 
(Miller,1993). The “why” component complemented this by delving deeper into the reasoning behind 
these emotional repones, uncovering the underlying expectations that were breached. This aspect 
was critical in this study because it provided insight into the participants’ interpretations of the 
incidents and expectations they believed were violated. Understanding why participants felt the way 
they did allowed the research to move beyond the trigger which is available at the surface-level to 
trigger to explore the broader expectations that informed their perceptions of offence. See a copy of the 
Taking Offence diary report (the original Arabic version) Appendix [A] and English version Appendix 
[E] 
3.8.2.2 Diary Report 2: Incivility 
Part One consisted of a single Yes/No question which stated, “In you daily life, have you ever 
encountered or witnessed behaviour that you consider inappropriate or unacceptable in public 
spaces?” This question served as a filter to identify participants with relevant experiences of incivility, 
ensuring that only those with such experience proceeded to the open-ended questions in Part Two. 
The inclusion of ‘public spaces’ narrowed the scope of the inquiry to behaviours observed or 
experienced in a public environment, which aligns with what this study focuses on. 
Part Two, consisted of two open-ended questions for participants who answered ‘Yes’ in the first part 
of the diary report. Participants were asked “where and what exactly happened”. The ‘where’ aspect 
of the question was particularly significant in the context of incivility as the location provided context 
for understanding why certain behaviours were perceived as uncivil in specific environments. 
Identifying the “where” offered a clearer understanding of the expectations of behaviour tied to the 
location. 
59 
 
It was followed by the question “How did you feel at that moment, and why?” This question served 
a dual purpose. First, it aimed to explore the participants’ immediate emotional reaction to the uncivil 
behaviour. Secondly, the follow up query “why” was particularly crucial in this study, especially when 
considered alongside the previous questions. It encouraged participants to reflect more deeply on 
the incidents, guiding them through this question to articulate the reasons behind their feelings. This 
reasoning offered valuable insights into the specific norms participants believed had been violated. 
See a copy of the Incivility diary report (the original Arabic version) Appendix [A] and English version 
Appendix [F]. 
3.8.3 Selection of Participants: 
The participants of the diary report were carefully selected to ensure a diverse range of perspectives 
on offence and incivility within Omani society. The recruitment process was designed to capture a 
broad cross-section of the Omani population, emphasising participants’ lived experiences and 
perceptions rather than specific demographics characteristics such as age, gender, social role or 
educational background. The decision not to collect demographic information was intentional and 
aligned with the study’s objective. Collecting demographic data might have shifted the emphasis 
towards analysing how demographics might shape perception, potentially detracting from the core 
aim of uncovering shared norms, and expectations. While demographic differences in evaluating the 
perceived offence and incivility might be important in future research, this study prioritised an 
inclusive and exploratory framework to capture the complexity of these phenomena across Omani 
society. 
To achieve this, the participants for this study were selected strategically using two primary sampling 
methods: snowball sampling for online recruitment and purposive sampling for in-person recruitment. 
These methods were employed to ensure the inclusion of participants from diverse walks of life while 
achieving both depth and breadth in the data collection (Teddlie & Yu, 2009). The practical 
implementation of these strategies is detailed in the diary report distribution section. It is important to 
note that the participants for the diary report on Taking Offence and Incivility were intentionally 
selected as separate groups. This approach was adopted to avoid any potential confusion between 
offensive behaviour and uncivil acts. By using distinct samples for each study, the research ensured 
that the participants could focus exclusively on the specific concept being explored, enhancing the 
reliability and clarity of the data collected. 
 
60 
 
3.8.4 Diary Report Distribution 
To operationalise the sampling strategies outlined in the previous section, this study used both online 
and paper-based diary reports to collect data from Omani participants. Online diary reports were 
distributed via WhatsApp, leveraging snowball sampling to reach a broad network of participants. In 
contrast, the paper-based diary reports were shared in public spaces through purposive sampling to 
engage participants from diverse backgrounds. This combined approach allowed for a wide range of 
participants to share their experiences. The details of each distribution method are provided in the 
following subsection: 
3.8.4.1 Online Distribution via WhatsApp 
Online diary reports were initially distributed via WhatsApp using a snowball sampling method. This 
is a recruitment strategy that relies on recruiting participants through existing social networks (Dattalo, 
2008), making it particularly useful for identifying participants who are otherwise difficult to locate ( 
Biernacki & Waldorf, 1981). Since this research was conducted in the UK, while the target 
participants were based in Oman, snowball sampling was chosen as a practical approach to bridge 
geographical and logistical challenges and to effectively reach participants who might not have been 
accessible through traditional recruitment methods. This method relied on referrals, leveraging the 
social networks of initial participants to connect with a broader pool of relevant individuals. To begin 
the process, the diary report link, hosted on the Online Surveys platform, was shared via WhatsApp 
with a small group of participants who met the study’s criteria- Omani individuals. While this approach 
facilitated access to a broader pool of participants through referrals, it is recognised that the contacts 
within the initial circle of participants may lack the diversity required, as individuals within the same 
social network might often exhibit shared characteristics, such as educational level, socioeconomic 
status, or personal values. 
However, efforts were made to encourage participants to share the link across diverse social groups, 
thereby mitigating this limitation and enhancing the diversity of the sample. Each participant provided 
referrals to additional participants which created a chain of contacts. This process expanded the 
network of participants allowing the study to access a broader and more diverse pool of individuals. 
WhatsApp was selected as the platform for distributing the online diary report link because it is the 
most widely used social media application in Oman. According to data shared by the Ministry of 
Transport, Communication and Information Technology (2020), approximately 92% of individuals 
over the age of 18 in Oman use WhatsApp. Unlike platforms dominated by specific age groups or 
educational levels, WhatsApp is used by a broad cross-section of society, making it an ideal tool for 
61 
 
reaching a diverse range of participants. Additionally, WhatsApp provided participants with a 
convenient way to access the diary report, allowing them to complete it at a time and place that suited 
their schedules. 
Furthermore, WhatsApp was also chosen for its functionality, which allowed the researcher to share 
the diary report link effectively beyond her immediate network. Using a friends-of-friends approach, 
the link reached participants across different regions of Oman, enabling the study to include a more 
diverse range of experiences. In addition to its reach, WhatsApp offered participants a private and 
convenient way to access the diary report. Receiving the link in a personal and comfortable 
environment likely encouraged participants to respond more openly. They completed the diary report 
at their own pace, free from the pressures of time constraints or the need for immediate submission. 
However, despite these advantages, challenges arose during the data collection process when 
reviewing the results midway. It became evident that some participants skipped questions or provided 
limited responses. 
The absence of direct engagement appeared to reduce the participants’ motivation to fully answer 
the questions or reflect deeply on the incidents they experienced. This observation underscored a 
key drawback of relying solely on an online method, as participants might not engage thoroughly 
when completing the report independently. Consequently, the need for a more interactive and 
supportive method became clear. 
3.8.4.2 Paper-Based Diary report Distribution 
To address the limitations observed in the online reports results, paper-based diary reports were 
immediately incorporated. The distribution of these reports followed a purposive sampling strategy 
targeting public spaces in Muscat such as cafes, parks and markets. 
These locations were selected for their potential to attract a diverse mix of Omani people, offering an 
ideal environment for collecting varied perspectives on offence and incivility within Omani society. 
Purposive sampling is particularly suited for this study in which participants are chosen based on their 
relevance to the research topic, as it allows researchers to focus on individuals likely to provide rich 
and meaningful data (Etikan, Musa & Alkassim, 2016). This approach also accounted for practical 
considerations, such as the access to these public spaces and the need for an efficient, cost-effective 
recruitment process. Public space required minimal logistical arrangement and allowed for timely 
recruitment of participants, making them a suitable choice. 
The participants were approached directly by the assistants of the researcher who lived in Oman, my  
62 
 
sisters, after being thoroughly briefed about the project and its objectives. Their presence during the 
distribution allowed for more direct engagement with participants, encouraging participants to 
complete the diary report while addressing any uncertainties. This interaction reduced the likelihood 
of skipped questions and contributed to more thoughtful responses. Additionally, the assistants’ 
presence fostered a sense of accountability; knowing that someone would follow up or return to 
collect the completed report reinforced the importance of their participation and encouraged them to 
complete the diary report conscientiously. 
Participants who agreed to complete the diary report were given time to do so, with flexibility built 
into the process. While some participants completed the diary report on the spot, others requested 
additional time. In such cases, the assistants accommodated their preferences by exchanging 
contact numbers, allowing participants to send a picture of the completed report via WhatsApp later. 
Additionally, some participants requested the assistant to return at a specified time to collect the 
completed diary report in person. This flexible and participant-centred approach was instrumental in 
maintaining engagement and ensuring high quality responses. Participants were not rushed, creating 
an environment where they could reflect thoughtfully on their experiences. 
At the same time, they were encouraged to answer all questions, resulting in richer and more 
complete data. Moreover, the personal follow-up for those who chose to send their responses later 
via WhatsApp significantly improved response rates, underscoring the value of direct interaction and 
personalised support in the data collection process. The integration of both online and paper-based 
diary reports was pivotal in this study. The online distribution via WhatsApp ensured wide reach and 
accessibility, capturing a diverse range of participants, while the paper-based method fostered 
deeper engagement, resulting in more detailed and thoughtful responses. This complementary use 
of methods enabled a comprehensive exploration of the phenomena of Taking Offence and Incivility. 
The next section delves into the process of cleaning the diary report preparing it for the analysis. 
3.8.5 Cleaning the Diary Report Data 
In Phase Two of the research, data cleaning was a crucial step before conducting thematic analysis. 
Since the data was collected through two methods, paper-based and online, several key tasks were 
involved in the cleaning process. It is important to note that the diary reports related to the topics 
Taking Offence and Incivility were cleaned separately. However, the cleaning process for both topics 
has a similar approach. 
Firstly, the responses from the paper-based diary reports were manually transcribed into a digital 
 
63 
 
 format to prepare them for the analysis phase. This step involved typing all the responses carefully 
into a Word document, ensuring that no data was omitted during transcription. However, one 
challenge encountered during this process was occasional difficulty in reading some handwriting. To 
address this, I consulted my assistants several times to clarify the content, ensuring the data 
remained as accurate and complete as possible. 
Furthermore, the inconsistencies in the data were addressed. These inconsistencies included 
skipped questions, lack of detail in responses, and occasional spelling errors. Skipped questions 
were more prevalent in the online diary reports. For instance, in the Taking Offence diary report, 
some participants did not answer the follow-up question (Part Two), which asked, “How did this 
situation make you feel, and why?”. This omission created a gap in understanding the data, as the 
question was essential for understanding the expectations that were violated rather than merely 
identifying the act of violation itself. Similarly, in the Incivility diary report, some participants provided 
partial answers. For example, participants often answered questions about the location and nature 
of the uncivil act but left the follow-up question, “How did you feel at that moment, and why?” 
unanswered or provided minimal information. To address these issues, I implemented a structured 
approach. To explain, skipped or incomplete responses were reviewed on a case-by-case basis. 
Entries that provided meaningful insights, even with minor omissions, were retained, while those 
lacking substantive information were excluded. 
Another issue addressed during the data cleaning process was spelling errors, which were 
particularly relevant because the diary reports were written in a mix of colloquial and standard Arabic. 
Colloquial Arabic, being primarily spoken and lacking formal spelling rules, often led to 
inconsistencies in how words were written. Meanwhile, errors in standard Arabic reflected challenges 
in switching between the two forms of the language. During the digitisation process, I corrected 
obvious spelling mistakes for clarity while ensuring the authenticity of the participants’ language was 
maintained. Preserving the original linguistic expression was crucial, as both colloquial and standard 
Arabic convey important contextual and cultural meaning that is central to understanding Taking 
Offence and Incivility. 
3.8.6 Phase 2: Diary Report Data Analysis 
Similar to the analysis of Twitter data in section (3.5.4.3), thematic analysis was employed in phase 
2 to identify, analyse, and interpret patterns within the diary reports collected for both Taking Offence 
and Incivility. However, unlike the X data analysis, where thematic analysis was informed by prior 
quantitative findings, the thematic analysis in this phase was purely qualitative and grounded in the 
64 
 
raw data and uninfluenced by previous analysis. To analyse the diary report data, Braun and Clarke’s 
six-phase framework as outlined in table (3.3) was applied, ensuring a systematic approach to 
identifying patterns and themes. NVivo software was utilised to manage and organise the data, 
streamlining the coding process. The software allowed efficient categorisation of the data at multiple 
levels, including organisation according to the structure of the diary report questions, and a deeper 
organisation at the level of themes within the responses. This structured approach facilitated a 
thorough analysis allowing for the identification of meaningful patterns and insights. 
3.8.6.1 Conducting the thematic analysis 
The thematic analysis began with familiarising myself with the data. At this initial stage, the diary 
report data, which had been cleaned and organised in Word files, was imported into Nvivo. I 
immersed myself in the data by reading through the diary entries multiple times to gain a thorough 
understanding of their content. This familiarisation process was crucial for identifying initial recurring 
ideas, and initial impressions that informed subsequent stages of the analysis. 
After becoming familiar with the data, I proceeded to generate initial codes in NVivo, the coding 
process was guided by the structure of the diary report, with each question serving as a framework 
for organising the responses (see Figure 3.5). I highlighted relevant portions of the text and assigned 
a descriptive label to them. Each diary entry was analysed line by line, ensuring no significant detail 
was overlooked. 
 
Figure 3.5: The initial guiding structure to code the responses 
 
The initial codes for the diary entry question “what happened?” reflected actions described by the 
participants that had made them take offence and were considered uncivil. At this point, the focus 
was on identifying and categorising the explicit behaviours or events mentioned in the responses, 
such as specific actions, words, or gestures that participants highlighted as problematic. These codes 
were grounded in the semantic content of the data, emphasising a detailed descriptive presentation 
of the participants’ experiences without delving into deeper interpretation. This approach ensured 
that the coding process captured the participants as they were expressed, providing a clear 
structured foundation for the later stages of the analysis where patterns and broader themes code 
65 
 
could be identified. By systematically coding these behaviours, the analysis began to reveal recurring 
actions that contributed to feelings of offence and perceptions of incivility. As the analysis progressed, 
I began grouping related codes into broader categories that captured common ideas (see Figure 3.6 
for taking offence and Figure 3.7 for incivility). 
 
 
 
Figure 3.6: Coding of taking offence 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Figure 3.7: Coding of incivility 
 
66 
 
 
For example, codes such as “false accusation” and “exclusion” were grouped under the category 
“Relational Transgression” reflecting behaviours that explicitly violated interpersonal expectations. 
Similarly, with incivility, codes like “crossing speed limit”, “overtaking cars from yellow line” and 
“taking more than one parking space” were categorised under “Not Following Traffic Regulations” 
which encompassed behaviours that explicitly broke societal rules of traffic. 
While these categories were meaningful and captured the semantic content of participants’ 
experiences, they remained descriptive in nature, focusing on what was explicitly stated in the data. 
To achieve a deeper understanding, it was essential to go beyond the surface-level description and 
analyse the latent meaning embedded within the accounts. This required exploring both the implicit 
and explicit assumptions, expectations, and social norms that influenced how the behaviours were 
perceived and evaluated. According to Braun and Clarke (2006, p.82), a theme “captures something 
important about the data in relation to the research question and represents some level of patterned 
response or meaning within the data set”. This perspective guided the analysis towards identifying 
broader themes that reflected underlying norms and expectations shaping participants’ responses. 
The process moved beyond the trigger, the behaviour itself, to explore the reasons why certain 
behaviours were considered offensive or uncivil, and to uncover the deeper personal, social, and 
cultural principles at play. 
The themes were then further refined to ensure they accurately represented the underlying norms 
and expectations. This involved a careful examination to confirm that each theme captured the 
essence of the participants’ responses and was well-supported by the data. The refinement process 
also ensured that the themes were distinct from one another while collectively contributing to a 
cohesive narrative. 
Themes that initially appeared overlapping were clarified and subdivided to better reflect the data. 
For example, themes such as “Traffic Regulation Norms” and “Respecting Public Spaces Norms” 
were initially grouped under one theme. However, upon further refinement, it became evident that 
these themes addressed distinct aspects of violated norms. Separating them ensured that each 
theme distinctly captured a type of broken norm. This refinement process enhanced both the clarity 
and the focus of the analysis. 
Following this, themes were named and defined to ensure they represented the core idea and aligned 
closely with the research questions. For instance, the theme “Adhering to Public Decency” was 
defined as the societal expectation to maintain appropriate behaviour in public spaces. This theme 
67 
 
encompassed incidents such as dressing inappropriately or engaging in behaviours considered 
obscene in public. 
In the final step of thematic analysis, the themes were synthesised into a coherent narrative, ensuring 
that they reflected the participants’ experiences and perceptions of taking offence and incivility in 
Oman. The examples provided by participants were used to illustrate the various ways norms and 
expectations were violated and how such violations shaped their understanding of perceived offence 
and incivility. Each theme was supported by data extracts that provided rich, detailed insights into 
how and why certain behaviours were considered offensive or uncivil. This comprehensive approach 
ensured that the analysis was deeply rooted in the participants’ lived experiences, providing a 
thorough representation of what makes Omani people take offence and the norms that shape their 
understanding of incivility within Omani society. The next section focuses on the theoretical 
framework that informed the analysis of this research. 
3.9 Conceptual Framework 
This study adapted the prismatic model of (im)politeness- as- perspective (Haugh, 2024) as the 
overarching conceptual structure for exploring the norms and expectations underlying perceptions of 
offence and incivility. The prismatic model provides a dynamic and multidimensional framework that 
moves beyond the typical binary distinction between first-order politeness and second-order 
politeness perspectives to capture the interplay between multiple layers of understanding (see Figure 
3.8) 
 
 
68 
 
Figure 3.8: Impoliteness as perspective (Haugh, 2024): integrating multiple 
perspectives for taking offence and incivility. 
 
 
Its model purpose by encompasses four different loci of understanding: 
 
1. Participant- meta-participant: The viewpoint of those directly engaged in an interaction; they 
are the (participants) narrating their personal experiences in the diary reports. Whereas (meta- 
participants) represent an understanding of individuals who are not directly involved in an event 
but engage with it through the actions or experiences of others, typically by observing, narrating, 
or commenting on it (Kadar & Haugh, 2013). They include some of the diary report narrators and 
X commenters. 
2.  Emic-etic: The emic perspective refers to the understanding of the insider, how members of a 
culture or community perceive, categorise, and interpret their own world (Kadar & Haugh, 2013). 
The way to elicit the emic perspective is to ask people about what they think and feel to uncover 
how they make sense of their experiences. In this research, the emic perspective is accessed 
through the narratives of Omani participants in X and the diary report, which reveal how culturally 
specific expectations and norms influence their judgements of what is considered offensive or 
uncivil. 
Understanding the emic perspective involves identifying culturally specific judgements about 
what is considered “constructive” and “nonconstructive” and exploring how thoughts and 
behaviours are evaluated as “appropriate or inappropriate” within their social context based on 
the assumption that individuals have internalised cultural norms and expectations that guide 
these evaluations. Through these insights, emic analysis seeks to describe the underlying 
“program” that shapes native judgements of contrast and appropriateness (Harris, 1976, p.8). 
Whereas (etic) is “an understanding formulated in terms of conceptual schemes and categories 
regarded as meaningful and appropriate to outsiders, that is those who are treated as lying outside 
the relational network in question” (Kadar & Haugh, 2013, p.102). The etic perspective in this 
research is the researcher bringing her own structure and system and her observations of the 
inside view, interpreting the inside in reference to the outside starting point. 
3. Lay observer-professional analyst: It is an understanding that emerges from non-expert 
individuals who describe behaviours based on everyday understanding, without formal 
theoretical grounding. In my research, the lay observers were the meta-participants in X and the 
participants in the diary reports who described their experiences of offence and incivility based 
69 
 
on their everyday understanding. In contrast, the professional analyst perspective involves my 
role as the researcher, where I systematically interpret these evaluative moments using 
evidence-based interpretation methods such as corpus analysis and thematic analysis. 
4.  Folk theoretic- scientific theoretic: Understanding is derived from personal experiences, 
cultural beliefs, and social interactions rather than from scientific reasoning. In this research, the 
folk theoretic perspective was reflected in the participants’ diary reports, where their 
understanding of what they consider offensive and uncivil was derived from personal 
experiences, cultural beliefs, and social interactions. These narratives provide insights into 
everyday, intuitive evaluations. Whereas (scientific-theoretic) refers to an understanding that 
involves systematically defined and formalised interpretations of interpersonal phenomena, 
developed and shared among scientific researchers. In this research, I adapted a theoretical 
framework and employed certain analysis methods such as corpus analysis and thematic 
analysis to formalise the intuitive narratives provided by the participants. 
Having established how this study adapted the prismatic model of (im)politeness- as- perspective 
(Haugh, 2024) and how each perspective informed the analysis of the phenomena of taking offence 
and incivility, the discussion now turns to the scientific-theoretical dimension in more detail, where 
the analytical framework guiding this study is explained. 
 
                  3.7.1 Adapting Spencer-Oatey’s framework  
 
Within the scientific-theoretic dimension of the prismatic model that was introduced in the previous 
section (see Section 3.7), this research developed a working theoretical framework informed by 
Spencer-Oatey’s (2000) bases of rapport management framework to further analyse the phenomena 
of taking offence and incivility (see Figure 3.9). The decision to adapt Spencer-Oatey’s framework 
was not predetermined; rather, it emerged from the inductive analysis (see Section 3.1 for detailed 
explanation of the inductive and deductive analytic approach). Consequently, the framework is not 
applied as a fixed model but rather evolved through engagement with the data. In the early stages 
of thematic analysis, the inductive processes of categorisation and coding revealed recurrent 
patterns that reflected a strong concern with how individuals wished to be perceived as individuals 
and as members of wider community and in relation to others. These concerns mapped closely onto 
Spencer-Oatey’s notions of face sensitivities, and sociality rights and obligations, which provided a 
lens for understanding the underlying norms and expectations evident in the data. These emergent 
patterns, therefore, informed the decision to integrate and adapt Spencer’ Oatey’s framework for this 
study. 
70 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
Figure 3.9: Adapted version of bases of management rapport 
(Spencer- Oatey, 2008) 
 
While Spencer-Oatey’s model aligned closely with the concerns emerging from the data, it was also 
important to recognise that this model is considered a Western model, grounded in a predominantly 
Western scholarly tradition, and developed through analyses of Western sociocultural contexts. Its use 
in this study was therefore not prescriptive but reflexive, meaning that the framework was adapted 
critically with attention to both my insider understanding of Omani cultural norms and to the meanings 
and evaluative patterns expressed by participants. The need for this adaptation also arose from the 
fact that Omani data showed patterns of judgement that extended beyond what the original Western 
model was designed to accommodate.  Therefore, this reflexive adaptation ensured that the framework 
reflected the cultural priorities and normative expectations evident in the Omani data, allowing the 
analysis to remain aligned with the lived realities and the evaluative judgements articulated by 
participants. 
 The decision to adapt this model was also driven by the need for a solid analytical tool for this research. 
Although the model originates in Western contexts and does not fully capture all aspects of offence 
and incivility in the Omani context, it nonetheless provides a robust foundation to support the analysis. 
This choice was further shaped by the fact that existing models grounded in Arab societies do not 
currently offer a fully developed analytical framework suitable for the type of analysis conducted in this 
research. There are, however, many contributions ranging from early studies to more recent studies, 
such as Feghail (1997) and Al Zidjaly (2009, 2017) that offer culturally distinctive patterns in Arabic 
speaking contexts. While these works enrich understanding and help to inform research in the region, 
they do not extend to an analytical model that can be systematically applied to the type of analysis 
71 
 
undertaken here. Having established the justification for adapting Spencer-Oatey’s model, the following 
section will outline the core elements of the framework to show how it informs the analysis presented 
in this study.  
One of the central components of this model is the notion of face sensitivities, as outlined in Spencer-
Oatey’s (2008) rapport management framework. In this research, face sensitivities act as a rationale 
for understanding people’s evaluative judgements of perceived offence and incivility. They provide a 
lens for understanding how individuals assess behaviour in relation to their sense of worth, dignity, 
and socially valued attributes such as respect, honour, status, and reputation, and competence. The 
concept of face in Spencer’s model (2008) is understood through three dimensions of identity, the 
self as an individual, the self as a member of a group and the self in relationship with others.  Table 
3.4 below summarises how face is understood in this research, drawing on Goffman’s foundational 
definition and Spencer-Oatey’s distinction between quality face and social identity face. In this study, 
these dimensions were adapted as analytic guides for interpreting the face related concerns evident 
in the data. This adaptation provided a structured way of examining how participants anchored their 
evaluation in different aspects of self.

to

(1967,p.5): "the positive

effectively

for

(related to the self as an individual): "We have a fundamental desire

Table 3.4: Categories in rapport management 
 
The second component of the model concerns sociality rights and obligations, which provide a basis 
for interpreting participants’ evaluations of behaviours they described as offensive or uncivil 
(Spencer-Oatey & Kadar, 2021). Sociality rights are used to identify the expectations participants hold 
regarding what they believe they are entitled to receive from others in everyday life, while sociality 
obligations are used to capture what they believe others are obligated to do or refrain from doing 
(Spencer-Oatey & Kadar, 2021). In this research, sociality rights and obligations guide the analysis 
by directing attention to moments in participants’ accounts where they refer to expectations they feel 
have not been met and to duties, they believe others should either carry out or avoid breaching. 
However, it must be noted that Spencer-Oatey’s framework model, as outlined in Table 3.4, specifies 
only two types of sociality rights, equity rights and association rights, and describes sociality 
obligations in broad terms. These rights categories are drawn on in this study because they align 
with the expectations reflected in participants’ accounts, although they do not encompass the full 
range of sociocultural expectations and norms evident in the data. Likewise, the model’s broad 
description of obligations provides a useful reference point but does not account for the types of 
obligations participants alluded to. Therefore, while Spencer-Oatey’s conceptualisation of sociality 
rights and obligations remains analytically valuable for this study, the model’s existing subcategories 
do not fully capture the breadth of expectations evident in participants’ accounts. The analysis in this 
study therefore builds on these two main categories by identifying additional context-specific forms 
 
 
73 
 
 of rights and obligations that emerged inductively from the data. These further rights and obligations 
are presented and discussed in detail in the data analysis chapters.       
3.10 Data Analysis Approach: inductive and deductive analysis 
Building on the theoretical framework discussed in the previous section, this study adopted a hybrid 
thematic analysis approach to analyse the data in both research phases, where it incorporated 
inductive and deductive analysis. The process primarily started with inductive analysis, as it focused 
on identifying patterns that emerged from participants’ evaluations. However, the analysis was also 
guided by deductive analysis, drawing on pre-existing concepts and the theoretical framework 
underpinning the study. This hybrid approach ensured that the analysis was rooted in the data while 
being supported by the theoretical lens, allowing for a robust exploration of the phenomena. 
The inductive aspect of the hybrid analysis was essential for ensuring that the findings were rooted 
in participants lived experiences and narratives. An inductive approach to to thematic analysis, as 
described by Braun and Clarke (2006), involves generating codes and themes directly from the raw 
data, without being constrained by pre-existing theoretical categories. This process enabled the 
recognition of patterns that emerged from participants accounts, capturing both personal experiences 
and perspectives shaped by indirect engagement through witnessing events or recounting others’ 
experiences of offence and incivility. It also facilitated an understanding of how individuals, as 
members of their own cultural and social environments, made sense of offence and incivility, 
revealing recurring themes in how behaviours were interpreted. It also brought to light culturally 
specific context sensitive patterns shedding light on social and cultural dimensions shaping these 
patterns. This approach further uncovered unexpected findings, including subtle norms that were 
particularly significant in shaping participants’ evaluations of offence and incivility. These insights, 
which might otherwise have been overlooked, offered a deeper understanding of what participants 
considered offensive and uncivil within their specific social contexts. 
Simultaneously, the deductive aspect of the analysis served as a guiding lens throughout the entire 
analysis process, alternating between operating in the background and moving into the foreground. 
In the early stages of the analysis, the deductive approach primarily operated in the background, 
serving as subtle yet active guide. Although the analysis at this stage focused on data-driven 
exploration, the deductive approach subtly influenced the focus of the coding process. To explain, by 
aligning the coding process with the study’s core constructs of offence and incivility, the deductive 
approach provided structure and focus. It ensured that the coding did not simply categorise behaviours 
labelled as ‘offensive’ and ‘uncivil’ but systematically captured key details about these incidents. This  
74 
 
included identifying the specific behaviours described (e.g., accusing someone, dressing 
immodestly), the context in which they occurred (e.g., road, park), and participants’ evaluations of 
why these behaviours were offensive or uncivil. In this way, the deductive framework provided 
direction and coherence to the analysis, even in the early stages, by ensuring that attention was 
systematically guided toward relevant aspects of the data, thereby laying the foundation for deeper 
exploration of norms and expectations in the later stages of the analysis. 
As the analysis advanced, the deductive approach moved into the foreground, where it played a 
more explicit role in naming and refining the themes and synthesising them into narratives. While the 
analysis was initially guided by participants’ explicit and implicit evaluations of taking offence and 
incivility, the patterns that emerged from the data highlighted recurring structures in how these 
behaviours were interpreted and evaluated. These structures revealed stable patterns in the 
reasoning participants used to make sense of their experiences, reflecting shared social norms and 
expectations. As these patterns became more evident, a systematic theoretical interpretation was 
required to refine and contextualise the findings. 
This shift emphasised the integration of theoretical grounding, ensuring that patterns were supported 
by broader analytical frameworks while remaining informed by participants’ perspectives. To achieve 
this, the analysis adapted the bases of Rapport Management framework which was modified to align 
with the dynamics revealed in the data. By adapting this framework, the analysis systematically 
connected participants’ evaluations to broader constructs that underpin them, such as face sensitivities 
and sociality rights and obligations. 
For instance, one recurring pattern in the data involved participants expressing feeling offended 
because they were not allowed to express themselves. This was initially coded inductively as being 
silenced. However, as these patterns became more evident, these instances were interpreted 
through the lens of association rights. It reflected an unfulfilment of association rights, which 
concerns individuals’ expectations to be included in interaction. The combination of inductive and 
deductive framing thus helped clarify not just what was offensive but why the participants evaluated 
it as offensive. 
By integrating inductive and deductive analysis, this study provided a comprehensive understanding 
of taking offence and incivility, bridging participant- driven insights with established theoretical 
structures. The inductive phase led the analysis, allowing patterns to be identified from participants’ 
evaluations, while deductive analysis remained in the background. As the analysis progressed,  
75 
 
deductive analysis took on a more prominent role, structuring these insights within broader theoretical 
perspectives. This interplay ensured that theoretical perspectives supported rather than 
overshadowed participant perspectives, reinforcing the hybrid analysis as essential for capturing both 
lived experiences and the underlying normative structures shaping the evaluations. 
3.11 Translation of Data 
In this research, the source language of the data gathered from X and the diary report was Arabic, 
while the target language used for reporting in this thesis was English. The decision to translate was 
essential to ensure the accessibility of the findings to an English-speaking academic audience and 
to align with the requirements of the thesis. This study required translating data that involved both 
colloquial Arabic and standard Arabic. Respondents used these forms of Arabic to narrate their 
stories, using the language they most preferred for sharing their experiences. Translation, as defined 
by Temple and Yong (2004), is “the process of transferring the meaning contained in one set of 
linguistic symbols to another across languages and cultures” (p.164). This highlights the complexity 
of translation, which extends beyond linguistic equivalence to include cultural understanding and 
contextual accuracy. 
One of the primary challenges I encountered during the translation process was ensuring that the 
cultural meaning of the expressions was retained. As Simon (1996) notes “translators must constantly 
make decisions about the cultural meanings which language carries and evaluates the degree to 
which the two different worlds they inhabit are the same” (p.8). This required careful assessment of 
whether specific expressions or terms in Arabic had an equivalent in English and determining the best 
way to present the original meaning without compromising its cultural significance. For instance, in 
some cases, literal translation risked losing the original intent, while overly interpretive translation 
risked altering the meaning altogether. My insider role as both researcher and translator enabled me 
to navigate these complexities effectively, drawing on my familiarity with the cultural and linguistic 
nuances of the participants’ language. 
The translation process was a critical component of this research, as the reliability of the findings 
depended on the accuracy and integrity of the translated material. Twinn (1997) emphasises that 
inaccuracies in translation can distort the meaning of the data and compromise the validity of 
research outcomes. Recognising this, the research employed a rigorous translation approach that 
aimed to preserve both the linguistic and cultural integrity of the original data. To further enhance 
accuracy, I collaborated with a certified translator who was also Omani and deeply familiar with the 
cultural and linguistic context of the participants. This collaboration complemented my insider 
76 
 
knowledge, allowing us to address cultural subtleties and linguistic ambiguities more effectively. 
3.12 Ethical Consideration 
The ethical considerations were a fundamental aspect of this research and were addressed at every 
stage of the study. Before any data collection commenced, this study received ethical approval from 
the Faculty Research Panel at Anglia Ruskin University. Given that two data collection methods were 
employed in this study, X and diary reports forms, the ethical considerations for each will be 
discussed separately. The ethics of using data collected from X has been, and continues to be, a 
topic of debate across disciplines, including the humanities and social sciences (Taylor & Pagliari, 
2017). A central issue in this debate is whether the consent of social media users is required for 
research purposes (Samuel & Buchanan, 2020). Samuel, Ahmed, Kara, Jessop, Quinton, and 
Sanger, (2018) and Zimmer (2020) argue that the mere fact that data is publicly available does not 
automatically make its use in research ethical, as some content, despite being accessible, may still 
be in some way private. 
Nevertheless, X’s policy explicitly states that “By publicly posting content when you tweet, you are 
directing us to disclose that information as broadly as possible…to make that information 
available to websites, apps, and others for their use - for example, displaying tweets on a news 
website or analysing what people say on Twitter” 1. This policy makes it clear that users who choose 
to post publicly are made aware upon registration, that their content can be used beyond the platform. 
Therefore, unless X revises its policies, the use of publicly available posts in research remains 
ethically permissible. 
In addition to the aforementioned ethical concerns, anonymity is a critical aspect that must be carefully 
considered in research involving publicly available social media data. While X’s policy permits the 
use of publicly accessible information, including usernames, ethical guidelines for research mandate 
the protection of user identities, requiring that all identifying details, such as usernames, profile 
pictures, and account handles, be anonymised or removed in accordance with established ethical 
guidelines (GSR, 2006). 
While the ethical considerations for X focused on issues related to consent and data usage policies, 
the diary report required a different ethical approach due to the direct involvement of participants. 
Unlike X, where data was publicly available, the diary report involved individuals sharing personal 
experiences, necessitating strict adherence to ethical guidelines to protect their anonymity, ensure 
voluntary participation, and safeguard their well-being. 
77 
 
Before participation, individuals were fully informed about the purpose and objectives of the study 
through a detailed participant information sheet and informed consent was obtained. Participants 
were made aware of how their responses would be used and their right to withdraw at any stage 
before data analysis without providing a reason. Participation was entirely voluntary, with no 
obligation to complete the report. Copies of the consent form and participants' information are 
provided in the Appendix [B ]. 
To maintain anonymity and confidentiality, no personal information was intentionally collected; 
however, any identifying details that participants voluntarily included in their responses, such as 
names, were removed during data cleaning. In line with these measures, all responses were securely 
stored in a protected file, ensuring compliance with the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) 
and the Data Protection Act (2018). Access to the data was strictly limited, with only the researcher 
having permission to view the files, further safeguarding participants’ confidentiality. Moreover, given 
that the diary report involved narrating personal experiences of offence and incivility, ethical 
considerations also focused on minimising potential distress. Participants were informed of potential 
emotional discomfort and were given the option to withdraw from the study at any time. 
The ethical considerations in this study were tailored to the different challenges posed by the use of 
X data and the diary report. While X data required handling publicly available content while ensuring 
anonymity, the diary report required informed consent, voluntary participation, and precautions to 
minimise potential distress. These measures, along with the university's ethical approval, ensured 
that the study upheld ethical research standards and maintained integrity throughout. 
3.13 Conclusion 
This chapter has outlined and critically discussed the methodological foundations and procedures 
adopted in this study. It detailed the philosophical orientation, research design, data collection 
methods and the challenges that I faced when collecting the data. It also highlighted the analytical 
approaches used to explore the two phenomena. At the end of the chapter, attention was also given 
to the ethical considerations and the translation process that were essential in ensuring the contextual 
accuracy and integrity of the research.

CHAPTER 4: RESULTS - PHASE 1 (A) 
 
 
4.1 Introduction 
This chapter presents the findings of Phase 1 of the study, which investigates how the offence is 
expressed, constructed, and evaluated within online discourse, specifically on X in the Omani 
context. As outlined in Chapter 3, the data for this phase were collected using four hashtags (see 
Section 4.2, Table 4.1) in the Arabic language. These hashtags were selected to capture a wide 
range of written expressions reflecting moments in which users expressed taking offence. This phase 
aims to offer an initial understanding of the nature of taking offence as it is reflected in everyday online 
interactions. 
The analysis undertaken in this chapter is structured in two layers. The first layer draws on a corpus- 
driven approach to examine the collected texts to identify dominant lexical patterns and collocational 
relationships and their frequencies in the dataset, particularly around the selected hashtags. Building 
on the corpus findings, the second layer of analysis applies thematic analysis to explore the 
meanings and expectations embedded within these expressions of offence. While the corpus 
analysis sheds light on salient lexical patterns, the thematic analysis focuses on interpreting how 
these patterns reflect unfulfilled expectations. These expectations are examined through the lens of 
Spencer-Oatey’s rapport management framework, which provides insight into how perceived threats 
to face, and sociality rights and obligations contribute to the construction and expression of offence 
in the data (Spencer-Oatey, 2008, 2011). 
This chapter is divided into two main sections. Section 4.2 presents the corpus-based analysis of 
four selected hashtags. Each of the subsections (4.2.1 to 4.2.4) focuses on a different hashtag, 
examining its lexical context. Following this, Section 4.3 presents the thematic analysis, which 
discusses two key themes. Finally, the chapter concludes with a summary of the main insights from 
both layers of analysis and their relevance to the next phase of the study.

Table 4.4 displays the results of a word sketch analysis of the hashtag /aːhə nh/ taken from a corpus 
of 6968 words. An interpretive observation of the results found is provided below. It should be 
acknowledged that some of (+2 Gram) units are identical in meaning but are treated as separate 
entities in the table for some reasons that will be explained. 
1. From the table, it appears that there are two prepositions used by the commenters after the 
hashtag which are /laː/ & /fiː/. Their main function in this context is referral. Thus, what comes 
after the preposition, i.e. ( +2 Gram) is crucial to understand what the commenters refer to when 
using offending. 
2. The word “ الكرامة” [dignity] appears most frequently in this corpus, with a total of 18 occurrences, 
its repeated use suggests a heightened sensitivity to insults perceived to undermine or disregard 
[dignity]. 
3. The most common theme revolves around national identity with significant emphasis on insults 
directed at Omanis and broader regional identities such as [ Arabs] and [Gulf countries]. The 
high frequency of mentions related to national identity such as [ Oman], [ nation], [homeland], 
[country], and [national flag] suggests a strong sensitivity and response to perceived offences 
against national identity and pride and a strong protective sentiment towards national dignity. 
4. The frequent mention of terms such as “Omanis” and “Oman” reflects a strong focus on how 
Omanis, both as individuals and as a collective national identity, are represented and treated. 
This points to a heightened sensitivity around perceived insults to national identity, whether 
occurring within Oman or in international contexts. It is also worth noting that references to “the 
Omanis” appear in the corpus through a range of expressions, including “the citizen,” “Omani 
figure,” and “the Omani nation”. 
5. In several instances across the corpus, the insult is strongly tied to perceived violations of 
[dignity], [morality] and [manners]. The data suggests a strong sensitivity toward actions that are 
seen to undermine moral values indicating that such domains are central to how insults are 
evaluated in public discourse. The frequency of these words, although lower compared to those 
related to national identity, still indicates an active discourse around concepts such as dignity, 
85 
 
morality, and manners, which appear to carry evaluative weight in how certain behaviours are 
described. 
4.3 Thematic Analysis of the Corpus 
Building on the findings of the corpus analysis presented in Section 4.2, this section offers a deeper 
examination of how frequently occurring patterns in the data reflect shared but often implicit 
expectations that shape evaluations of offence. This analysis aims to uncover the underlying 
expectations that trigger such reactions by examining specific instances of the recurring patterns in 
how X users express offence. The analysis is grounded in the understanding that such evaluations are 
socially constructed and negotiated through discourse (Culpeper, 2011). Expressions of offence often 
point to violated expectations, even when those expectations are not made explicit, a point 
emphasised by Tayebi (2021), who highlights how underlying assumptions are discursively signalled. 
To systematically identify and understand the nature of the expectations being violated based on the 
most common pattern identified in Section 4.2, this study employs Spencer-Oatey’s (2002, 2008) 
rapport management framework. This framework offers a useful lens for interpreting the types of 
expectations at stake, whether related to face sensitivities, obligations, or sociality rights, which 
provides deeper insight into what triggers evaluations of offence in the data. The thematic analysis, 
therefore, moves beyond surface-level lexical patterns to explore how users construct offence in 
relation to broader sociocultural norms and expectations. 
4.3.1 Unfulfilled expectations of portraying Omanis and Oman’s national identity and 
the regional identity in a positive and dignified way. 
The first prominent theme reflects an unfulfilled expectation regarding how Omanis and their national 
and regional identity are portrayed. A recurring pattern in the corpus indicates that users express 
offence when representations are perceived as inaccurate, disrespectful, or diminishing to the 
collective image of Oman. 
As evidenced by the repeated use of country- and people-related lexical items such as [Oman], 
[nation], [homeland], [country], [national flag], [Omanis], and [citizens], which frequently appear in 
close proximity to evaluative terms like “ إساءة” (offence) and “ إهانة” (insult) (see Sections 4.2.2–4.2.4). 
The frequency and distribution of these terms suggest that users construct the offence not simply as 
individual discontent but as a broader concern for how the country and its collective identity are 
positioned and represented in public discourse. 
86 
 
This pattern reflects what Spencer-Oatey (2002, p. 540) defines as social identity face; it involves 
any group that a member is concerned with and is described as “we have a fundamental desire for 
people to acknowledge and uphold our social identities or roles”. In this context, the data show that 
offence is often constructed in response to representations that are seen as misaligned with core 
cultural values or that undermine how Omanis perceive their national character. The expectation that 
the country and its people should be portrayed in a dignified, accurate, and positive way appears to 
be deeply embedded in the discourse, particularly when such representations are circulated in 
visible, public-facing contexts such as X. The following analysis focuses on selected posts in which 
these expectations are made explicit or strongly implied. These posts provide insight into the kinds 
of portrayals that trigger offence, the values being defended, and how users frame these violations 
as threats to the collective identity of Oman and its people. 
 
 
 
 
Post (1) expresses a sense of anger towards a video clip made by an Omani social media influencer 
who approached men in public telling them that an egg has fallen from them as they walked, implying 
that they had laid an egg. The post implies an expectation that public content involving Omanis 
should uphold a certain standard of respect towards Omanis; however, what was done in the video 
clip was “derogatory to the Omani character”. In other words, it is disparaging towards the traits, 
values or qualities that are typically associated with the core of the community’s self-image. 
As a result of this, the commenter viewed this clip as to have made him feel “a great amount of insult 
to Omanis”. The use of the phrase “a great amount of insult” reflects that the level of insult is not 
minor but rather substantial and significant to the collective image of the community. 
87 
 
 
 
 
Additionally, post (2) was made to comment on an advertisement2 from Nissan claiming it is “an 
insult” to Omanis by depicting an Omani man emerging from a manhole. The use of the term “ إهانة”, 
which is translated to “insult”, implies that the content of the advertisement is perceived as demeaning 
towards the Omani citizens, as it portrays Omani men in a manner that conflicts with the dignified 
expected representation of the Omani citizens. Such a portrayal can be seen as reducing the 
individual’s and, by extension, the nation’s image, projecting a negative image that is not in alignment 
with how Omanis view themselves or wish to be viewed by others. As a result, the commenter insisted 
on getting “an official apology”. The call for an “official apology” further indicates that an insult has 
been taken, and therefore, a corrective action is “necessary”. 
 
Moreover, post (3) expresses a strong reaction to a Kuwaiti TV drama where an Omani was portrayed 
in a servant role. The post starts with a question with multiple exclamation marks “Since when have 
you have been employing Omanis as your servants ?!!!!!!”. It sets a confrontational tone and serves 
as a rhetorical device that aims to critique the practice of casting Omanis in such roles, as there is a 
clear expectation that Omanis should not be portrayed as servants.

The above tweet (5) was written in response to a public apology issued by the CEO of Al Jazeera 
Iron Company, following his controversial statement that “Omanis are incapable of producing a single 
gram without foreigners”. The tweet explicitly stated that “the expatriate” referring to the CEO, had 
“violated the dignity of Oman” and must be held accountable for “insulting the dignity of the nation”. 
The language used in this post signals that the speaker’s remarks were perceived as a serious 
offence on two levels: the country and the nation. The reference to both “Oman” and “the nation” 
suggests that the perceived violation was not only directed at the country as a political entity but also 
constituted a deeper offence against the collective identity, pride, and dignity of the Omani people. 
To explain further, the CEO’s original statement directly questioned the competence of Omanis, which 
was framed in the user’s response as an attack on a core aspect of national identity. By stating that 
the expatriate “violated the dignity of Oman” and “insulted the dignity of the nation,” the tweet 
constructs the denial of competence as an offence against how Omanis are collectively represented 
and understood. In this context, national identity appears to be closely tied to perceptions of capability 
and contribution. While national pride may also be implicated, the data primarily reflects a concern 
with how competence forms part of the collective self-image. The claim that Omanis are unable to 
produce anything without foreign assistance implies a lack of capability, which is constructed here as 
deeply insulting. Moreover, the fact that legal action was taken by the government further underlines 
the severity of the perceived offence. The government’s involvement signals that the matter touched 
on collective dignity, not only offending individuals but challenging broader expectations around how 
Oman and its people should be spoken about.

Furthermore, the above posts show more tweets of Omanis reacting to what they perceived as an 
insult to significant figures in Oman: the Sultan, who is the previous leader of Oman, and Al Khalili, 
who is the grand mufti of Oman. In post (6) the commenter started the post with superlative language 
" العماني  للشعب  إهانة  اكبر  هذي" which translates to “ This is the greatest insult to Omani people” marking the severity 
of the behaviour and implying that any disrespect towards the deceased Sultan of Oman is taken as 
a direct affront to the people themselves. In response to the insult, the commenter called for a divine 
intervention by saying “I ask Allah, the lord of the great throne to burn the heart of the closest person 
to you”. The appeal to divine authority, which is Allah, underscores the seriousness of the 
transgression, and the curse-like prayer exemplifies the depth of the perceived affront; it reflects a 
wish for the offender to experience a similar feeling. Moreover, the commenter invoked another

is evident from the language used that there is a deep feeling of pride and protection people feel 
toward the late Sultan who is considered a key national symbol and part of their national identity.

In contrast to these outsiders, Omanis are described as engaged in meaningful national practices 
such as “loyalty to our government” and “service to our nation.” The repeated use of inclusive

Through this discursive move, the post reaffirms an expectation that Omanis should be portrayed as

This assertation functions not merely as a denial of association with offensive conduct, but as a

actively construct and defend a shared sense of Omani identity.

“Everyone defends their homeland in their own way which they see as appropriate. Honestly,

93 
 
 
While the previous posts (see examples from 1 to 8) focus on national identity, the data also shows 
that Omani users’ expectations around respectful representation are not limited to portrayals of Oman 
or Omanis themselves, but also extend to include neighbouring Gulf countries. The data has shown 
that Omanis consider it an “offence” and/ or and “insult” when someone offends any of the Arab Gulf 
countries. This construction of regional identity is grounded in recurring references to brotherhood 
and solidarity, which emerge across several posts. To illustrate, the commenters in posts (9), (10), 
and (11) draw on these notions to adopt a defensive stance against perceived disrespect toward Gulf 
states. Phrases such as “our brothers,” “our people,” “they are our family,” “part of us,” and “we see 
you all as brothers”, are used to construct a sense of shared belonging, in which Gulf nationals are 
positioned as close enough to warrant the same protective stance that Omanis extend to their own. 
This symbolic proximity shapes how offence is perceived: any insult or derogation directed toward 
Gulf allies is treated as a personal affront to Omanis themselves. As expressed in post (9), ‘whoever 
offends them offends us’, and in post (10), ‘we do not accept this for any Emiratis’. These responses 
reinforce a broader discourse of regional solidarity and brotherhood, in which Gulf nationals, 
particularly Emiratis, are framed not as outsiders but as being ‘one of us’. 
 
While posts (9), (10), and (11) demonstrate how Omanis consider any offence directed at Gulf 
countries as equally offensive to themselves, they also implicitly highlight specific aspects of Omani 
group identity that are expected to be upheld. To explain, in post (9) the commenter highlighted that 
the act that was directed to the Emirates lacks ‘the simplest meanings of morality that we were raised 
on as Omanis’. This reference to shared upbringing invokes collective moral values assumed to be 
widely understood within the Omani community. The statement not only defends a regional ally but 
also evaluates the behaviour through the lens of Omani values, implying that the act violated the 
values that Omanis were raised with. 
 
The post goes further by explicitly articulating what adhering to Omani morality entails: ‘we will not 
mock anyone and let us rise above’. The repeated use of “we” highlights a communal stance and 
underscores the expectation that Omanis act in accordance with a shared moral code. In this case, 
rejecting mockery and exercising restraint are invoked as key expectations that Omanis are socially 
obliged to uphold. By calling on shared moral upbringing and explicitly encouraging others to “rise 
above,” the commenter is not only condemning the offensive act but reminding fellow Omanis of the 
values they are expected to maintain. The post thus serves a dual function: it expresses taking 
offence on behalf of a regional ally, while simultaneously reinforcing internal behavioural norms 
94 
 
rooted in a shared understanding of Omani morality. 
4.3.2 Unfulfilled expectation of respecting Islam and its religious symbols 
The second prominent theme reflects a recurring expectation among Omani X users that Islam, as a 
core part of their collective identity, should be treated with reverence. This expectation becomes 
particularly visible in how users respond to what they perceive as ‘offence to the religion’. In the data, 
offending religion refers to actions, depictions, or statements that are perceived as disrespectful 
toward Islam, particularly references to Allah, the Prophet Muhammad, or broader religious 
expressions. This pattern was evident in the corpus analysis, where such references frequently co- 
occurred with evaluative terms like “إساءة” (offence), “إهانة” (insult), highlighting the sensitivity around 
how religious content is represented in public discourse (see Sections 4.2.1 & 4.2.3). 
This pattern reflects what Spencer-Oatey (2008) defines as a concern for social identity face in this 
example unlike in the previous section 4.3.1, individuals are concerned with their religious group. In 
this context, Islam is not treated merely as a personal belief system but as part of group identity and 
any perceived disrespect towards it is seen as an attack on the group. In addition, these expressions 
of offence also suggest a violation of equity rights, particularly the expectation that a religious group 
will be treated fairly and in the same way as other religious groups. The examples discussed below 
demonstrate how users draw on their religious identity to assert boundaries around what constitutes 
acceptable treatment of religious references and to evaluate perceived violations as collective 
affronts. 
 
 
An illustration of this expectation is evident in posts (12) and (13), both of which respond to a

caricature published in a Danish newspaper depicting the Prophet Muhammad. The comments 
reflect a strong sense of offence, framed as a perceived violation of the sanctity of the Prophet 
Muhammad and expectations that Islamic beliefs be treated with the same respect as those of other 
religious groups. In both posts, the unfulfilled expectation of respecting religious references is 
intertwined with a broader critique of Western media freedoms and perceived inconsistencies in how 
different religious groups are treated. 
In post (12), the rhetorical question ‘Can’t they keep their freedoms away from offending religious 
symbols?’ challenges what is perceived as a misuse of free speech. It frames this transgression as 
crossing boundaries of religious sanctity, thereby threatening social identity face (Spencer-Oatey, 
2008). The comment suggests that core aspects of Islamic identity are being disrespected under the 
guise of media freedom. 
Furthermore, the phrase “let them bear the reactions” expresses that such violations are not only 
offensive but carry anticipated consequences. In post (13), the commenter further intensifies this by 
contrasting the treatment of Muslims and Jews in public discourse: ‘Offending Muslims is freedom of 
expression, but offending Jews… requires condemnation and criminalisation’. This comparison 
reveals a perception of unequal treatment, with Muslim identities being subjected to more frequent 
or accepted disrespect. This reflects a perceived violation of equity rights, particularly in terms of how 
different religious groups are handled in global discourse and how Muslim groups are handled. 
 
 
Post (14) illustrates a particularly intense expression of offence in response to the perceived 
desecration of Islamic sanctity. The commenter condemns the association of Islam’s most sacred 
names ‘Allah’ and ‘the Prophet Muhammad’ (peace be upon him) with extremist organisations like 
ISIS and Al-Qaeda, referred to as ‘suicide bombers’. Beginning with the phrase ‘I swear it is 
forbidden’ ( حرام  وللا), the post signals a religious transgression. The use of the word ‘ forbidden’ draws

“I swear it is forbidden to raise the name of the lord almighty (Allah) and his Prophet
