96 
 
directly from Islamic religious discourse, framing the offence as a breach of sacred norms. The 
offence lies not only in the use of sacred names but also in their symbolic association with acts of 
violence, which is seen as a distortion of Islamic values and the identity of the religious community. 
 
 
 
Example (15) presents a related but distinct form of religious offence, this time in response to a local 
event, a film screening on a public beach in Salalah, which the commenter labels as ‘an insult to the 
religion’. This evaluative judgement in this example signals that offence is taken as a moral and 
religious transgression, one that signals a failure to uphold the standards of reverence expected in a 
Muslim society. To underscore this claim, the commenter draws on the prophetic saying, ‘If you have 
no shame, do as you wish’ which is grounded in the Islamic concept of (ḥayā). While often translated 
as ‘shame’, ḥayā’is more accurately understood as a multi-dimensional religious awareness 
encompassing a person’s internalised sense of accountability before God, moral conduct toward 
others, and personal conscience (Sawai et al., 2020). 
The type of ḥayā’ being invoked here reflects what Sawai et al. (2020) describe as ḥayā before Allah, 
a sense of reverence and accountability toward God that compels individuals to avoid actions that 
contradict divine commandments as well as self-ḥayā, the internalised moral consciousness that 
deters a person from engaging in inappropriate behaviour. In this context, the commenter implies 
that the offence, namely allowing the film screening, could only occur in the absence of ḥayā, 
positioning it as the root cause of the perceived transgression. The post, therefore, does not merely 
condemn the act but frames it as a failure to uphold the values that define proper conduct in a 
religious society. 
4.4 Conclusion 
This chapter aimed to offer an initial understanding of what causes offence in Omani society’s 
discourse by analysing how individuals express and evaluate offence in online contexts. The analysis 
combines corpus linguistics with thematic analysis. The analysis combined corpus linguistics and 
Example (15) 
" فليم 
 العرض على شاطي من شواطئ صاللة لهو إهانة للدين يكفي ما الخفي ولكن إن لم تستحي 
 ففعل ما 
،شئت ربنا ال 
 تؤخذنا
بما فعل السفاء منا" 
“The film being shown in one of Salalah beaches is an insult to the religion. What is 
hidden is already enough, if you have no shame, then do as you wish. O our Allah, do 
not hold us accountable for what the foolish among us have done” 
97 
 
thematic analysis to explore both the linguistic patterns and the underlying expectations that shape 
the discourse. The corpus was built from tweets collected through four key hashtags/keywords, which 
are # مسيء [offensive], # مستاء [upset], # إساءة [offence], and # إهانة [insult]. These hashtags served as key 
linguistic markers into how people in Omani society articulated offence in response to perceived 
violations. Despite the distinct uses of each hashtag, several unifying patterns emerged across the 
data, reflecting collective concerns around how Omanis and Oman are represented, how religion is 
represented. 
A prominent finding across all hashtags was the tendency for users to frame offence as a collective 
experience rather than a personal reaction. This was indicated through the use of plural terms such 
as ‘everyone’, ‘people’, ‘all’ , ‘Omanis’, and ‘citizens. These words were frequently used to 
emphasise that the perceived offence affected the wider community. 
In #  مستاء (upset), offence was most often tied to dissatisfaction with everyday issues such as service 
quality. These expressions commonly co-occurred with the preposition من [from], signalling a pattern 
of attributing emotional discomfort to a specific person or action. The commenters under this hashtag 
justified being upset by indicating unmet expectations regarding how a particular service should have 
been delivered. In contrast, the hashtags # مسيء [offensive], # إساءة [offence], and # إهانة [insult] revealed 
deeper sensitivities around religious and national identity. There was a high frequency of terms related 
to religion such as ‘the Prophet’, ‘religion’, and ‘Muslims’. 
Also, words related to national identity were among the most frequently mentioned in the whole 
dataset.  Words such as ‘homeland’, ‘country’, ‘the people’, and  العلم ‘the national flag’ appeared 
frequently across the posts, suggesting that many users perceived certain actions or representations 
as an affront to Oman and its people. Moreover, alongside religious and national concerns, words 
there were repeated use of terms such as ‘modesty’, ‘manners’, and ‘morality’. Nonetheless, in all of 
the examples that used the word morality and modesty, it was tied to the collective image of Oman. 
Building on these findings, the thematic analysis in the second part of this chapter unpacked the 
underlying expectations that shaped these evaluations of offence. Across the data, users took 
offence when they perceived that either their collective identity as Omanis or their religion was 
disrespected or misrepresented. In cases involving words related to national identity, offence 
stemmed from a perceived threat to their social identity face; Omanis felt offended when they were 
mispresented not only by others, but also when their Omani fellows failed to uphold the shared values 
that reflect how Omanis are. 
98 
 
Similarly, in religious contexts, offence arose when sacred figures such as the Prophet and Allah 
were seen as misused or associated with terrorist groups. This not only signalled a violation of 
religious sanctity, but also a failure to accord Islam the same respect given to other religions thus 
challenging both their group religious identity and equity rights. To conclude, the corpus and thematic 
analyses demonstrated that participants grounded their judgements based on shared sociocultural 
norms and expectations. The findings here set the stage for the next chapter, which shifts focus from 
public expressions on X to personal narratives, offering a more detailed and individual perspective 
on offence. 
 
 
 
99 
 
CHAPTER 5: RESULTS - PHASE 2 (B) 
 
5.1 Introduction 
Building on the analysis of X data presented in Chapter 4, this chapter examines personal accounts 
of offence as narrated by individuals through diary reports to further investigate what causes Omanis 
to take offence. The findings from the previous phase revealed that offence was frequently triggered 
by representations or behaviour perceived to misrepresent Omanis and reflect negatively on the 
group. Such portrayals were often interpreted as public mischaracterisation that prompted strong 
reactions, particularly when they touched on national identity. Religion also emerged as a prominent 
source of offence, especially when religious symbols were perceived to have been violated. These 
patterns revealed the expectations that, when breached, prompted public expression of offence. 
The diary report data analysed in this chapter provides an additional layer to this inquiry by capturing 
participants detailed personal, lived experiences of offence. By focusing on how individuals narrate 
and evaluate specific incidents, this phase contributes to a deeper understanding of the social 
expectations and contextual factors that underpin offence in everyday life. The primary objective of 
the diary report was to explore, in greater depth, the range of situations and perspectives that lead 
Omani individuals to take offence. Accordingly, the design of this phase was directly informed by the 
findings of Phase 1. Patterns observed in the data, particularly the ways in which these incidents 
were framed, guided the wording and sequencing of diary report questions. This helped ensure that 
the prompts aligned with how participants naturally expressed offence. In this way, Phase 1 provided 
the communicative framing for the data collection in this phase. 
The analysis presented in this chapter is guided by Braun and Clarke’s (2006) approach to thematic 
analysis, allowing for the identification of recurring patterns in how offence is experienced and 
explained. In addition, Spencer-Oatey’s (2008) rapport management framework is employed to 
interpret participants’ evaluations of expectation violations, particularly in relation to face sensitivities, 
sociality rights and obligations. This combination provides a structured yet flexible lens through which 
to explore expectations underlying participants’ accounts. 
The chapter is organised as follows. Section 5.2 provides an overview of the thematic patterns 
identified in the data. This is followed by six individual sections (5.3 to 5.8), each dedicated to one of 
the main themes that emerged from the analysis. Each thematic section presents a detailed 
discussion supported by illustrative excerpts from the diary reports, highlighting how participants 
100 
 
interpret and evaluate moments of offence. The chapter concludes with a synthesis of the key 
findings and a reflection on their relationship to the broader patterns observed in the public discourse 
analysed in Chapter 3. 
The following section summarises the themes that emerged throughout the analysis: 
5.2 Themes overview 
The data analysis shows that the Omani participants are generally offended because one or more of 
the following expectations are not met: 
 
Unfulfillment of quality face expectations caused by underestimating one’s abilities 
Unfulfillment of interpersonal expectations caused by betrayal within close relationship 
Unfulfillment of equity expectations caused by lack of consideration 
Unfulfillment of reciprocity expectations caused by misunderstanding of intentions 
Unfulfillment of association right expectations caused by being denied expressing 
oneself 
Unfulfillment of deference expectations caused by crossing boundaries 
 
5.3 Unfulfillment of quality face expectations caused by underestimating one’s 
abilities 
A recurring pattern across the data involves participants taking offence in response to being 
perceived as lacking competence, experience, or credibility. These accounts reflect a deeper affront 
to what Spencer-Oatey (2002, 2008) terms quality face, the desire to be positively evaluated in terms 
of one's personal qualities, including skills, capability, and appearance. Based on the data, when 
this aspect of face is undermined, individuals experience a threat to their self-worth, particularly in 
contexts where competence is central to how one is positioned or recognised by others. The offence 
expressed in these narratives stems from a perceived mismatch between how participants viewed 
themselves and how others evaluated them. In many cases, this face threat was compounded by a 
perceived lack of fairness, such as not being given adequate support, recognition, or opportunity to 
demonstrate one’s abilities. This relates to what Spencer-Oatey (2002, 2008) refers to as equity 
rights, which involve the expectation to be treated fairly, without undue imposition. While equity rights 
were not the primary source of offence, they were often entangled with quality face concerns, 
reinforcing the sense of being undervalued or unfairly judged. 
 
101 
 
The following examples illustrate situations in which participants perceived their quality face 
expectations to have been undermined or disregarded. 
 
 
In the narrative above, the narrator expressed offence and frustration at being underestimated in the 
workplace. She reported experiencing “the underestimation of my ability at work,” which she 
attributed to others pointing out that she “did not obtain a university degree” and “had no work 
experience”. These remarks dismissed her personal qualities and reduced her value to formal 
credentials, reflecting a clear violation of quality face. The narrator did not question her own 
capability, but felt that others failed to see her potential, creating a mismatch between her self- 
perception and how she was evaluated by others. She also noted that “there was no appreciation at 
work” which added to her sense of being undervalued. This lack of recognition suggested an unmet 
expectation that her efforts and abilities should be acknowledged, regardless of her qualifications. 
While the primary source of offence was the threat to her quality face, the narrative also hinted at a 
broader concern with fairness, as the narrator implicitly expected to be acknowledged for her efforts 
and potential, rather than judged solely on formal qualifications. The combined effect of negative 
evaluation and the absence of appreciation resulted in feelings of discouragement and offence.

“It happened during my studies that the person supervising me criticised my work in an 
insulting way and made me feel that I was not responsible and that I did not have enough 
skills to complete the task assigned to me. I felt offended and humiliated even though I 
was confident in myself and that I could complete the task. I just needed enough time. 
This feeling led me not to accept working with him and therefore I was unable to adapt 
to environment in which I was studying”. 
102 
 
Narrative 2 illustrates how offence arises when an individual’s expectation of being recognized as 
competent and capable is dismissed by supervisors’ negative evaluation. The narrator reported that 
her supervisor criticised her work ‘in a humiliating way’, which made her feel ‘not responsible’ and 
that she lacked ‘the necessary skills’ to complete the task. However, she stated that she was 
‘confident in [herself]’ and believed she could complete the task successfully. The offence did not 
stem simply from receiving negative feedback, but from the perception that it contradicted how she 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
saw herself and how she wished to be perceived by others. This disconnect between her self- 
perception and the way she was positioned by the supervisor reflects a clear violation of quality face, 
as her competence was not only questioned but disregarded. 
 
The narrative also reflects an implicit expectation of fair treatment. Her comment that she “just 
needed enough time” suggests that she believed the judgement was premature and that she was 
not afforded a fair chance to demonstrate her capability. While quality face was the primary concern, 
this perceived lack of opportunity added another layer to the offence by reinforcing the sense that 
her abilities were dismissed without justification. The narrator’s account shows how the offence was 
shaped not only by the way her abilities were dismissed, but also by the sense that she was not given 
the opportunity to prove herself. This combination of negative evaluation and limited support reflects 
how threats to quality face may be reinforced by unmet expectations of fairness in how one is 
assessed. 
5.4 Unfulfillment of interpersonal expectations within close relationships 
This theme centres around offences that arise when expectations embedded within close personal 
relationships are violated. These expectations are shaped by what Chen, Boucher, and Tapias (2006) 
describe as the relational self, the self in relation to “significant others”, defined as “ individuals whom 
one knows, (v.s just met), with whom one feels some degree of closeness, and usually with whom 
one shares a relationship that can be normatively (e.g., friend) or idiosyncratically labelled (e.g., my 
closest high school friend)” (p. 153). From a relational perspective (Spencer-Oatey, 2011), offence in 
these contexts arises not simply from the act itself, but from the fact that it violates relationship-

“I was described to be a crow by my cousin. I felt so sad because this insult came from 
someone close”. 
103 
 
specific expectations shaped by emotional closeness and shared history. In this study, these 
expectations are referred to as interpersonal expectations norms of behaviour that are context- 
dependent and often implicit, varying according to the quality, role, and type of the relationship. The 
examples in this category involve offences committed by individuals with whom the participant shared 
a close personal relationship, such as a family member or friend. In each case, the offence was 
shaped not only by the action itself but by the fact that it came from someone with whom the 
participant had an established relational history. That history gave rise to implicit expectations 
grounded in trust, emotional closeness, or loyalty that the offender was expected to uphold. Several 
participants indicated that their sense of offence was heightened precisely because the behaviour 
came from someone close, rather than a stranger or casual acquaintance. The following examples 
illustrate how the unfulfillment of interpersonal expectations within close relationships led to 
particularly meaningful and emotionally charged experiences of offence. 
 
Extract 3 presents an incident in which the narrator felt offended when his cousin, described as 
someone close, called him “a crow.” What appears to have caused the offence was not the insult 
itself, but the fact that it came from a close relative. The narrator explicitly states that he felt sad 
“because this offence came from someone close,” indicating that if the same comment had come 
from someone else, it may not have provoked the same emotional response. This suggests that the 
evaluation of offence does not depend solely on the content of what was said, but significantly on 
who said it. 
The narrator’s use of the word “close” points to a long-standing relationship, likely marked by emotional 
familiarity and shared history. His sadness reflects a sense of violated interpersonal expectations, 
namely, that someone within his inner circle would avoid using insulting language. In this context, the 
offence arose from the violation of expectations commonly associated with close relationships, where 
individuals anticipate care, support, and restraint in how they are addressed. The narrative suggests 
that the closer the relationship, the higher the expectation for positive and affirming interactions, 
which makes derogatory remarks feel particularly hurtful and disappointing. 
104 
 
 
Narrative (4) clearly shows how offence can arise when expectations in close relationships are not 
met. The narrator begins with, ‘you turn your back to someone you trust and consider a sister’ which 
highlights the emotional closeness and trust that defined the relationship. This closeness creates 
certain expectations about how one should be treated, expectations that are often unspoken but 
assumed. As Spencer-Oatey (2011) notes, close relationships carry relationship-specific behavioural 
expectations. In this case, the narrator likely expected emotional loyalty and protection from hurtful 
behaviour. 
The narrator saw that her friend, who is like her sister, was ‘speaking ill’ of her; this act itself was 
perceived as a direct violation of the expectations associated with being ‘like a sister’ . It is important 
to mention here that the narrator does not present the offence as rooted in the specific words spoken, 
or even the action itself but in who committed the act. The offence that emerges here stems from the 
rupture of a bond framed as sister-like. In this way, the narrative reinforces that interpersonal 
expectations in close relationship carry a deeper weight, and when violated, trigger more intense 
emotional consequences. 
The reported emotional impact, ‘my mental state was affected for four months… I felt distress, 
disappointment, and a loss of trust in others’ which further highlights the depth of the offence. What 
makes this account particularly powerful is the contrast between the assumed role of the offender “a 
sister’ and her actual behaviour, creating a gap between expectation and reality that lies at the heart 
of the offence. 
 
5.5 Unfulfillment of equity expectations caused by lack of consideration 
The data reveals that in certain contexts, participants expected to receive a level of personal 
consideration from others, grounded in notions of fairness. This expectation relates to what Spencer- 
Oatey defines as equity rights and is defined as “ We have a fundamental belief that we are entitled

suddenly you turn around and see that she is making gestures behind your back,

upon, that we are not unfairly ordered about and that we are not taken advantage of or exploited" 
(2008, p.16). Such expectations may not always be explicitly stated, but they reflect a normative 
understanding of how people believe they ought to be treated within a given social context. 
 
In the examples that follow, participants describe moments in which these equity-based expectations 
were not met. The offence in these cases stems from a perceived lack of fair and considerate 
treatment, such as being expected to sacrifice personal needs without acknowledgement, having 
one’s time or efforts disregarded, or being repeatedly denied what they believed was rightfully theirs. 
 
 
This example illustrates how offence can emerge when equity expectations are violated specifically, 
the expectation that others will take one’s circumstances into account when making requests. The 
narrator describes feeling upset and frustrated after being expected to contribute financially toward 
purchasing a car for a close relative, despite being in a difficult financial situation himself. What 
makes the incident offensive is not merely the financial demand but the fact that “everyone was 
aware” of his ongoing house construction and financial strain. His emotional response reflects a 
perceived failure of others to act with fairness and consideration of core elements of equity rights as

“ I committed to paying an amount exceeding 500 Omani Rials to a first degree relative to 
contribute to buying a car for one of my close first-degree relatives. This commitment came 
at a time when I did not have this amount in my bank account, I was in dire need of help, 
in the first place, due to my commitment to building a house for myself. Everyone was 
aware that I was building a house without a bank loan. When the amount became 
available, I had to proceed with the car purchase contribution, and postpone a crucial 
matter related to completing the construction, delaying many other constructions related 
matters. I was frustrated and upset because I realized that people prioritise their own 
interests, and there is no one who believes in the priorities of others or considers 
their circumstance, even if they are aware of them”. 
106 
 
defined by Spencer-Oatey (2008). The narrator presents the situation as a conflict between his own 
pressing needs and the expectations placed upon him by others. While he emphasises being ‘in dire 
need of help’ and under pressure to complete his home construction, he also recounts how he ended 
up prioritising the contribution for the car, delaying important building-related matters in the process. 
His language, including phrases like “postpone crucial matters” and “everyone knew,” underscores 
the offence taken: not at the financial act itself, but at being expected to give at personal cost without 
acknowledgment of his situation. The comment that ‘people prioritise their own interests’ and ‘do not 
consider the circumstances of others, even if they are aware of them’ reveals a breakdown of 
interpersonal fairness. The offence, in this case, stems from a lack of consideration for his needs, 
and a perceived disregard for his situation, ultimately reflecting an unmet expectation of fair and 
considerate treatment from those who were aware of his circumstances. 
 
Narrative 6 presents a case in which the narrator, a taxi driver, takes offence due to a perceived 
violation of fairness in a work-related interaction. He recounts waiting a long time for a customer, 
emphasised through the repetition of ‘waiting and waiting and waiting’, only for another driver to 
intervene and take the customer instead. This incident triggered feelings of ‘anger and resentment’, 
reflecting a strong emotional response tied to a perceived injustice. 
The narrator’s reaction points to an unspoken but socially recognised norm of turn-taking among taxi 
drivers, an informal practice that supports fairness in accessing customers. Although the narrator 
does not explicitly use the word ‘unfair’, his frustration reflects a clear violation of equity expectations.

“One day I was on my taxi, waiting and waiting, and waiting until a customer arrived, and I 
was ahead, but another taxi driver came and took the customer from me, making me 
wait again, unfortunately. That person is the one who keeps taking customers from me, 
and I felt very sad, and I couldn’t say anything to him due to my values, I sat waiting, 
and I was patient, another better customer arrived, better than the previous one, and I 
charged him with a larger amount. This is the reward of patience, relief. The situation made 
me feel angry and resentment towards that person, but faith in God and patience made me 
stronger”. 
107 
 
To explain, the narrator followed the expected process and invested his time, yet another driver 
disregarded those norms, and took his customer. The statement that ‘this person is the one who 
keeps taking customers from me” indicates that this was not an isolated case, but part of a repeated 
pattern, which further reinforced the sense of being treated unfairly. 
Despite his anger, the narrator states that he “couldn’t respond because of [his] values,” introducing 
a moral dimension to the narrative. While he does not specify what values he is referring to, his 
silence in the face of perceived unfairness reflects a tension between personal values and repeated 
experiences of unfairness. 
 
 
 
This narrative presents a situation in which the narrator, accompanied by her daughter, experiences 
offence due to the unfulfillment of equity expectations at a stationery shop. The scenario involves 
three key actors: the customers waiting in line, including the narrator and her child; the individual 
who bypasses the queue; and the cashier who fails to intervene. 
The first offence arises when a person skips the queue “without respecting those already standing 
in line,” directly violating the shared social norm of turn-taking. The narrator explicitly attributes her 
feelings of frustration and dissatisfaction to this disruption, stating that it was the “lack of respect for 
the queue” that caused her offence.

“ I was with my daughter in one of the primary schools to buy school supplies while 
waiting in the line. The cashier was busy talking to the printing staff at the office and did 
not pay attention to the customers standing in the line. Suddenly, a person came to the 
machine and took his items and advanced to the front of the queue without respecting 
those already standing in line. Additionally, the cashier did not show any reaction to 
his behaviour causing annoyance among the customers and the children. I felt 
frustrated and dissatisfied due to the lack of respect for the queue and because the 
staff did not take any action”. 
108 
 
While the term “respect” is used, it should be understood not as a face concern but as a matter of 
equity. In this context, “respecting the queue” refers to adhering to the principle of fairness in public 
interactions: following the order of arrival and waiting one’s turn. The act of bypassing the queue 
disregards this unspoken agreement, creating a situation in which the time and presence of those 
waiting, particularly those who had complied with the norm, are devalued. 
A second layer of offence emerges from the cashier’s lack of response. His failure to address or 
correct the queue-jumping behaviour is perceived as a further violation of equity expectations. Not 
only does it signal indifference to the breach, but it also suggests a lack of professional impartiality 
and fairness expected of service providers. The narrator notes that this inaction led to visible 
annoyance among other customers and children, reinforcing the sense that the principles of fair 
treatment were not upheld. Together, these two actions, the breach of turn-taking and the absence 
of a corrective response, reflect a breakdown in the normative expectations of equitable and 
considerate treatment in everyday interactions. 
5.6 Unfulfillment of reciprocity expectation 
This theme explores how offence arises when participants’ acts of goodwill are not reciprocated. 
Across the data, participants describe offering a favour or act of assistance, often using the culturally 
salient terms maʕrūf (a favour) or xidma (a service or help provided to someone), through which their 
gestures were ultimately not reciprocated in any meaningful way. These accounts point to a recurring 
pattern in which helping others appears to carry a tacit expectation of reciprocation in the form of 
recognition. Based on the data, this expectation operates across different kinds of relationships, 
however loosely defined, whether it involves a close connection (such as a friend or relative) or a 
brief encounter with a stranger in public. In all cases, the absence of an expected response, ‘ 
reciprocation’ becomes a source of offence. 
 
The principle of reciprocity is about maintaining a balance of payment in social interaction (Culpeper 
& Tantucci, 2021); in other words, “we owe others certain things because of what they have 
previously done for us, because of the history of previous interaction we have had with them” 
(Gouldner,1960, p.171). Therefore, “if someone fails to reciprocate politeness work, it is likely that 
their actions will be perceived as breaking some implicit social norm” (Culpeper, 2011, p.38). 
 
In this context, the present data show that reciprocation can take the form of appreciation, 
acknowledgment, or any gesture that signals the act was recognised and valued. When these forms 
of reciprocation are absent or worse, when the favour is met with suspicion or hostility, the expected 
109 
 
social balance is disrupted resulting in a sense of offence grounded in the violation of normative 
expectations around reciprocity. 
 
 
Narrative 8 reveals a fundamental mismatch between the speaker’s intended act of “ favour” and the 
interpretation it received, a misalignment that ultimately gave rise to offence. The narrator describes 
attempting to assist a woman whose abaya was dangerously caught outside her car door. Although 
motivated by concern for her safety, his repeated efforts to alert her were misinterpreted as flirtation 
or inappropriate attention; he reported that the woman looked at him “as if [he] were her enemy” or as 
though he were “flirting with her,” and responded with “very, very bad words” and even reporting him 
to the police. 
From the narrator’s perspective, this was a clear maʕrūf. He explicitly labels his intervention a “favour” 
and expresses disbelief and frustration that this favour “ended with [him] receiving insults.” The fact 
that he frames his help in this way signals an implicit expectation that such an act will be socially 
recognised if not appreciated. What makes the event offensive is not simply the absence of 
recognition, but the fact that the narrator’s act of help was entirely misinterpreted. His reference to the 
proverb ‘you do good, you receive harm’ underscores a broader sense of disillusionment, where a 
well-intentioned act is not only unreciprocated but socially penalised. favour’ is misinterpreted. In this

“one day as I was driving my car, I was surprised to see a car next to me driven by a 
woman and it appeared to me that part of her abaya was outside the car, and it was 
close to the car tire. I tried to stop the woman, but to no avail, … and she was looking at 
me as if I were her enemy or in other words, as if was flirting with her. I was worried 
that something will happen to this woman and her car. After I got out of my car, I could 
not inform her of the situation as she immediately started saying very very bad words to 
the extent that she informed the police about me, and after the favour I done to her 
which ended up with me receiving swear words, I told her that part of her abaya was 
outside the car, which could have led her to being pulled outside through the tire and being 
suffocated. I felt a lot of resentment and wished that I did not stop that woman and receive

110 
 
case, the narrator’s act of maʕrūf was not simply unreciprocated; it was reframed as inappropriate 
behaviour, contradicting the meaning the narrator had attached to it. 
 
This idea of offence arising from a misalignment between intended favour and received interpretation 
is further echoed in Narrative 9. Here, the narrator describes a moment when he attempted to help his 
grandmother wash her hair, an act he explicitly identifies as a maʕrūf (favour). However, instead of 
receiving his gesture as one of care and assistance, the grandmother misinterpreted it as an attempt 
to cause harm. The narrator’s emotional response, feeling “shame and embarrassment”, highlights 
how the offence was not triggered by the dissonance between his intention and how it was perceived. 
As with the previous example, the core issue is not the absence of reciprocation, but from the failure 
of the act to be received within its intended frame. While it may seem natural to feel offended when 
one’s intentions are misunderstood, the examples in this section show that the offence is not simply a 
reaction to misunderstanding itself. Rather, it emerges from a deeper sense of frustration when a 
favour especially one framed as a maʕrūf is not only misread but stripped of its intended meaning. 
Participants did not take offence because the other person was deliberately hostile, but because their 
goodwill was interpreted in ways that contradicted their purpose. Even when the misunderstanding 
was acknowledged, as in Narrative 9, the emotional impact remained.  
This highlights that the offence stems not just from a lack of reciprocation or not reciprocation, but from 
the failure of others to recognise the act as a favour and understand it as it was intended. In this way, 
reciprocity is shown to depend not only on returning a favour, but on correctly interpreting and 
acknowledging the intent behind it. In this case, the close familial relationship adds further weight to 
the expectation of recognition, making the misinterpretation more emotionally charged. This suggests 
that reciprocity depends not only on returning a favour, but also on correctly interpreting and 
acknowledging the intent behind it particularly in relationships where mutual understanding might be 
assumed.

Unlike the previous examples (8 &9) where offence arose from misunderstood intentions, Narrative 
10 highlights a different breach of reciprocity, one in which a past favour was disregarded and 
followed by a publicly offensive response. The narrator recounts being bullied on X (formerly Twitter), 
receiving “a wave of sarcastic and insulting comments” that left him feeling “very sad and angry.” 
However, what makes the incident particularly offensive is not the online aggression itself, but the fact 
that one of the individuals involved was someone he had “helped before.” The narrator uses the term 
xidma a culturally salient term equivalent to maʕrūf (favour) and repeats the verb “helped,” 
underscoring his expectation that past assistance should have at least secured respectful treatment, 
if not solidarity. 
This expectation is explicitly voiced in the phrase “especially because I helped that person,” 
indicating that the breach lies not just in being insulted, but in being insulted by someone with whom 
the narrator had a prior relationship shaped by a previous act of help, thereby creating an implicit 
expectation of respectful or considerate treatment in return. 
5.7 Unfulfillment of association rights expectation caused by being denied 
expressing oneself 
Some examples in the data show that individuals took offence when they were denied the opportunity 
to connect emotionally or socially with others in ways they felt were appropriate to the relationship. 
These experiences relate to association rights, which, within the Rapport Management Framework 
(Spencer-Oatey, 2008), refer to people’s expectations for inclusion, emotional involvement, and 
participation in social interaction. Unlike equity rights, which concern fairness and balance in the 
distribution of benefits and obligations, association rights are concerned with whether individuals are 
granted or denied the space to engage, express, and connect. These expectations vary depending 
on relational closeness, cultural norms, and personal boundaries, but when individuals feel socially

excluded or emotionally shut down, offence may arise. The examples that follow demonstrate how 
participants experienced offence when their attempts to express themselves were restricted or 
dismissed.  
 
 
In the narrative above, the speaker describes a situation in which she felt offended due to being 
“marginalised” within the school community. She recounts how her freedom to express her opinions 
“openly and transparently” was constrained, as the institutional focus was primarily on listening to 
teachers and completing the curriculum. This experience reflects a perceived denial of what 
Spencer-Oatey (2008) terms association rights the expectation of being allowed to engage in 
meaningful interaction and to express oneself within a social setting. The narrator’s sense of 
marginalisation indicates a breach of her anticipated entitlement to be heard and included, both 
emotionally and conversationally. The offence arises from being excluded from the communicative 
and relational dynamics of the environment. The clash between her expectation of participatory 
engagement and the limited opportunity for expression highlights a violation of association rights. 
 
Narrative (12) presents a situation in which the narrator’s association rights were not fulfilled due to 
being denied the freedom to express her emotions without fear of reprimand. The narrative begins

“At home, since I was a child, I was not allowed to be upset, or frown. If I did, my mother 
would reprimand me, make me feel guilty as If I had done something wrong. These 
situations made me feel lonely and isolated from the world. When I try to engage with 
people, I feel fear, tension, and anxiety, along with a sense of guilt. I feel that no matter how 
much I do, it is impossible to meet or satisfy people’s expectations”. 
113 
 
with a reflection on the home environment, where the speaker states that she was “not allowed” to 
show emotional discomfort, such as being upset or frowning. This phrase signals a perceived 
restriction on emotional expression within a close relational setting. The consequences of attempting 
to express such emotions were significant: her mother would reprimand her and make her feel guilty, 
‘as if [she] had done something wrong’. The offence arises from the implicit belief that she is entitled 
to an emotional openness within her family, but does not get it. 
As a result, the speaker reports feeling “lonely and isolated from the world,” suggesting that her 
inability to express herself at home contributed to a broader sense of emotional disconnection. This 
disconnection extended beyond the family setting and shaped her experiences in wider social 
contexts. She describes feeling “fear, anxiety, and guilt” when engaging with others, and expresses 
a belief that “it is impossible to meet or satisfy people’s expectations.” These reflections illustrate how 
the early denial of her association rights within her family undermined her ability to feel socially 
connected and emotionally secure in broader interactions. 
5.8 Unfulfillment of deference expectation based on role 
The data from in the diary report revealed that offence arise when individuals feel that the level of 
deference they are entitled to based on their social or institutional role, is not granted. In this context, 
deference refers to the recognition individuals expect in accordance with their perceived position 
within a social hierarchy. As Shils (1982, pp. 145–146) explains, deference is linked to “macrosocial” 
properties such as occupational role, accomplishment, income, lifestyle, and level of education. 
These attributes influence how people are positioned in society and shape expectations for how they 
should be treated. When such deference is expressed, it communicates a recognition of the 
individual's higher status or authority. Conversely, when it is withheld such as failure to observe role- 
appropriate boundaries, it implies that the person’s position is not acknowledged or valued. in certain 
contexts, people express either implicitly or explicitly that they are entitled to receive a certain level 
of deference based on their social or institutional role. This perceived denial of recognition can lead 
to feelings of offence, particularly when individuals, whether implicitly or explicitly, believe that their 
role entitles them to a certain kind of treatment. 
Moreover, deference expectations are shaped by broader sociocultural norms, which influence the 
perceived importance of social attributes such as occupational role, age, or institutional status. These 
norms govern the extent to which such attributes are socially valued and regarded as deserving of 
114 
 
differential treatment, thereby shaping individuals’ expectations for how they should be 
acknowledged or positioned within interactions. In this context, participants frequently used the 
Arabic term احترام (/ʔihtiraːm/), commonly translated as respect. However, in these narratives,  احترام 
refers more specifically to role-based deference, the culturally grounded expectation that one’s social 
or professional status will be acknowledged and upheld through appropriate behaviour. While the 
extent of these expectations varied among individuals, the denial of (/ʔihtiraːm/), in this sense, 
consistently emerged as a trigger for taking offence. The following examples illustrate how such 
perceived violations of role-based expectations were evaluated as offensive. 
 
Extract (13) presents a layered account of offence in which the narrator responds to what she 
perceives as a violation of role-based deference expectations. The incident involves a student 
making a negative comment about the narrator’s contact lenses in front of his classmates. While the 
content of the remark is framed as negative, the narrator’s emphasis on the public setting “in front of 
the students” suggests that the central issue is not simply the comment itself, but the failure to meet 
expectations regarding how she should be treated in front of others. Her statement that the student 
“did not respect me in front of the students” indicates that she anticipated a certain level of restraint 
or recognition in line with her role, most likely a position of authority, which was not upheld. 
This expectation of deference appears to be rooted in the hierarchical nature of the relationship 
between the narrator and the student. Although her role is not explicitly stated, her evaluation of the 
interaction implies that she viewed the student’s behaviour as inappropriate within the boundaries of 
their respective roles. The public nature of the act intensifies the offence, as it not only violates her 
expectation of being treated with deference but also does so in a way that exposes her to possible 
embarrassment or loss of face in front of others. 
 
(13)

“One of the students made a negative comment about my contact lenses in front of his 
classmates, and he was spoken to directly, and he came to apologise, and I did not accept 
his apology because he did not respect me in front of the students and also because he 
did not show remorse for what he has done but rather insisting that he did nothing”. 
115 
 
The final layer of offence emerges from the student’s apology, which, rather than repairing the 
situation, appears to have exacerbated the narrator’s sense of disrespect. Although the student 
offered an apology, the narrator rejected it, explaining that it lacked remorse and was accompanied 
by a denial of wrongdoing “he insisted that he did nothing wrong.” This failure to acknowledge 
disrespect falls short of the narrator’s likely expectations for how the situation should have been 
addressed. As noted by Shafa et al. (2017), perceptions of sincerity in an apology are closely tied to 
the degree of blame acknowledged and remorse expressed. In this case, the absence of both made 
the apology ineffective and further intensified the narrator’s offence. The apology was not perceived 
as a recognition of the speaker’s role or a restoration of the disrupted social boundary, but rather as 
a refusal to accept responsibility, reinforcing the initial violation of expected deference. In other 
words, it reflects not just a failure to restore deference, but a refusal to accept that any deference 
was due in the first place. 
 
Extract (14) provides another example in which the informant expresses offence in response to a 
student’s behaviour that she perceived as both disrespectful and inappropriate within the 
professional context. According to the narrative, the student accused the speaker of failing to fulfil 
her work responsibilities and added that she did not deserve the salary she received. The narrator 
reports feeling “annoyed and provoked,” and frames the incident as an act of “disrespect” and a case 
of the student having “crossed his boundaries”. 
The narrator’s emphasis on having been “disrespected” and on the student having “crossed 
boundaries” indicates an implicit expectation of role-based deference. In institutional settings, 
particularly academic or professional ones, there are often unspoken norms that govern interaction 
based on status and role. The student’s behaviour is perceived to have breached these norms, not 
only by questioning the narrator’s professional integrity, but by doing so in a way that disregards the 
expected asymmetry in their social positions. The notion of “boundaries” here refers to socially 
understood limits on what kind of evaluative behaviour is acceptable from someone in the student’s

5.9 Conclusion 
This chapter examined the lived experiences of participants who reported feeling offended in various 
contexts. Through thematic analysis of the diary report data, seven distinct themes were identified, 
each pointing to a specific type of expectation whose unfulfillment triggered feelings of offence. 
A recurring source of offence across the data was the unfulfillment of quality face (see section 5.3, 
examples 1 &2 ), which is the desire to be recognised for one’s abilities and competence (Spencer- 
Oatey, 2002, 2008). Participants often recounted being dismissed or underestimated, particularly in 
professional settings, where their skills or potential were overlooked. These moments not only 
threatened their self-worth but were frequently accompanied by a sense of unfairness, pointing to an 
overlap with equity rights, the expectation of being treated justly (Spencer-Oatey, 2008). 
Another common theme involved violations of interpersonal expectations within close relationships 
(see Section 5.4, examples 3,4). In these cases, offence was not triggered by what was said or done, 
but by who said it. To explain, participants emphasised that they were offended because the 
behaviour came from someone described as ‘close’ and someone the participant considered ‘like a 
sister’. The relationships in these examples were marked by closeness, which came with implicit 
expectations of how the other person should behave. When these expectations were not met, and 
the behaviour did not align with what is normally expected in close relationships, participants reported 
feelings of sadness, disappointment, and, in some cases, a loss of trust (Chen, Boucher & Tapias, 
2006; Spencer-Oatey, 2011). 
Participants also described taking offence when their equity expectations were disregarded (see 
Section 5.5, examples 5,6 & 7). They expressed frustration in situations where they felt people were 
not considerate and were unfair with them by asking them to contribute financially during hardship, 
ignoring turn-taking among taxi drivers, or skipping the queue. In all of these situations, offence was 
perceived due to the violation of implicit expectations that others would act with fairness and 
consideration (Spencer-Oatey, 2000). 
Further, several participants narrated experiences where they had ‘extended help’ or ‘favours’, often 
framed using culturally significant terms like maʕrūf and xidma, but were met with suspicion, or even 
hostility (see section 5.6, examples 8,9 & 10). In these instances, participants reported feeling 
117 
 
offended when their intention behind the favour was misunderstood, and on some occasions, the 
favour was not acknowledged or reciprocated in any meaningful way. These accounts point to the 
social expectations surrounding acts of help, where providing assistance carries an implicit 
expectation of some form of recognition. While traditional understandings of reciprocity often centre 
on a balance of exchange (Culpeper & Tantucci, 2021), the data here suggest that offence also arose 
when the intent behind the favour was misinterpreted. 
Moreover, other participants described feeling offended when they were denied the opportunity to 
participate, engage, or express themselves in contexts where such engagement was expected (see 
section 5.7examples 11 & 12). The examples narrated point to the violation of association rights, 
which is the entitlement to inclusion and emotional involvement in interaction (Spencer-Oatey, 2008). 
For instance, one participant reported feeling marginalised in a school environment that prioritised 
finishing the curriculum over letting students express their opinions, while another described being 
frowned upon at home for being upset. 
The final theme in this chapter involved offence arising from the failure to receive the level of 
deference participants expected based on their institutional roles (see Section 5.8, examples 13 and 
14 for more details). In this context, the term iḥtirām was used by participants to express expectations 
of role-based deference, rather than respect. Offence was perceived when others failed to show 
appropriate recognition in line with the participant’s role; for example, when students made 
inappropriate remarks in front of others or accused them of not doing their job. These incidents were 
seen as violations of expected treatment tied to social and institutional roles (Shils, 1982; Spencer- 
Oatey, 2008). 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
118 
 
CHAPTER 6: RESULTS - PHASE 1 (A) 
 
6.1 Introduction 
This chapter presents the findings of Phase 1 of the study, which investigates how incivility is 
perceived, framed, and evaluated in online discourse within the Omani context. The focus is on 
naturally occurring posts from X, where users comment on or report behaviour they consider 
inappropriate in shared spaces. The data were collected using two hashtags in the Arabic language 
(see Section 6.2, Table 6.1), chosen to capture a range of expressions that reflect users’ responses 
to perceived incivility. 
The analysis is structured in two interconnected stages. The first stage involves a corpus-driven 
analysis, with a focus on frequency and concordance analysis of phrases surrounding the selected 
hashtags. This stage aims to identify common lexical items and recurring patterns that appear in 
users’ descriptions of uncivil behaviours. By examining how often particular terms occur and how 
they are used in context, this layer of analysis offers insights into the linguistic framing of incivility 
and the kinds of behaviour that attract public critique or disapproval. 
The second part of the analysis builds on the patterns observed in the corpus analysis through a 
thematic analysis, which explores the underlying norms that inform users’ evaluations of incivility. 
These norms emerge through users’ reactions to perceived breaches of appropriate conduct. The 
thematic analysis draws on Spencer-Oatey’s (2008, 2011) rapport management framework to 
interpret how judgements of incivility are shaped by perceived violations of face, sociality rights and 
obligations within the Omani context. 
This chapter is divided into two main sections. Section 6.2 presents the corpus-based analysis of 
four selected hashtags. Each subsection (6.2.1 to 6.2.2) focuses on a different hashtag, examining 
its lexical context. Following this, section 6.3 presents the thematic analysis, and outlines the main 
themes that emerged from the data, demonstrating how incivility is constructed in relation to social 
norms. Finally, the chapter concludes with a summary of the main insights from both layers of 
analysis and their relevance to the next phase of the study. 
 
 
 
119 
 
6.2 Corpus driven analysis 
 
Given that two hashtags were selected for this study (see Table 6.1), two separate corpora were 
constructed accordingly. To explore how incivility is expressed in connection with each hashtag, 
attention was directed toward the immediate textual context in which the hashtag appeared. 
Specifically, the analysis focused on identifying and extracting the words or phrases that occurred 
directly before the hashtag across the dataset, as these often carry important evaluative or 
descriptive content. 
The goal was to determine how users linguistically frame or introduce acts they consider uncivil, and 
to examine whether particular lexical patterns consistently emerge across different posts. This 
process was guided by the following questions: 
a. What comes before the hashtag?

(referred to as frequency 1) 
c. What is the concordance of each -1gram + the hashtag? 
(referred to as concordance) 
d. What is the frequency of the concordance? 
(referred to as frequency 2)

“expatriate workers” in touristic sites specifically “ Nizwa Market”. 
11. Furthermore, there are a few tweets that described the “ lack of organisation” as uncivil “ view” 
and “scene”. For example, the lack of organization in front of touristic site “Sultan Qaboos 
port” and the struggle to arrange a taxi. In another scene, the commenter described Mutrah 
Fish Market parking to be in “ a chaotic mess” due to lack of organised parking. In both 
examples, the commentor considered this uncivil not just because of the act of “lack of 
organization” but because the locations of both of these places are considered to be touristic 
and the center of Muscat. 
12. Moreover, the corpus also highlights several acts that are considered uncivil such as “ spitting 
124 
 
on the road”, and “writing on the wall” as well as the presence of “ stray animals” close to the 
residential area and presence of “non-stray dogs” along the coast. 
13. Other behaviours that were reported on X as “uncivil scenes” are driving in reverse during 
congestion and “filming and crowding” and “taking pictures at accident scenes”.

in the table. The words are " عمل" ," محتوى" ," بشكل " ," تصرف" ,"  منظر "and which are translated to [view], 
[behaviour], [in a way], and [action]. There are a few other words mentioned that were used once 
like but were included in the table due to some similarities in the concordance line like " مقاطع" and 
" وزخارف  الوان" which translate to [video clips] and [ colours & decoration]. 
2. The most frequent word used before the hashtag /ɣeːr laːʔiq/ is /ˈmanðar/, [ view] which is similar

commenters usually label “views” to be uncivil and inappropriate. It is followed by /tˁaˈsˤar.ruf/ and 
biˈʃikl/ or /bɑ.tˤɑˈriː.qɑ/ which both occurred 3 times. 
3. Throughout all the categories, the most recurrent inappropriate act is “throwing garbage”; it 
occurred 6 times. 
4. The most mentioned setting in which this view and behaviour were witnessed is “public parks” and 
“tourist places”. 
5. In addition to “throwing garbage”, there are few more environmental related issues that were 
reported in the tweets that are viewed to be “inappropriate” such as “unpleasant odours, “ smell 
of barn” close to residential areas and printing ATM receipts despite not needing it. 
6. Moreover, the table highlights that there is concern about wearing pyjamas in public as well as 
wearing pants in certain settings. To explain, Omanis consider it “inappropriate” to wear clothes 
in public that are typically worn at home for sleeping or lounging. The commenter specifically 
mentioned places where he witnessed the act, which are streets, mosques and hospitals and in 
governmental places. In regard to wearing pants, it is considered inappropriate to wear anything 
127 
 
other than the Omani traditional attire within government entities. 
7. Furthermore, data also highlighted elements of traditional attire such as the Omani dishdasha, 
cap, and dagger goes in relations to certain acts like dancing and eating during Ramdan. 
8. A number of traffic safety concerns were described to be inappropriate such as worshippers 
gathering In front of car due to not having pedestrian bridge near Sultan Qaboos Mosque as well 
as lack of proper sidewalks and poor lighting near the Grand Mall. 
9.  Moreover, some of the tweets reflect deep concern about practices and behaviours that are seen 
as contrary to cultural and the Omani morals such as “ vendors blackmailing shoppers by forcing 
them to buy” as it “ offends the morals of the Omani traders”. Also, by “violating our leader” was 
considered not only inappropriate but also was described to be “ a moral degradation”. 
6.3 Thematic analysis of the corpus 
Building on the patterns identified in the corpus analysis, this section presents the thematic analysis 
of the X dataset, focusing on how incivility is constructed and evaluated. While the frequency and 
concordance analysis offered an initial view of commonly co-occurring expressions and the 
immediate linguistic context surrounding the hashtags, the thematic analysis moves beyond these 
surface patterns to offer a deeper exploration of how frequently occurring patterns in the data reflect 
widely held, but often implicit, norms that inform evaluations of incivility. This analysis aims to 
uncover the underlying sociocultural norms that shape users’ judgements by closely examining 
specific instances of recurring linguistic patterns associated with the hashtags  حضاري  غير 
(‘uncivilised’) and  الئق غير (‘inappropriate’). As with offence, these evaluations are not arbitrary but 
socially constructed and negotiated through discourse (Culpeper, 2011; Locher & Watts, 2005). 
Moments in which users call out incivility often reveal expectations about proper public conduct 
expectations that, while not always explicitly stated, are nevertheless discursively invoked. 
To interpret the nature of these violated norms and expectations, the thematic analysis is informed 
by Spencer-Oatey’s rapport management framework (2002, 2008), which offers a structured 
approach to understanding the grounds that participants allude to when justifying their judgements 
particularly those related to face sensitivities, sociality rights, and obligations. This framework 
enables a more nuanced interpretation of why certain behaviours are judged as uncivil in the data, 
and what these judgements reveal about the values embedded in the sociocultural context in which 
they are made. Nonetheless, as explained in the methodology chapter, Section 3.7, it is the data that 
direct me, not the categories of the framework itself. 
The following analysis reveals the underlying norms that shape and inform societal expectations 
128 
 
regarding appropriate behaviours. While the data includes a variety of behaviours regarding 
appropriate behaviour, such as throwing rubbish, eating during Ramadan, and wearing inappropriate 
clothing, the thematic analysis does not group the findings according to these individual acts. 
Instead, the themes are organised around the broader social norms that these behaviours are 
perceived to violate. This approach allows for a clearer understanding of how users construct and 
evaluate incivility in discourse, not simply based on the nature of the act, but on the specific 
normative expectations it is seen to breach. 
6.4 Themes overview 
The following table summarises the themes and the sub-themes found in the data, followed by sections 
elaborating on them: 
 
Themes 
Failure to uphold the norm of adhering to Islamic teachings 
Failure to adhere to the norm of projecting a positive image of the area 
Failure to uphold the norm of shared responsibility for maintaining cleanliness 
Failure to uphold Omani identity norms 
Table 6.4: Overview of X data themes 
 
 
6.5 Failure to uphold the norm of adhering to Islamic teachings 
A key normative expectation evident in the data is that individuals should behave in ways that reflect 
and uphold Islamic teachings. In the Omani context, Islam is not only a religious belief system but a 
deeply embedded reference for social conduct, contributing to people’s understanding of what is 
appropriate and inappropriate. Acts such as littering on many occasions were judged as violations of 
religious obligations. These judgements are frequently supported by references to Islamic principles, 
such as ‘cleanliness is part of faith’ and public appeals to religious conscience. 
These reactions are understood as responses to breaches of sociality obligations where the source 
of the obligations is religion. As Spencer-Oatey (2008) and Spencer-Oatey and Kádár (2021) note 
sociality obligations may arise from behavioural conventions, and in this context, those conventions are 
shaped by Islamic teachings that establish cleanliness as a religious norm. In this sense, incivility is 
constructed as a religious failure, in which individuals are judged not only for what they did but for 
failing to live up to a norm that is expected of them as members of a religious community. 
129 
 
The following examples from the data illustrate how incivility is framed as a violation of Islamic 
teachings:

“…tires discarded next to the sports complex in Al Buraimi uncivil and inappropriate view at the 
entrance of the wilaya and it distort the public view (message to the concerned authorities)- 
cleanliness is part of faith”. 
 
In post (1), the commenter expressed frustration over the trash left behind by attendees after an 
event, stating that “everyone just threw their trash.” To highlight that this behaviour deviated from 
expected norms, the commenter emphasised a shared Islamic identity by writing, “People, we are 
people of religion and cleanliness.” The use of the collective “we” constructs a shared identity 
grounded in Islamic values, implying that there are common religiously informed norms about how 
members of society should behave in public spaces. 
130 
 
By identifying the group as ‘people of religion’, the commenter positions Islam as the framework that 
guides everyday conduct. In this context, cleanliness is not simply a matter of civic duty, but a 
religious expectation. This is further reinforced in post (4), where another user writes, ‘Cleanliness 
is part of faith’. By framing cleanliness as an aspect of religious identity, both tweets reveal how 
uncivil behaviour, such as littering, is not just seen as inappropriate, but as a violation of religiously 
derived social obligations. The act is evaluated as uncivil precisely because it fails to align with norms 
that are grounded in Islamic teachings, which function as a shared moral and behavioural reference 
point in Omani society. 
Along the same lines, post (2) reflects deep concern about repeated uncivil behaviours, particularly 
the act of leaving trash in public spaces and the improper disposal of waste. These actions are 
described as behaviour that “does not befit a civilised person who is commanded by Islam to be 
clean.” This unified statement merges both social and religious expectations into a single evaluative 
judgement. The phrase suggests that being "civilised" involves not only meeting communal standards 
of cleanliness but also adhering to religious obligations. By referencing Islam’s command to be clean, 
the commenter introduces a religious dimension to the concept of civility, framing these acts as 
violations of Islamic teachings. In this context, cleanliness is not merely a matter of social etiquette or 
civic responsibility; it is presented as a religious duty. Therefore, when individuals fail to maintain 
cleanliness, their behaviour is judged not only as inappropriate but as a breach of obligations 
grounded in both social and religious norms. 
Moreover, example (3) reinforces this religious framing by linking the absence of religious conscience 
to the emergence of uncivil behaviour. The commenter attributes the act of littering to a lack of 
religious awareness stating that such behaviour is “unacceptable to a Muslim person”. This phrase 
underscores the belief that Islamic teachings provide clear behavioural guidance, and that deviation 
from these expectations results in conduct judged as uncivilised. Here, incivility is constructed not as 
a failure to internalise and act upon religious obligations. 
To sum up, these posts (1,2,3 & 4) demonstrate that littering is judged not only as a civic or 
environmental issue but more importantly, as a violation of Islamic norms. They reflect a broader 
understanding within the discourse that cleanliness is an expectation derived from religious teachings, 
and that breaching this expectation constitutes a violation of sociality obligations whose source is 
Islam. 
 
131 
 
6.6 Failure to adhere to the norm of projecting a positive image of the area 
A significant number of posts demonstrate that littering is perceived as an act that tarnishes the 
image of the area. This notion refers to behaviours that are seen to contribute negatively to how a 
location is perceived, both by locals and outsiders. The commenters place great concern on how the 
area is presented to others and how the area is perceived by others, which indicates that members 
of the society are expected to uphold a positive image of the area. In other words, individuals are 
held accountable not just for their actions, but also for how these actions impact the collective image 
of the area. This concern with public image aligns with Spencer-Oatey’s (2008) concept of social 
identity face, which refers to the desire for one’s group to be positively evaluated. 
While Spencer-Oatey’s (2008) notion of social identity face typically refers to groups and their desire 
to be positively evaluated, this study extends the concept to include the way places are discursively 
treated as extensions of the community’s image. When a public space becomes dirty, neglected, or 
misrepresented particularly in tourist sites like Ras Al Jinz Turtle Reserve or public parks like Riyam 
the behaviour is judged as uncivilised or inappropriate, because it reflects poorly on the identity and 
values of the area’s residents or caretakers. The following examples demonstrate how incivility is 
constructed as a threat to the social identity associated with the area and, by extension, the wider 
community.

“My brothers at Muscat Municipality, this is completely inappropriate scenes in public park

132 
 
 
 
The data reveals a recurring concern with how uncivil behaviour, particularly littering, impacts the 
way public spaces and, by extension, entire communities are perceived. In tweet (5), the commenter 
contrasts the past and present condition of Dibba, noting that it “was one of the cleanest wilayas” 
but is now associated with “uncivilised images.” This shift is attributed to the “negligence of a 
responsible party,” and the rhetorical question “Why are we projecting an uncivilised image of the 
wilaya?” clearly reflects an underlying expectation: that residents and officials are responsible for 
maintaining a positive and respectable image of the place. The emphasis is not only on cleanliness 
but on the collective image the area conveys to outsiders. This suggests a norm in which public 
spaces are expected to visually represent the values and standards of their communities. 
A similar concern is expressed in tweet (6), where the commenter criticises the presence of garbage 
at a popular tourist destination. The post highlights that the issue “reflects negatively on the place,” 
suggesting that the act of littering is evaluated not just for its environmental harm but for how it affects 
the site's public image. The concern is intensified by the fact that the location is frequently visited by 
tourists, which elevates the expectation that such spaces should be well-maintained and orderly. 
Tweet (7) builds on this theme by noting the presence of “many visitors” to Riyam Park and calling 
the visual state of the area “completely inappropriate.” Here, the presence of an external audience 
makes the failure to maintain the space more consequential, as it threatens the way the area and, 
implicitly, the community is perceived. 
Across these tweets, incivility is not judged solely as a violation of cleanliness or environmental 
standards, but as a threat to the social identity face of the community. In this case, the places 
themselves like ‘parks’ and ‘tourist sites’, are discursively treated as representations of the 
community, and uncivil behaviours such as littering are evaluated based on the damage they do to 
that collective image. The concern is not only with what was done, but with how it makes the area, and 
by extension, its people appear to others. 
6.7 Failure to uphold the norm of shared responsibility for maintaining cleanliness  
Another recurring norm identified in the data is the expectation that all members of society share the 
responsibility for maintaining cleanliness in public spaces. Across tweets, behaviours such as 
littering are frequently described as ‘uncivil’ or ‘inappropriate’ and these evaluations are often 
accompanied by concerns about a lack of communal responsibility. Rather than focusing solely on 
the physical act, commenters highlight how such behaviour reflects a broader disregard for the 
shared duty to care for public environments. In this sense, the act is constructed as uncivil not solely 
133 
 
due to its visible impact on the environment, but because it signifies a failure to fulfill a socially 
expected obligation of collective responsibility. 
In this context, shared societal responsibility refers to the collective obligation of individuals and 
groups within a society to maintain the cleanliness and orderliness of public spaces. The data reveals 
a clear belief that this responsibility should not fall solely on specific groups such as municipal 
workers or local authorities but is a role shared across the community. This expectation reflects what 
Spencer-Oatey (2000, 2008) describes as sociality obligations the behaviours individuals feel others 
should or should not perform based on socially shared expectations. In the data, this sense of 
obligation is explicitly appealed to, as seen in repeated calls such as “مسؤوً ال  كن” which translates to 
‘be responsible’. These appeals demonstrate that the evaluation of incivility is grounded in a clear 
expectation that individuals must contribute to the upkeep of shared spaces. The obligation here is 
shaped by behavioural conventions tied to communal life: individuals are expected not only to act 
responsibly themselves but also to ensure that others, such as family members or domestic workers, 
act in accordance with these norms. The following examples illustrate how the expectations and 
obligations discussed above are reflected in everyday discourse, where acts of littering are evaluated 
as breaches of shared social responsibility:

as ‘uncivil practices’ that reflect both ‘a lack of awareness’ and ‘weak societal responsibility’. This 
framing constructs incivility as a recurring failure to meet the normative expectation that all members 
of society contribute to the care of public spaces. The evaluation goes beyond the act of littering 
itself, linking it to wider social consequences such as environmental and health damage, and to the 
degradation of the area's aesthetic and civilised image. In doing so, the tweet reinforces the idea 
that incivility arises not merely from what is done, but by from, whether individuals are seen to meet 
the socially expected obligation to contribute to the upkeep of shared spaces. Similarly, in tweet (9), 
the failure to fulfil this shared responsibility is made explicit through the phrase ‘be responsible’ which 
functions as a direct appeal to individual behavioural obligation. Moreover, although the commenter 
acknowledges that no rubbish bins were available nearby, this is immediately dismissed as a 
justification for littering. The assertion that the absence of bins is “not an excuse” signals that the 
expectation to act responsibly remains intact, regardless of situational constraints. 
Further, this sense of obligation is extended beyond individuals to include family members and 
domestic workers. Tweet (10) condemns the act of placing garbage beside an empty bin, stating that 
“even if it is the housemaid, the family is responsible for teaching her that this behaviour is wrong.” 
This view reflects an expectation not only to act appropriately but to guide others in accordance with 
social norms. In this case, the conventions surrounding the use of public space carry prescriptive 
force. The frustration expressed in the data reflects that these conventions have evolved into clearly 
perceived obligations which when unfulfilled, lead to the behaviour being labelled as uncivil. These 
examples show that incivility is evaluated in relation to failure to meet culturally embedded 
expectations of shared responsibility, rather than the act itself. 
6.8 Upholding Omani identity norms 
One of the most dominant themes that emerged in the thematic analysis is the failure to uphold 
135 
 
Omani identity norms. This theme captures public reaction to behaviours that are perceived as 
incompatible with what is expected of individuals who visibly or culturally represent Omani identity. 
These expectations encompass both cultural values, social traditions, and moral traits associated 
with the Omani character, as well as everyday social conventions such as how individuals dress and 
present themselves in public settings like government offices or mosques. 
In the dataset, Omani identity is constructed as something both symbolically marked and socially 
performed. Cultural symbols such as  العماني  الزي [Omani dress],  العماني  الخنجر [the khanjar], and  الكمة  العمانية 
[the Omani cap], as observed in the corpus analysis (see Chapter 6, Section 6.2.2), not only signal 
belonging, but also activate specific behavioural expectations. When individuals who display these 
markers act in ways that contradict those expectations, they are judged not merely for the behaviour 
itself, but for the perceived mismatch between their visible identity and their public conduct. 
In addition to symbolic markers, Omani identity is also expressed through behavioural expectations 
that operate at both explicit and implicit levels. These include explicit institutional obligations such as 
dress codes in government offices as well as widely shared, unwritten obgliations about how 
individuals should dress, and present themselves in public. When these expectations are not met, 
behaviour is not simply judged in isolation but evaluated in light of broader social standards of 
appropriateness. In this way, identity is not just worn through visible symbols it is also performed 
through everyday conduct that aligns with collective understandings of what it means to behave like 
an Omani. 
This pattern is best understood through Spencer-Oatey’s (2008) concepts of social identity face and 
sociality obligations, which help explain how incivility is evaluated. Social identity face, which refers 
to the desire for one’s group membership to be positively evaluated by others. In these instances, 
identity carries behavioural obligations. In parallel, sociality obligations are the behavioural 
expectations individuals believe others should or should not do (Spencer-Oatey, 2008) . In parallel, 
sociality obligations are the things individuals feel others should or should not do that are derived 
from various sources. In  the context of this study, when individuals are visibly marked as Omani 
through dress for; example, they are implicitly expected to act in ways that align with what the group 
collectively values and upholds. These visible markers come with a sense of obligation, whereby the 
person is expected to behave in accordance with shared norms. When such expectations are not 
met, the behaviour is not simply seen as inappropriate on an individual level, but as a violation that 
risks undermining the group’s positive identity in the eyes of others. In this way, the concepts of 
identity face and sociality obligations work together to explain how and why certain acts are judged 
136 
 
as uncivil: not because of the acts alone, but because of the social expectations attached to the 
identities of those performing them.
