In tweet (11), the commenter strongly condemns an individual who was seen eating and drinking in 
public during Ramadan. The act is described as " أبدًا  الئق  غير  تصرف" (“a completely inappropriate act”). 
However, the criticism is not directed at the behaviour in isolation. Instead, it is embedded within a 
broader evaluation of who the person is and how he appears. The disapproval intensifies as the 
commenter points out that the individual was “a Muslim,” “an Omani,” and “wearing the Omani 
dishdasha” a layered identity description that foregrounds both religious and national affiliation. 
Crucially, these identity references do not operate independently. Rather, they work together to 
construct the behaviour as a serious transgression of shared norms, particularly those tied to 
maintaining social identity face. In this context, the individual’s Muslim identity is not invoked to 
appeal to religious obligations per se, as was the case in the earlier theme on adhering to Islamic 
teachings ( see Section 6.5). Instead, religion functions here as part of a layered and unified identity, 
137 
 
it reinforces that being Muslim is implicitly integrated into what it means to be Omani. 
Moreover, the references to the individual as “an Omani” and “wearing the Omani dishdasha” are 
what intensify the perceived violation in this post. To explain, wearing the dishdasha places the 
individual within a recognised normative framework, where certain behaviours such as observing 
appropriate conduct during Ramadan are socially expected. The dishdasha is not merely traditional 
attire; it is a visible symbol that signals the wearer’s alignment with a normative framework and its 
associated obligations. As such, when someone visibly marked by this symbol fails to meet these 
expectations, the act is judged not simply as inappropriate behaviour, but as a public breach of the 
norms tied to Omani identity. This perceived contradiction between appearance and conduct 
constitutes a threat to social identity face (Spencer-Oatey, 2008), as the individual is seen to 
undermine the image of the group they visibly represent. 
Similarly, in posts (12) and (13) the expression of disapproval is anchored not only in the perceived 
inappropriateness of the actions themselves such as dancing or appearing in videos deemed “  غير 
 الئقة” (inappropriate) but more significantly in the fact that the individuals were wearing Omani national 
symbols, such as the traditional attire and the khanjar, while engaging in these acts. The critique is 
not directed merely at the content of the behaviour, but at the contradiction between that behaviour 
and the expectations evoked by what the individuals are wearing. In both cases, the behaviour is 
framed as a violation of what it means to visibly and publicly embody Omani identity. 
In post (12), the commenter links this kind of behaviour to a broader societal failure to respect Omani 
heritage, explicitly referencing the neglect of ‘history’, ‘Omani identity’, and ‘customs and traditions. 
The disapproval is heightened by the fact that the individuals in the video are wearing the Omani 
dress while engaging in what is perceived to be inappropriate conduct. Here, the attire is evaluated; it 
signals that the individuals are expected to represent and uphold the cultural values embedded in 
the nation’s dress. The act of wearing the Omani attire in a context perceived as socially improper 
creates a dissonance between appearance and conduct. In this context, the Omani dress carries 
implicit expectations of decorum, respect for tradition, and national pride. When those wearing it 
engage in behaviour deemed inappropriate, the act is interpreted as a public misrepresentation of 
Omani identity. 
In post (13), the commenter expresses increasing frustration over the recurrence of videos showing 
young men dancing “inappropriately” while wearing the ‘Omani khanjar’. The phrase “In the past two 
or three months, I’ve seen more than one clip’ highlights the repeated nature of the behaviour, 
suggesting a growing concern about a pattern of conduct that contradicts expected norms. What 
138 
 
intensifies the disapproval is not just the act of dancing, but the fact that those performing it are 
wearing the khanjar, a visible marker of Omani identity that positions the wearer as a representative 
of what it means to be Omani. 
This framing becomes clear in the comment “Honestly, this does not please me,” which initially 
conveys a personal reaction but is immediately followed by a broader appeal: “When will they be 
stopped?” The shift from individual disappointment to a call for intervention suggests that the concern 
extends beyond the specific behaviour to a wider concern about how Omani identity is being 
portrayed. In this context, the criticism is not simply about social appropriateness but about the 
perceived misrepresentation of cultural values in a public and highly visible way. Wearing the khanjar 
carries an implicit obligation to uphold the behavioural norms associated with Omani identity, and 
when those expectations are violated, the action is judged as a breach of shared cultural standards.

“It is inappropriate to see a young man over twenty years old wearing pajamas in the 
mosque, on the street, in the hospital and various public places and government 
offices. They are called pajamas for a reason, meaning you should only wear them when 
you are going to sleep. In other words, do not embarrass yourself by dressing up in pajamas 
wherever you go, thinking it is stylish or fashionable”. 
 
While the previous tweets presented the Omani attire as a symbolic marker of national identity, where 
the disapproval arose from individuals engaging in inappropriate behaviour while visibly dressed in 
national attire (see posts 10,12 &13), the following tweets shift the focus toward contexts where 
traditional clothing operates not just symbolically, but as a prescriptive code. In these cases, attire is 
139 
 
framed as something that must be adhered to either through formal regulation or implicit social 
expectation, particularly in institutional or religious settings. Here, the act of dressing inappropriately 
is constructed as a breach of the sociocultural norms, and commenters’ posts reflect an effort to 
reinforce both implicit social norms and explicit institutional norms. 
Both tweet (14) and tweet (15) focus on individuals who failed to adhere to expected dress norms in 
specific public settings. In tweet (14), the commenter describes instances where citizens have turned 
away from government offices for arriving in trousers and shirts rather than the traditional Omani 
attire. This highlights the enforcement of an explicit institutional dress code, where wearing the 
national dress is not just encouraged but required. The tweet further mentions cases in which 
individuals were asked to leave civil centers due to shaving their beards “in an inappropriate manner,” 
suggesting that appearance expectations extend beyond clothing to grooming practices. Here, 
deviation from accepted standards of presentation is not framed as a breach of formal norms tied to 
institutional respectability. 
Tweet (15), by contrast, highlights implicit social norms governing dress in public spaces beyond 
formal institutions. The commenter expresses disapproval at seeing “a young man over twenty” 
wearing pyjamas in places such as mosques, streets, and government buildings. Unlike the context 
in tweet (14), these settings ‘mosques’, and ‘street’ may not be governed by formal dress codes. 
However, the tweet reflects an implicit sociocultural norm that shapes expectations about 
appropriate attire in public places. The commenter finds it inappropriate that a man “over twenty 
years old” would appear in pyjamas outside the home, implying that by this age, he should have 
internalised social standards of public presentation. The reference to age highlights a perceived 
maturity gap between what is expected and what was displayed. The act becomes inappropriate not 
only due to the clothing itself, but because the individual’s failure to align his public appearance with 
the age-related expectations of appropriate social behaviour. Moreover, the norm violation is 
reinforced through the commenter’s literal interpretation of the pyjamas’ function: ‘It’s called pyjamas, 
meaning you only wear them if you are going to sleep’. The commenter sets clear boundaries around 
the appropriate use of pyjamas, reinforcing the norm that they are not suitable for public settings and 
framing their use outside the home as a clear breach of expected behaviour. 
To sum up, tweets (14) and (15) illustrate how traditional and everyday clothing are not only symbolic 
markers of identity but are also governed by both explicit regulations and implicit sociocultural 
expectations. While tweet (14) reflects institutional enforcement of dress codes where attire becomes 
a formal requirement, tweet (15) reveals that  behaviour can be judged inappropriate as public even 
140 
 
in the absence of formal rules. In both cases, the construction of incivility stems from perceived failure 
to adhere to explicit and implicit norms of the dress code. 
 
In earlier examples under this theme, the evaluation of incivility was based on the mismatch between 
visible identity markers and the behaviour performed (see examples 11, 12, and 13). In examples 14 
and 15, the judgement was grounded less in symbolic markers and more in the failure to adhere to 
explicit and implicit expectations surrounding dress in public settings. 
In examples (17) and (18), the focus shifts to moral expectations explicitly linked to Omani identity. 
In example (17), the commenter expresses concern over exploitative sales tactics in Muttrah Souq, 
stating that the phenomenon “offends the morals of the Omani trader.” The act of coercing customers 
into buying is not only described as “ الئق  غير” (inappropriate) but also as repellent to both tourists and 
locals. This judgement reflects a deep-seated moral expectation: that traders should embody honesty, 
hospitality, and integrity qualities traditionally associated with Omani commercial identity. The 
comment thus invokes a normative ideal of how Omani traders should behave, and positions 
deviation from this ideal as both culturally inappropriate and socially damaging. While this 
phenomenon was described to be offensive the morality of the Omani merchants, it was deemed “ 
inappropriate”. The inappropriateness stemmed from the action of the vendors “extorting shoppers 
by forcing them to buy in an inappropriate and off-putting manner for tourists and shoppers".

Similarly, in example (18), the focus is on influencers or public figures who produce content perceived 
to be morally inappropriate. The commenter questions whether citizens have the right to sue 
influencers who publish material that “incites rebellion against values, customs, and traditions” and 
who appear in “ الئق  غير  لبس” (inappropriate dress). This post reveals a concern with how public figures, 
especially those visibly situated within Omani society, may threaten shared moral and cultural 
standards through their behaviour. Incivility in this context is not just framed as an individual 
transgression, but as a challenge to the collective moral fabric that defines the community. 
In both cases, the judgement of incivility is tied to a perceived violation of moral expectations that 
are integral to Omani identity expectations, not only about how one appears in public, but also about 
acting in ways that reflect and uphold shared moral values and social customs. 
6.9 Conclusion 
The findings from this chapter bring together insights from both corpus analysis and thematic analysis 
to reveal how public evaluations of incivility on X (formerly Twitter) in Oman are shaped by distinct 
but overlapping normative frameworks. While the behaviours being judged often fall within a narrow 
range, most notably, the act of throwing rubbish, the rationale behind these judgements varies 
considerably depending on the specific norm that is perceived to have been breached. This 
conclusion synthesises the key insights from both layers of analysis by highlighting how the same act 
can be differently constructed and viewed through different lenses. 
6.9.1 Throwing rubbish: a singular bhaviour, multiple normative violations 
Across both corpora, the most frequently cited uncivil or inappropriate act was "throwing rubbish", 
which appeared 25 times in the /ɣeːr ħɑˈdˤɑːriː/ [uncivil] corpus and 6 times in the /ɣeːr laːʔiq/ 
[inappropriate] corpus. However, thematic analysis showed that this act was not evaluated in uniform 
terms. Instead, it triggered multiple types of disapproval depending on which normative lens was 
applied: 
• In the theme "Failure to uphold the norm of adhering to Islamic Teachings" throwing rubbish 
was framed as a violation of religious obligation. Tweets explicitly referenced Islamic principles 
such as ‘cleanliness is part of faith’ making it clear that incivility was constructed as failure to 
follow Islamic norms. The commenters explicitly emphasised that ‘we are people of religion’ 
thus such behaviour is especially inappropriate and contradicts the Islamic obligations 
• The theme "Failure to adhere to the norm of projecting a positive image of the area" the same 
142 
 
behaviour was judged through the lens of communal image and reputation. Here, incivility was 
not only about littering, but about how such acts were perceived by outsiders, especially tourists. 
The damage was framed not in religious terms, but in symbolic terms of how the area and by 
extension, its residents are represented to others. This connects with Spencer- Oatey’s (2008) 
notion of social identity face, where individuals as a group are concerned about their image and 
how they want to be perceived by others. 
• The theme "Failure to uphold the norm of shared responsibility for maintaining cleanliness" 
framed the act in terms of collective duty. This time the concern was not external perception or 
breaching religious norms, but a broader sense of a communal obligation. Tweets explicitly 
referenced "weak societal responsibility" and included direct appeals to individuals saying ‘be 
responsible’. In the examples presented, the expectation that all members of society to 
contribute to maintaining public cleanliness was highlighted. 
Thus, although the corpus findings revealed the prominence of rubbish-related terms across 
hashtags, it was the thematic analysis that unpacked the qualitative depth of these evaluations by 
linking them to distinct normative expectations. 
6.9.2 Performing the Omani identity: dress and behaviour 
The second major focus of the analysis revolved around attire, particularly in the theme "Failure to 
uphold Omani identity norms". While the corpora reflected recurring terms related to traditional attire 
such as ‘Omani dress’, ‘the Omani Khanjar’, and ‘Omani cap’, the thematic analysis revealed that 
these symbols functioned as more than just indicators of national belonging. They activated 
behavioural expectations tied to what it means to embody Omani identity. 
• In some examples, incivility was constructed through the mismatch between visible identity 
markers and public conduct. Individuals wearing the Omani dishdasha while engaging in 
behaviour deemed inappropriate (e.g., dancing, eating in public during Ramadan) were judged 
not only for the acts themselves, but for failing to meet the behavioural obligations carried by 
their visible identity. This reflects an evaluative logic grounded in social identity face, where the 
individual is expected to act in a way that protects the group’s image. 
• In other instances, tweets focused on violations of explicit and implicit dress codes, 
particularly in public institutions or sacred spaces. Whether it was pyjamas worn in mosques or 
individuals arriving at government offices without the traditional attire, these breaches were 
framed as failures to perform socially appropriate behaviour. The norms here were rooted less 
143 
 
in symbolic identity and more in codified and context-specific behavioural expectations. 
• Lastly, a smaller set of tweets drew attention to moral expectations embedded in Omani 
identity. For example, traders who pressured customers were criticised for violating "the morals 
of the Omani trader," indicating that morality itself is considered a defining trait of the Omani 
identity. 
144 
 
CHAPTER 7: RESULTS - PHASE 2 (B) 
 
7.1 Introduction 
Building on the analysis of X data presented in Chapter 6, this chapter examines personal accounts 
of incivility as narrated by participants through diary reports, in order to further explore the norms that 
shape how Omani people perceive uncivil behaviour. The findings from the previous phase indicated 
that incivility was most commonly associated with specific public behaviours such as throwing 
rubbish, wearing inappropriate attire, or acting in ways deemed morally improper. However, these 
actions were not evaluated in isolation. Instead, participants judged them through the lens of 
underlying sociocultural norms, including religious obligations (e.g., maintaining cleanliness), 
expectations tied to Omani identity (e.g., dressing or behaving in a way that reflects well on the 
community), and shared responsibilities (e.g., collective care for public spaces). 
This chapter builds on those findings by turning to individual, lived experiences. While the themes 
identified in Phase One were not used to inform the content of the diary report, the wording and 
structure of the questions were shaped by the communicative framing observed in the tweets ( see 
Chapter 3 section 3.6.2 for further explanation). This phase explores how participants themselves 
narrate and evaluate incidents they perceived as uncivil, revealing both the types of behaviours they 
found inappropriate and the social norms that underpin their judgements. It is also worth noting that 
not all participants explicitly justified their judgements. Some simply reported the behaviour and 
described their emotional response such as anger or discomfort without articulating the reason for 
considering an act as uncivil. In these cases, the disapproval appears to stem from a general sense 
that the act was inappropriate or out of place, highlighting how certain forms of incivility are 
recognised as self-evident within the cultural context. 
The diary report data thus provides a deeper, more contextualised understanding of incivility by 
highlighting how individuals interpret and respond to norm violations in everyday life. It moves 
beyond public discourse to examine private evaluations, offering insight into how uncivil behaviour 
is recognised, justified, and emotionally experienced. The analysis presented in this chapter is 
guided by Braun and Clarke’s (2006) thematic analysis approach and draws on Spencer-Oatey’s 
(2008) rapport management framework particularly the concepts of sociality rights and obligations, 
and social identity face to interpret how participants construct and articulate evaluations of 
inappropriate conduct. 
145 
 
The chapter is structured as follows: Section 7.2 outlines the key thematic patterns identified in the 
diary report data. Sections 7.3 to 7.6 each examine one of the core themes in detail, using participant 
excerpts to illustrate how incivility is constructed and assessed through the lens of social norms.

7.3 Failure to uphold the norm of public decency norms 
A key norm that shaped participants’ evaluations of incivility was the expectation that individuals 
uphold standards of modesty and moral conduct in public, particularly in how they dress, behave in 
public and interact with the other gender. Public decency refers to a level of behaviour in public 
spaces that is considered socially acceptable and not obscene according to shared moral standards 
(Dalton & Klingemann, 2007). In this context, participants’ perceptions of incivility were often 
triggered by actions that violated these expectations such as immodest clothing, inappropriate 
gestures or speech, and behavior that blurred gender boundaries in ways deemed culturally 
unacceptable. The judgement of these behaviours was not only tied to social identity face the desire 
for the group but also to sociality rights and obligations (Spencer-Oatey, 2008). When individuals act 
in ways that undermine public modesty, they are seen to breach the shared obligations to 
146 
 
maintain socially appropriate conduct. This is especially pronounced in settings where such 
behaviour reflects poorly on the group’s collective image. Thus, the evaluation of incivility arises not 
simply from the actions themselves, but from the perception that these actions threaten both the 
group’s social standing and the implicit expectations of appropriate conduct in public spaces. 
 
The following subsections examine the three most common ways in which this norm was perceived 
to be violated: failure to dress modestly in public, failure to refrain from engaging in obscene or 
socially inappropriate acts and interacting with the other gender in ways deemed culturally 
unacceptable. 
7.3.1 Omanis find it uncivil to dress immodestly in public 
The informants in all the examples in this section collectively underscore a concern for public 
decency norms, particularly concerning clothing choices in public places such as shopping malls. It 
should be mentioned here that to “dress inappropriately” and “dressing immodestly” means not to 
follow an implicit dress code in public areas, which is to wear clothes that cover the shoulders, upper 
arms and fall below the knees, and women that are expected to cover their hair. 
 
The narrator in the narrative (1) expresses disapproval towards what he observes as “inappropriate 
clothes” worn by Omani women and having their hair being shown out of their hijab. There is a clear 
expectation for women here to uphold modesty standards by dressing and wearing the hijab in a 
specific way to avoid bringing “shame” upon themselves, and the broader community “because 
shame is on everyone”. The narrator accentuated this expectation by using a proverb which suggests 
that an individual’s negative action will affect everyone collectively, therefore, the narrator says “ 
women are women, so take care of yourself” implying a communal responsibility to maintain societal 
values, by dressing appropriately in public.

these expectations can reflect poorly on the society as a whole. It implies a belief that members of the 
society have a responsibility to uphold and project their values through their personal appearance 
“dressing modestly”, and that deviation from these norms can be seen as a failure to respect or 
maintain social standards.

In all of the examples presented, the narrators describe individuals who did not adhere to modesty

7.3.2 Omanis find it uncivil to commit any obscene activity in public 
The following narratives highlight instances where the narrators witness obscene behaviour in public 
in various contexts. Obscenity in this context refers to any action, image or words that offend sexual 
morality, such as committing a sexual act or displaying affection or using vulgar language in public. 
 
 
In narrative (5), the narrator describes uncivil behaviour committed by men and women in public 
such as “someone hugging a girl” and engaging in “an indecent behaviour in the car”. The narrator 
describes these behaviours as “indecent”, reflecting a breach of societal norms and expectations 
regarding adhering to public decency norms.

deviates from the accepted norms of decency. Furthermore, there is an implicit sense of regret for

7.4 Failure to uphold the norm of treating expatriate workers with dignity 
One of the key norms that shaped participants’ evaluations of incivility was the expectation that 
people, especially those perceived as socially or structurally vulnerable, should be treated with dignity. 
In this context, dignity refers to the basic recognition of a person's worth and humanity, and it includes 
being treated with fairness, and consideration, and without humiliation or degradation. This norm 
was especially salient in how participants responded to the treatment of expatriate workers, who 
frequently occupy service roles in Omani society. There is a strong social expectation that these 
individuals should be treated with dignity and not subjected to public humiliation or mistreatment. 
The examples in this section focus on incidents in which expatriate workers were yelled at, 
demeaned, or physically mistreated by Omanis in public settings. These behaviours were 
consistently evaluated as violations of the norm of treating expatriate workers with dignity. 
Participants expressed strong emotions such as anger, shame, and embarrassment, viewing such 
acts not merely as individual failings but as public violations that reflect poorly on Omani society. In 
many cases, the mistreatment of expatriates was seen to undermine the group’s identity face that 
is, the positive group image of Omanis as respectful and considerate in their treatment of others. 
Participants expressed concern that such behaviour contradicts the values traditionally associated 
with Omani society, thus harming the group’s collective reputation. In other instances, the evaluation 
was grounded in sociality obligations: the belief that individuals, especially those who are socially or 
structurally vulnerable, should be treated with dignity and fairness 
 
In narrative (14), the informant describes a customer yelling at a café employee for not bringing their 
order to the table which caused the observer to feel “furious”. The informant does not explain why 
“yelling at the café employee” made the narrator feel angry, however, yelling at the café employee is 
a direct violation of the norm of treating people with dignity.

Similarly, narrative (17) describes a situation where a seller in shop is treated with disrespect. The 
incident elicits a reaction as she said, “felt annoyed” highlighting the narrator’s discomfort with 
“disrespecting the seller”. The narrator expresses that this conduct does not only impact the individual 
subjected to it, but also the broader societal perception of the person who committed the uncivil act 
as the narrator stated, it “gives a negative impression of both the society and the individual”. To 
explain, in terms of its impact to the society, “this behaviour tarnishes … the society”. This statement 
implies that the behaviour of individuals within a society reflects on the society as a whole. Therefore, 
the act of “disrespecting the seller” deviates significantly from the accepted behaviour within Omani 
society which shows a disregard for the shared norm which is to treat people with respect. 
Furthermore, in terms of its impact on the individual who exhibited this behaviour, the narrator feels 
“pity” towards this person, recognising that their disrespectful behaviour is a sign of poor upbringing 
“, not well parented”, and a diminution of their own self-worth. This perspective implies a deeper 
societal norm: respect is not only about how we treat others, but also significantly influences how we

Narrative (19) intensifies the breach of the norm of respect when an individual, a driving instructor, 
physically assaults a learner “hit a worker inside the car”, in the middle of a driving lesson on the 
street. This act contravenes the fundamental societal expectation that all individuals regardless of 
their status or role, deserve to be treated with dignity and respect. The narrator’s emotional response 
to witnessing this violation is one of hatred “I felt hatred towards this person’s behaviour” highlighting 
the gravity of the instructor’s misconduct. Moreover, the desire to intervene “I wanted to get out of 
the car and go toward him”, underscores the narrator’s recognition of the unjust nature of the act, 
emphasising the inherent understanding that such behaviour is unacceptable and deserving of 
immediate condemnation. However, the intervention was hindered by the traffic light adding a layer 
of helplessness to the scenario. The text clearly delineates the expectation that the learner, who has 
paid for a service, should receive instructions and guidance, not abuse of offence, “This worker has 
paid for the driver to get driving lessons, not to beat him and offend him”. This expectation is rooted 
in the norm of respect, which demands that all interactions be conducted with courtesy and humanity. 
Further compounding the violation is the learner’s background. To explain, the learner is described 
by the narrator as someone who has come from afar and works to support his family in the instructor’s 
country “he came from a far place to work in your country and provide for his family”, highlighting the 
learner vulnerability. The narrator concludes his narrative with advice embedded with the expectation 
that “you should help him, extend a helping hand, and respect him as you want to be respected and 
treat him kindly”; the expected act in this situation is to support the learner, who has not only paid 
for this service but also carries the burden of supporting a family. The beating inflicted upon him 
is not just a beach of “respect”, but a disregard for his human dignity and precarious situation he 
navigates coming from afar to earn a livelihood.

the workers, who are expatriates. Moreover, all of the emotions expressed in the narratives whether 
anger, sadness, shame, dismay, hatred, annoyance, or pity, serve to highlight the social expectation 
of respect that has been breached. These narratives underscore the importance of maintaining 
respectful interactions and the social consequences when these norms are not adhered to. 
7.5 Failure to uphold the norm of treating the children with care and guidance 
One of the key norms shaping participants’ evaluations of incivility is the expectation that children 
should be treated with empathy, dignity, and proper guidance. Across the data, participants judged 
behaviours such as name-calling, physical punishment, public shaming, or failure to intervene in 
misbehaviour as inappropriate and uncivil. These acts were not only seen as harmful to the child but 
also as violations of broader social expectations about how children should be treated in public 
places and guided. 
These evaluations are best understood through sociality rights and obligations and face sensitivities 
(Spencer-Oatey, 2008). Under this theme, the relevant sociality obligation is the parental duty to treat 
children with care and to guide them. When parents or those in caregiving roles fail to meet these 
obligations, either through harsh punishment or passive neglect, their actions are seen as breaches 
of fundamental social responsibilities. At the same time, many of the narrator’s reactions express 
concern for the child’s quality face that is, the child’s sense of self-worth, which was publicly 
undermined through verbal or physical mistreatment. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
. 
 
The following narratives describe incidents where children were mistreated in various forms. 
Narrative (20) involves a child being publicly called “an animal” by their parent in a market after 
accidentally dropping some products on the floor. The observer felt “embarrassed” and “sorry for this

“There were two children with their parents in a park. They were tempering with roses,

Narrative (23) outlines a scenario in a public park where two children were engaging in disruptive 
behaviours, “tempering with roses, flowers, the fence and the water pipes”. Additionally, the 
children’s loud and annoying screaming drew attention and significantly disturbed others around them. 
Nevertheless, while all of that happened “in front of their parents”, the parents did not do anything “ 
without any reaction or guidance or correction for their wrong behaviour”. The fact that the parents 
did not react to their children’s “wrong behaviour” is what made the narrator angry. The narrator’s 
frustration with the parents’ non-reactive stance points towards a breached norm where the parents 
were expected to guide or correct their children. Thus, the parents’ decision not to act or correct their 
children’s behaviours highlights a neglect of parental obligations to discipline and teach their children 
. Their inaction suggests a tolerance for disrespect towards public spaces and indifference towards 
the disturbance caused to others. The narrator warns of the long-term consequences of neglecting 
these obligations suggesting that today’s uncorrected behaviours could evolve into tomorrow’s more 
serious societal problems “raising a generation that does not value what is around them, potentially 
leading to more dangerous behaviour in the future”. In other words, failing to discipline and guide 
children not only leads to immediate disturbance in public spaces but also risks breeding a future 
generation that “doesn’t appreciate or take responsibility”. 
The norm broken in all of the above narratives is the fundamental expectation that children should 
be treated with care, kindness, understanding, and protection. The narratives show that this norm 
was violated by the children’s parents through the acts of name-calling, beating and using hurtful 
words with them in public, and through not guiding them. It also touches on the bystanders’ dilemma, 
feeling anger, and sadness, embarrassed but hesitating to intervene due to fear of worsening the 
situation. 
7.6 Failure to uphold norms of road conduct 
A recurring concern in participants’ accounts of incivility was the failure to observe expected norms 
of road behaviour. Across the dataset, informants described behaviours such as overtaking via the 
yellow lane during traffic congestion, taking up more than one parking space, and reckless driving in 
areas where families and children are present. These acts are consistently described as 
‘inappropriate, and ‘ uncivil’, highlighting their perceived incompatibility with the norms. 
Several participants explicitly noted that such actions like parking across multiple spaces or driving 
on the shoulder during traffic, involve ‘infringing on others’ rights’, highlighting a violation of the 
shared equity rights. The frustration was not only directed at the behaviour itself, but at the way it 
reflected on the collective image of Omani society. Several participants expressed concern that such 
162 
 
conduct ‘portrays Omanis in a negative way’, particularly in the presence of tourists, which directly 
ties with the concept of identity face, where individuals feel responsible for protecting their group’s 
image (Spencer-Oatey, 2000,2008). 
The following examples illustrate how breaches of road conduct norms were framed as violations of 
shared sociality obligations and, in some cases touch on identity face concerns.

One prime example presented in narrative (24), is the act of overtaking in congested traffic, “when 
someone overtakes you in the congestion from the yellow line”, and another example presented in 
narrative (25) is “overtaking cars from the storage lane to get to the front”. These acts made the 
narrators in both narratives feel “very angry”, as both of them not only feel that this behaviour is 
“inappropriate “and “distasteful”, but also the overtaker “was violating the rights of others without 
justification” as the narrator in narrative (24) explained that “we were all stuck in the traffic”. There is 
an element of unfairness here, as narrator mentioned that the overtaker “was violating the rights of 
others”, and “we were all stuck in the traffic”, which highlights that the overtaker prioritised his own 
163 
 
progress at the expense of others “the rights of others” despite the fact that being stuck in a traffic is 
a collective experience. Therefore, the narrators’ anger here stems from witnessing a blatant 
disregard for the collective expected norms that require the individuals to wait for their turn in 
situations of shared inconvenience. 
Moreover, another uncivil behaviour that was highlighted by the narrator in a narrative (25) is “not 
adhering to parking cars correctly in the parking lot”, which made the narrator angry “because the 
car was taking more than one parking space without any right”. The mention of “without any right” 
emphasises again the fact that the rights of others are infringed upon through the lack of 
consideration for others. 
 
Another incident that was highlighted in the data, narrative (26), is the “recklessness of young people 
driving in an area not designated for speeding”. The narrator emphasises that the reckless driving 
happens “despite the presence of families and children” which makes him feel disturbed and wish for 
a more “deterrent for this group of young people”. The violation here is not just a matter of breaking 
traffic laws but also reflects a disregard for the safety and well-being of others, particularly in spaces 
that should be safe for families and children. 
7.7 Failure to uphold the norms of maintaining public cleanliness 
A recurring theme in participants’ evaluations of incivility was the failure to maintain cleanliness in 
shared public spaces. Acts such as discarding rubbish in non-designated areas, throwing cigarette 
butts from cars, and neglecting to properly dispose of food waste were described as ‘uncivil’ and 
‘tarnishing the image of the country’. These evaluations were not simply based on the physical act 
of polluting, but on deeper social meanings attached to those actions. Participants viewed 
maintaining cleanliness in shared spaces as a collective responsibility an obligation that each 
individual is expected to uphold. When people fail to meet this obligation, they are seen as 
disregarding a key social duty, which reflects a violation of sociality obligations (Spencer-Oatey, 
2008).

one that is “still lit”, not only pollutes the environment but also poses a fire risk, highlighting a 
disregard for both environment and the safety of others. This action sparked a strong emotional 
response in the narrator leading them to physically retrieve the discarded cigarette butt and “threw it 
in his face”. This reaction underscores the narrator’s frustration and anger towards the individual’s 
disregard for not only environmental cleanliness but also public safety as the narrator emphasised 
that “the cigarette was still lit with cars moving on the road”.
