"In more than one occasion during my visits to parks and public tourist places, I have noticed 
that many residents, especially in the plains and mountains, throw waste in inappropriate 
places and are too lazy to put it in the right place, even with the simplest thing like paper 
tissues. They just toss them aside, as well as water bottles, which can distort the image 
of the place and lead to the spread of insects, especially if the waste includes leftover food. I 
felt angry and sad because, as a member of this society, I do not want anyone tarnishing 
the reputation of my country or risking turning this clean country into one filled with 
insects. Oman is a country that prioritizes environmental preservation and makes utmost 
efforts to maintain it. So, when someone neglects this concern, it makes me very angry”. 
 
 
In narrative (28) and (29), the narrators describe repeated observations of improper waste disposal 
by visitors in parks and touristic areas, particularly in valleys and mountains. Individuals are seen 
discarding trash in non-designated areas “they are too lazy to put in it the right place”. The behaviour 
includes throwing away “paper tissues... water bottles” according to the narrator, “distort the image 
of the place” and can contribute to the “spread of insects, especially if the waste includes leftover food”. 
This behaviour made the narrator feel “angry and sad” because the narrator “ does not want anyone 
tarnishing the reputation of my country or risking turning this clean country into one filled with 
insects”. The phrase “as a member of this community”, emphasises the narrator’s identification with 
their community and nation, stressing a shared responsibility and collective identity. It highlights the 
belief that individuals’ actions reflect on the community as a whole as the narrator said “ I don’t want 
anyone tarnishing the reputation of my country. The concern about someone “tarnishing the 
reputation of the country”, speaks to a national pride and the importance of maintaining a positive 
image internationally. It reflects a recognition of how environmental care and cleanliness are 
166 
 
essential to the nation’s identity. Also, the narrator acknowledges and praises the country’s effort 
towards environmental preservation “Oman is a country that prioritises environmental preservation”; 
this statement serves to contrast the actions of those who litter against the collective efforts made 
by the country, further emphasising the gravity of the irresponsible behaviour. 
The uncivil acts observed in the above narratives, ranging from traffic misconduct to environmental 
negligence, highlight a critical deviation from the established norm of adhering to traffic regulations 
and respecting public spaces. The observer’s reactions of anger, disgust, and sadness not only 
underscores the personal impact of witnessing such behaviour but also reflects a collective concern 
for the violation of this norm. The observers express a strong emotional responses, like “ I felt 
disgusted” and “ this makes me very angry”, highlighting the deep-seated frustration and 
disappointment in the community’s failure to uphold these standards. 
7.8 Conclusion 
This chapter has explored how participants evaluate everyday uncivil acts based on diary report 
data, revealing that the judgements made in the narratives are not solely grounded in the behaviours 
themselves but in the extent to which they are perceived to violate sociocultural norms. Drawing on 
a diverse range of lived experiences, the analysis demonstrated that incivility was consistently 
assessed in relation to shared norms about appropriate conduct in public spaces. When behaviours 
were seen to undermine these collective understandings, they were judged as uncivil and socially 
unacceptable. 
Participants’ evaluations of incivility was primarily triggered by perceived threats to the social identity 
face of the Omani society, which was clearly evident across the themes presented in this chapter 
(Spencer-Oatey, 2000, 2008). For instance, it was evident in breaches of public decency norms (e.g. 
immodest dress and men’s inappropriate conduct toward women in public), the mistreatment of 
expatriate workers, disregard for road conduct norms (e.g., reckless driving), and neglect of public 
cleanliness (e.g. littering in tourist areas). Their evaluation of incivility was also triggered by unfulfilled 
sociality rights and obligations (Spencer-Oatey, 2000, 2008), specifically expectations related to 
equity rights, which was evident in the treatment of expatriate workers (e.g. shouting at service staff) 
and in road conduct (e.g. overtaking in traffic or taking multiple parking spots). It was also triggered 
by failure to fulfil obligations of care and guidance towards children (e.g. verbal abuse and lack of 
parental guidance), and finally failure to uphold cleanliness (e.g. leaving rubbish in public or natural 
spaces). 
167 
 
Across all of the previously discussed findings, participants did not merely report behaviours they 
perceived as uncivil rather they evaluated those behaviours in relation to deeply held norms and 
expectations. These evaluations were shaped by both concern for the collective image of Omani 
society and a shared understanding of sociality rights and obligations (Spencer-Oatey, 2000, 2008). 
In this case, incivility is socially judged, drawing upon sociocultural norms and expectations. 
168 
 
CHAPTER 8: DISCUSSION 
 
 
8.1 Introduction 
This chapter critically engages with the findings presented in Chapters 4, 5, 6, and 7, drawing insights 
from both phases of the study to reflect on what makes Omani people take offence and what norms 
govern the perception of incivility. The discussion in this chapter situates these findings within the 
broader literature, highlighting areas of alignment as well as contributions that offer new 
perspectives. This chapter is structured thematically around a series of key insights and interpretive 
observations that emerged through close engagement with the data across both phases as well as 
through sustained interaction with the literature throughout the research process. Section 8.2 
introduces how social identity face informed participants’ evaluations of both taking offence and 
incivility. Section 8.2.1 focuses specifically on taking offence, discussing how social identity face 
informed the judgement of offence in relation to the various strands of literature. It then moves to 
Section 8.2.1 which considers how similar face concerns underpin participants’ evaluations of 
incivility. Following this, Section 8.3 discusses taking offence in response to unmet sociocultural 
expectations in the diary report narratives. It is then followed by a discussion in Section 8.4 of how 
power dynamics and social hierarchy influence the perceptions of incivility. Finally, section 8.6 
concludes the chapter 
8.2 Social identity face in the evaluation of offence and incivility 
A key theme emerging from the analysis of X data for both taking offence and incivility is the strong 
concern with how behaviour reflects on the social groups with which individuals identify.  In this 
chapter, social identity face is understood in Spencer-Oatey’s (2007, 2008) relational approach, 
referring to the aspect of face linked to one’s membership in a social group and the expectations 
associated with representing that group.  It captures participants’ desire for their group identities such 
as nationality, religion or community belonging to be respected, acknowledged, and positively 
represented. In this sense, individuals’ actions are not judged in isolation but are evaluated in relation 
to how well they reflect the social norms associated with their group. According to Culpeper (2011), 
the potential for face damage is intricately linked to the degree of sensitivity of the face component 
in question as well as the perceived degree of exposure. Sensitivity refers here to how crucial and 
emotionally significant a particular aspect of face to the evaluator, with more central components 
eliciting stronger reactions to perceived offence or uncivility. This was evident when the values and 
attributes that Omani people claim to have and are expected to uphold were threatened (Culpeper, 
169 
 
2011, Spencer- Oatey, 2007). 
The degree of exposure, on the other hand, pertains to the extent to which these components are 
subject to public scrutiny and potential criticism. As Culpeper (2011) posits, higher exposure in a 
public setting amplifies the potential for face damage. This was evident in X data narratives, where 
social identity face concerns were strongly expressed in both taking offence and incivility, particularly 
when behaviours seen as inappropriate or unrepresentative of Omani identity occurred in highly 
visible contexts. However, in the diary reports, social identity face concerns were primarily associated 
with incivility, given its focus on public spaces, where actions are more visible and subject to greater 
social scrutiny. 
8.2.1 Social identity face in the evaluation of offence 
Findings from X demonstrated that offence was often expressed in response to representations or 
behaviour perceived as misrepresenting Omanis and diminishing their dignity.  Participants oriented 
strongly to social identity face In collectivist societies like Oman, where social identity face, treating 
portrayals of Omanis not merely as depictions of individuals but as reflections of the whole group. 
As a result, misrepresentations or negative portrayals were evaluated as often perceived as direct 
affront/infringement to Omani society as a whole. 
One of the most evident expressions of this concern was seen in responses to an advertisement 
portraying an Omani emerging from a manhole, which was evaluated as “an insult” ( see for section 
4.2 in chapter 4). The reaction was not simply about how one individual was depicted but about the 
negative connotation of the image, which was seen as degrading and misrepresenting Omanis as a 
whole. This pattern was further evident in reactions to a statement by the CEO of Al Jazeera Iron 
Company who claimed that “Omanis are incapable of producing a single gram without foreigners” 
(see section 4.5 in chapter 4). The response framed this remark as a direct violation of “Oman’s 
dignity ”, underscoring that it was perceived as an attack on Omani competence and therefore on 
group identity face. These findings align with recent research on identity positioning on Facebook in 
Tunisia ( Labben, 2022). The study found that Tunisian Facebook users actively responded to identity 
face threats particularly when external representations were perceived as damaging “ the face of the 
country” (p.48). This strong concern for public image of the country mirrors the reaction observed in 
the Omani context. 
Moreover, Omanis did not only take offence at misrepresentation of their own country, but also 
reacted strongly to negative portrayals directed at other Gulf countries, particularly the UAE and Saudi 
170 
 
Arabia (see Chapter 4 examples 9,10 &11). These responses framed offences against 
Gulf countries as offences to Oman as a nation, reflecting how Omanis perceived their social identity 
face as intertwined with the broader Gulf identity. This sentiment was encapsulated in the assertion 
that ‘those who offend them offend us’ and further reinforced by explicit statement ‘ the Gulf countries 
are part of us, and ‘they are our family’. However, Omanis’ strong reactions to perceived 
misrepresentation contrast with findings from Culpeper, J., Marti, L., Mei, M., Nevala, M., and 
Schauer, G. (2010), which examined impoliteness perceptions across England, China, Finland, 
Germany and Turkey. Drawing on Spencer-Oatey’s rapport management framework (2000), the 
findings showed that one of the least prominent categories of offence was social identity face, 
whereas quality face concern emerged as the most significant. This suggests that in these contexts, 
offence was primarily linked to one’s self-worth rather than social identity face. 
This distinction aligns with Ting-Toomey (1988, 2005), arguments that patterns of face concern vary 
across social contexts, with some placing greater emphasis on self-face concerns, making direct 
threats to personal identity more salient. In other contexts, people orient more strongly to social 
identity face, meaning they place higher priority on the self as a member of a group, and they tend 
to view individuals’ negative behaviour as something that reflects on the group (Ting-Toomey, 1994 
& Al Zidjaly, 2012). In the present study, a heightened sensitivity to threats to social identity face was 
evident in the way participants consistently framed behaviour in relation to the groups with which 
they identified. Participants were particularly concerned with how certain actions misrepresented 
“Omanis” or projected a negative image of the country. This pattern was evident in the corpus 
findings, where the frequent use of collective pronouns (we, us, our) and collective nouns (the 
people, the Omanis, the country) underscored that offence was framed in relation to the group rather 
than the individual. This also aligned with Labben’s (2022) corpus linguistic findings, which 
highlighted the intensive use of deictic expressions to speak on behalf of the group, reinforcing how 
concerns about social identity can shape responses to perceived threats     
Offence was also triggered by some of the in-group members whose behaviour was perceived as a 
direct affront to Omani social identity face. This offence stems from the deeply held expectation that 
Omanis should consistently embody and uphold the values and attributes that characterise their 
group members as a whole. These expectations are rooted in the social norms dictating that as a 
member of a particular group community, “ you ought to behave in a particular circumscribed manner” 
( Hogg & Abrams,2007, p.159). Having said that, Barth (1969) maintains that the identification of 
another person as a fellow member of a group implies a mutual understanding of these norms, 
171 
 
essentially suggesting that all members are expected to “ play the same game” (p.15). This was 
evident in multiple instances where Omanis were criticised for behaviour perceived as not aligning 
with “ the authentic Omani”. One such case involved an Omani who used offensive language toward 
a Bangladeshi person during a match ( see excerpt 8). The backlash was not merely about the 
language but about what it symbolised, a failure to embody the implicit ideals expected of an Omani. 
This criticism was framed in terms of a violation of in-group norms, reinforcing the idea that being an 
“authentic Omani” entails adherence to specific behavioural expectations. This mutual understanding 
is not merely about adhering to norms, but also about embodying the prototype of the ‘authentic 
Omani’, which serves as a representation of the ideal or most typical group member ( Hogg, 2005). 
That is, their beliefs, values, and behaviours are strongly aligned with group norms ( McGarty, Turner, 
Hogg, David and Wetherell, 1992). The data analysis revealed that participants assume, often 
without explicitly stating it, that every member of the group inherently understands what constitutes 
the “ authentic Omani”. This taken for granted assumption is characteristic of social norms, which as 
Bicchieri (2006) argues, operate implicitly and only become visible when violated. This was reflected 
when participants labelled certain behaviours as “against Omani values” or “doesn’t represent us as 
Omanis”, without further clarification, suggesting unspoken agreement about what is deemed 
acceptable within Omani society. 
Embedded within this understanding of the ‘authentic Omani’ are implicit moral attributes that are 
perceived as inherent to the group’s identity, shaping both individual behaviour and collective 
belonging. The moral attributes that participants alluded to, whether implicitly or explicitly, revolved 
around fundamental values such as respect, fairness, and dignity. According to Giner-Sorolla (2012) 
when people agree on key values that characterises their group, these values function as a moral 
standard that defines group identity and guides the social expectations. Rather than treating these 
values as universal moral principles (Haidt, 2001), they are framed as group-specific moral 
standards that differentiate their group members from others. This was evident in participants’ 
tweets, where morality was explicitly linked to group belonging (see Chapter 4 examples 8,9 &10). 
In these instances, morality was invoked as an essential aspect of being an ‘ authentic Omani’, they 
used moral discourse to assert ingroup distinctiveness, framing certain behaviours as either aligning 
with or violating shared moral expectations. For example, statements such ‘ an action that lacks the 
most basic morals we were raised with as Omanis’ and ‘Oman and its people are a school of noble 
morals’ highlight how morality functions as part of the sociocultural norms and expectations. 
 
172 
 
8.2.2 Social identity face in the evaluation of incivility 
A similar pattern emerged in the evaluation of incivility, where individuals perceived uncivil behaviour 
as threats to social identity face (Spencer- Oatey, 2007, 2000). Across various uncivil acts, including 
littering, inappropriate dress, spitting and reckless driving, participants judged these behaviours not 
merely as individual misconduct but as violations of social norms that define and maintain Omani 
identity. The significance of these acts stemmed from how they contradicted the social norms 
regulating how Omanis should present themselves in public, embodying the values and attributes 
that define their group identity, and illustrating that identity is not merely possessed but performed 
and regulated through social evaluation (Goffman, 1967, Kádár & Haugh, 2013). 
Concerns about social identity face were particularly evident in discussions about littering in public 
spaces. In the X data and diary report, Omanis did not merely see littering as an issue of 
environmental irresponsibility ( see Chapter 6 excerpts 8,9 & 10 ) or personal negligence but as a 
behaviour that tarnishes the image of the nation (see Chapter 6 excerpts 5,6 &7 and Chapter 7 
excerpt 29). Participants frequently framed littering as a practice that negatively reflects on the place 
due to an individual’s action reinforcing the notion that individual actions contribute to the collective 
reputation of the country. Similarly, one respondent questioned, “why are we presenting an 
uncivilised image of the region?” underscoring the broader concern that such behaviours distort the 
image of the region. 
This perception aligns with broader discourse on incivility, where actions that disrupt social order are 
not only condemned for their immediate effect, but also their symbolic implications. The act of 
littering, like many other uncivil behaviours analysed in chapter 6 and 7, was not merely seen as a 
failure to uphold the value of cleanliness that Omanis collectively endorse but also an act that tarnishes 
the nation’s image, threatening the social identity face of Omanis. As one participant articulated this 
sentiment explicitly, stating “I do not want anyone to tarnish the reputation of my country”, reinforcing 
the idea that incivility threatens the national identity. This was especially apparent in highly visible 
locations such as tourist destinations, where exposure heightens the potential for identity face threat 
(Culpeper, 2011). The presence of litter in public parks, valleys and beaches, frequented by both 
locals and visitors from outside the country, reinforced concerns about how such behaviour reflected 
on the nation’s image. The presence of foreigners or visitors, in these spaces further exacerbated the 
perceived inappropriateness of the behaviour, as it gives a negative image of the country and its 
people. 
This dynamic closely parallels Zhang, Pearce, and Chen’s (2019) study on Chinese tourists and 
173 
 
uncivilised behaviour, which found that fear of “losing collective face” motivated Chinese travellers 
to adopt more “civilised” behaviours when travelling abroad. Zhang et al. (2019) noted that some 
Chinese travellers would actively correct or reprimand fellow tourists who engaged in behaviours 
perceived as inappropriate such as littering, or speaking too loudly, or disregarding local etiquette 
because they feared that such acts would reinforce negative stereotypes about the Chinese as a 
whole. 
Another concern that was frequently narrated was wearing immodest or inappropriate clothing in 
public, which was seen as directly violating the norms of modesty that define acceptable public 
appearance and that are tied to the social identity face ( see Chapter 7, excerpts 2,3 & 4). Participants 
framed these violations as a threat to group identity face, as one stated “it gives bad impression of 
Omani people”. The disapproval of immodest attire stemmed from its perceived misalignment with 
social norms that dictate modest, and appropriate public attire. 
In several excerpts, immodesty and other uncivil acts such as littering were framed through a 
religious lens (see Chapter 7 excerpt 4 and Chapter 6 excerpts 1,2,3 & 4), as one participant noted 
“it does not reflect our religion” and in another excerpt “we are people of religion and cleanliness”. 
This suggests that some evaluation of social norms stemmed directly from religious beliefs that 
regulate behaviour, shaping what is considered acceptable (Durkheim, 1916). In Muslim societies 
like Oman, religion plays a significant role in shaping social norms, providing a framework for what 
‘ought to be’ performed, influencing how people behave in public life However, not all participants 
invoked religion when discussing modesty and other uncivil acts. Instead, their disapproval stemmed 
from the act deviating from social values, tradition, and moral attributes that they uphold as a group. 
Nevertheless, even when participants did not explicitly invoke religion, the values they alluded to were 
inherently rooted in religious teachings, demonstrating how religious principles continue to shape 
social norms, even when framed through a cultural or identity-based lens. 
While adhering to modest attire was considered important, wearing traditional clothing while 
behaving in a way that deviates from cultural expectations was perceived as a more serious violation 
of social norms. Traditional attire, such as the dishdasha and khanjar, is a visual marker of Omani 
identity, but its significance extends beyond appearance; it carries implicit expectations of conduct 
(Watzlawik, 2012). To wear these symbols is to embody the behaviours and values they represent, 
making actions that contradict this expectation particularly, threatening to the group identity face 
(Spencer- Oatey, 2007). These symbols “are not significant in themselves but derive meaning from 
the ways in which individuals within a culture ascribe meaning to them (Banks, 2011). That is, 
174 
 
traditional dress is more than an external expression of identity; it is a social contract that requires 
the wearer to uphold certain behaviours. This was evident in the public disapproval of individuals 
who wore these symbols while engaging in behaviour deemed “inappropriate” and “uncivil”. For 
instance, young men wearing the the khanjar (dagger) while dancing in an “inappropriate way” 
(see excerpt 12) faced strong criticism. The disapproval was not just about the act of dancing but 
about the perceived misuse of Khanjar in a context that contradicted how some believed it should 
be used. The Khanjar is considered a national symbol (Al Busaidi, 2014) representing pride, 
masculinity and identity (Althagafi, 2022). By engaging in behaviour seen as “ الئق غير” inappropriate, 
the individual was perceived as misrepresenting what the Khanjar stands for. The reaction “honestly, 
this does not please me, when will they stop?”, highlights the belief that wearing the khanjar carries 
implicit expectations of appropriate conduct. 
As a national and identity symbol, those who wear it are expected to embody the qualities it signifies. 
When behaviour contradicts this expectation, it is not viewed as an individual act but as something 
that reflects on the larger group, leading to public disapproval as a way to uphold its meaning. Similarly, 
an Omani was condemned for publicly eating and drinking during Ramadan while wearing a 
dishdasha (see excerpt 13). The criticism was not just about failing to fulfil a religious obligation but 
about the contradiction between the symbolic significance of the dishdasha and the behaviour that 
undermined its expected meaning. By wearing it, the individual implicitly declared himself  to be part 
of Omani community, which in turn implied adherence to the values it represents, including religious 
observance. When this alignment is broken, it is seen as violating a religious norm that is embedded 
in Omani identity. 
8.3 Taking offence in the diary report due to unmet expectations 
While the previous discussion of X data and diary report on incivility highlighted how social group 
identity face influences perceptions of offence (Spencer-Oatey, 2007), the diary report findings also 
revealed that taking offence can stem from violations of more personal expectations in various 
interactions. These expectations are not linked to collective identity, i.e. self in relation to the group 
(Spencer-Oatey, 2000), but rather linked to self in relation to the individua himself/ herself, and in 
some cases linked to the self in context on ongoing relationships. The diary report findings revealed 
six types of expectations that when violated led participants to report feelings of being offended. 
These included expectation related to quality face (being recognised for their competence), 
interpersonal expectations (appropriate behaviour based on relational history), equity (fair and 
considerate treatment), reciprocity (the expectation that one’s action will be met with a corresponding 
175 
 
or appropriate response), deference (respect based on social role) and association (inclusion, and 
self- expression in social interactions) were violated (Spencer-Oatey,2005,2007,2008;Culpeper, 
2011;Shils, 1982). Among these I will discuss two in detail: interpersonal expectations and 
reciprocity expectations. Notably, interpersonal expectations extend into reciprocity, as individuals 
expect that the dynamic of their relationship will involve mutual exchanges and balanced responses 
(Spencer-Oatey, 2008; Gouldner, 1960). 
Starting with interpersonal expectations, the findings indicate that one of the most significant sources 
of offence stems from relational history. Participants anticipated a certain level of appropriate 
behaviour based on prior interactions and the nature of the nature of their relationships (see Chapter 
4, examples 4 & 5). When these expectations were violated, participants perceived the offence as 
a failure to uphold interpersonal obligations, particularly in relationships where mutual understanding 
was assumed. In this regard, Kádár and Haugh (2013) maintain that in closed relational networks, 
conventions are often evoked by relational history, meaning that patterns of interaction develop over 
time and establish implicit expectations for behaviour within the relationship. In these networks, 
members repeatedly engage with each other in ways that reinforce shared norms, making violations 
of these norms particularly salient. This aligns with the findings in the present study, where 
participants took offence not only because of what was done or said but because of who said it. For 
instance, offence was reported when participant experienced insult and betrayal from individuals 
with whom they shared relational history. This was evident in statements like ‘this insult came from 
someone close’ and ‘someone you trust and consider a sister’. These examples highlight that 
perceived offence is deeply tied to expectations embedded within relationships. In the participant 
accounts, the perceived betrayal was amplified because the offence came from a person she trusted, 
reinforcing how relational history shapes evaluations of offence. 
 
Tayebi (2016) similarly found in her study, on Persian speakers, that offence frequently arose from 
violations of implicit relational expectations shaped by their relational histories. Participants reported 
feeling offended when family members, friends, or partners behaved in ways that contradicted the 
expectation formed in the context of that relationship. These expectations typically remain unnoticed 
when they are met, as they align seamlessly with the established relational norms, but become highly 
visible when breached, triggering offence (Kádár and Haugh, 2013). This highlights how perceptions 
of offence are deeply embedded within relational contexts and are significantly influenced by the 
implicit norms developed over time. 
176 
 
Closely connected to these interpersonal expectations were the expectations of reciprocity, referring 
to the expectations that one’s actions will be matched by an appropriate or corresponding response 
(see Chapter 4, examples 8, 9 & 10). The analysis indicated that reciprocity expectations often 
overlapped with interpersonal expectations, as relationships inherently involve mutual exchange and 
shared obligations (Ohashi & Chang, 2017; Gouldner, 1960). An offence was reported when a 
reciprocal expectation tied to a prior act of service was met with ingratitude or disregard. For 
instance, one participant took offence when a favour intended as an act of care was misinterpreted 
as harm, stating: ‘I wanted to do a favour to my grandmother’. Another participant described feeling 
deeply offended when a person they had previously helped later offended them publicly on social 
media, stating ‘I felt so angry because I helped this person before’. Both cases illustrate that the 
offence stemmed from the absence of reciprocity, which is strongly tied to the relational context. In 
the case of the grandmother, the expectation of acknowledgment was rooted in an established 
relationship, reinforced by previous interactions, where familial bonds typically assume mutual 
understanding (Spencer-Oatey, 2008). 
In contrast, the social media example reflects an expectation of reciprocity based on a prior 
interaction, where a past act of support established an implicit social obligation that was later violated 
(Gouldner, 1960). However, example 8 ( see Chapter 5, section 5.6) highlights a different type of 
reciprocity expectation, one that is not based on prior relationships or interaction but on immediate, 
‘here and now’ reciprocity expectation (Tantucci,Wang, and Culpeper, 2022). In this case, the 
participant extended help to a stranger in real-time, anticipating acknowledgement or gratitude in 
return. The offence arose when this expectation was met with hostility instead of recognition, violating 
an assumed norm of immediate reciprocity in social exchanges (Gouldner, 1960; Trivers, 1971). This 
demonstrates that reciprocity violations do not only emerge from past relational histories but also 
from momentary interactions where individuals expect their act to be reciprocated within the same 
exchange rather than future repayment (Culpeper & Tantucci, 2021; Tantucci,Wang, and Culpeper, 
2022). 
8.4 Power and the perception of incivility and offence  
The analysis in chapter 7 (see examples 14,15,16,17,18 & 19). demonstrated that incivility is 
particularly salient when power is exercised in ways that violate equity norms. While impoliteness 
can be legitimised within certain power structures, people do not necessarily render it socially 
acceptable (Bousfiled, 2008; Locher & Watts, 2005, Culpeper, 2011). Many participants in the study 
evaluated certain interactions as uncivil when they perceived a misuse of power that disregarded 
fundamental expectations of fairness and consideration (Spencer-Oatey, 2002,2005), particularly in 
177 
 
encounters between Omanis and foreign workers. These perceptions were especially pronounced 
in interactions between customers and service employees in coffee shops and supermarkets, as well 
as between students and instructors during driving lessons. All of these interactions come with 
inherent power imbalance (e.g. worker/employee). Beyond the focal event, Omanis occupied a 
structurally advantaged position, not only due to their unequal role relations in the immediate 
interaction (Spencer-Oatey, 2000), but also because of broader socioeconomic factors, such as 
citizenship status, economic stability, and societal privilege (Goodwin and Duranti, 1992). In contrast, 
foreign workers often held lower social standing, occupying subordinate positions with limited agency 
in day-to-day life. This broader context shaped how participants evaluated the act as uncivil and 
inappropriate. One participant acknowledged the difficult conditions of foreign workers, remarking 
that ‘they came from their country and work hard to earn a living’. Such a comment reflects an 
awareness of systemic inequalities, which in turn influenced how participants judged acts of 
unfairness or inconsideration. Although the power imbalance was rarely made explicit, it was often 
assumed rather than explicitly acknowledged, yet it structured the interactions and shaped 
participants’ judgements of incivility (Locher & Watts, 2005). 
This was evident in various incidents where customers and driving instructors mistreated the foreign 
worker by yelling at them, speaking to them harshly, or even resorting to physical violence (see 
chapter 7, examples 14,15,16,17,18 & 19). Participants reacted with anger, frustration, and moral 
condemnation, emphasising the unfairness of such treatment, particularly given the disadvantages 
faced by foreign workers. Wartenberg (1990) maintains that power is exercised when one social 
agent strategically constrains another’s action-environment. However, as the examples illustrated, 
such constraints are not always deliberate but instead emerge through established power structures. 
These structural limitations restrict foreign workers’ ability to challenge mistreatment, while 
simultaneously granting those in privileged positions greater freedom to exercise their power over 
them (Locher & Watts, 2005). Incivility is therefore most likely to be manifested by the powerful to 
the powerless, because it has few consequences (Lakoff, 2005). This asymmetry in these 
interactions reinforced an unspoken power imbalance, limiting the ability of foreign workers to 
respond to mistreatment. 
Participants’ reactions suggest an awareness of these implicit constraints, particularly when power 
was used in ways that violated expectations of fairness and respectful treatment. In response to such 
incidents, participants articulated what they believed an instructor or customer ought to do in 
interactions with foreign workers (Spencer-Oatey, Kádár, 2021), for example, one participant said, 
178 
 
‘you should help him and extend a helping hand and respect him’. By articulating what ought to be 
done, the participants implicitly acknowledged the power asymmetry in these interactions. Their 
responses suggest that considerate treatment, fairness and respect are obligations expected from 
those in dominant positions. 
In addition to the ways power dynamics involving people in vulnerable positions shaped particpant’s 
evaluation, judgements of offence and incivility were also shaped by a broader social context in 
which men hold greater power in the society. In Oman, as in much of the region, men have historically 
held more power in the society, and this influenced how some male participants judged what they 
considered offensive or inappropriate. This context is particularly relevant in this study given that the 
online dataset was predominantly contributed by men, which inevitably shaped the evaluative 
patterns observed. Within this gendered power structure, taking offence can intertwine with 
questions of authority, where what is perceived as offensive may in fact signal a perceived challenge 
to one’s position of power. For instance, when male participants expressed feeling offended by 
women’s dress or public conduct, their reactions may be understood not only as violation to 
sociocultural norms such as breach of modesty expectations, but also as an indication that their 
sense of authority and power felt undermined. In this sense offence or incivility function as response 
to a perceived threat to one’s social authority. Acknowledging these gendered dynamics is important 
in this research, as they shape how participants interpret and evaluate behaviour, and therefore 
influence the patterns that emerge in the findings. These insights also point to the need for future 
research that examines taking offence and incivility from the perspective of women and marginalised 
groups in the society, whose experiences may bring different evaluative patterns.  
8.5 Conclusion 
This chapter has brought together findings from both phases of the study along with findings from 
other studies to provide an integrated discussion of the sociocultural norms and expectations that 
ground the people’s judgement of offence and incivility in the Omani society. It highlighted the central 
role of social identity face in shaping the judgement, particularly in response to behaviour that 
threatens the image of Omanis. The discussion showed that taking offence in X was mainly tied to 
group identity, while the diary reports revealed that offence was often rooted in personal expectations. 
Whereas incivility in both X data and diary reports was tied to breach of public norms that are tied to 
religion, social identity face, and shared responsibility. It also discussed incivility and taking offence 
in relation to power, showing that many behaviours perceived as uncivil were evaluated through the 
lens of structural and interactional asymmetries.  
179 
 
CHAPTER 9: CONCLUSION 
 
9.1 Introduction 
This study offered an in-depth investigation into what causes Omani people to take offence and the 
norms that govern the perception of incivility within Omani society. By focusing on norms and 
expectations perceived to be violated, the research examined how individuals evaluate behaviour 
as offensive and uncivil in both X (previously known as Twitter) and diary reports. 
The current chapter aims to revisit the key findings in relation to the two research questions (see 
Section 8.2). It then reflects on the study’s limitations (see Section 8.5) and proposes directions for 
future research. 
9.2 Key Findings of the Research 
Having presented the findings in detail in Chapters 4, 5, 6, and 7, this section synthesises the key 
insights that emerged from both phases of the study. It draws together the results of the corpus-based 
analysis and thematic analysis of X data and the diary report narratives. The findings are organised 
in relation to the two central research questions, which focus on the role of violated norms and 
expectations in shaping Omani individuals’ evaluations. 
9.2.1 Understanding what causes Omani people to take offence in Omani society 
The first research question aimed to explore what causes people in the Omani society to take 
offence. As outlined in earlier chapters (see Chapters 1, 2, and 4), this study adopts an expectation 
and norm-based approach to offence. It draws on Parvaresh’s (2022) definition of taking offence, 
which was adapted from Culpeper (2011) and Haugh (2015). Parvaresh defines offence as 
“emotionally bad feeling created by what is perceived to be a transgression of (social) expectations 
and (moral) norms” (p.62). In this study, I adopt Parvaresh’s definition but focus specifically on social 
expectations and norms rather than (moral) norms. As argued earlier in Chapter 1, Section 1.2, 
recent research on impoliteness has increasingly highlighted morality-anchored evaluations of 
offence. However, the data in this study show that even when morality is evoked, it is based on 
locally shared values tied to Omani culture and identity, rather than universal moral principles. In 
other words, participants’ references to morality were tied to what it means to be Omani, that is, to 
the shared sociocultural norms that shape expectations of appropriate behaviour. 
Taking offence is therefore not simply “a bad feeling” but a socially grounded judgement involving 
180 
 
both emotional expression and evaluative justification. This was evident across the corpus   
and thematic analyses of X data, as well as the thematic analysis of the diary report narratives, which 
consistently demonstrated that offence involved both registering and sanctioning (Culpeper, 2011; 
Haugh, 2015). Registering offence refers to the expression of a negative emotional response, such 
as when respondents said, ‘I felt bad’ and ‘I felt embarrassed’ when a transgression is perceived. 
Sanctioning offence, on the other hand, is “a process by which interactants ground their moral claim 
of prior transgression, affront or misdeed” (Haugh, 2015, p.38). In other words, while registering 
reflects how participants felt, sanctioning reflects why they believed their feelings were justified. 
Based on the data, both processes were often present, but not always equally explicit. In some 
cases, particularly in the diary reports, participants registered taking offence without clearly 
sanctioning the behaviour. The emotional reaction was evident, but the specific social or cultural 
norm being violated was left implicit or assumed rather than directly stated. 
In the X data, offence was often framed as a group concern, with users employing inclusive terms such 
as everyone, Omanis, or citizens to suggest that the offence impacted the wider social group, not 
just the individual. This collective framing was reinforced through emotionally charged language that 
registered offence on behalf of the group. Phrases like ‘Isn’t this an insult to the country and its 
people’ and ‘This is the greatest insult to the Omani people’ illustrate this pattern. Most of the 
expressions of registering offence in X data were accompanied by sanctioning, where the users 
justified their reaction by referring to violated norms and expectations. These norms were closely tied 
to concerns about how Omanis and their national and regional identity were portrayed in public 
discourse. A recurring pattern in the data showed that users expressed offence when representations 
of Oman and Omanis were seen as inaccurate, disrespectful, or undermining the image of the country. 
This included not only portrayals by outsiders but also instances where fellow Omanis were perceived 
as behaving in ways that misrepresented the group. This concern reflected what Spencer-Oatey 
(2002, 2005) refers to as social identity face, where individuals are concerned with how their group is 
represented and evaluated in public discourse. In this context, taking offence functioned as a form 
of public sanctioning, used to call out behaviour or discourse that contradicted how Omanis believe 
their group should be portrayed. 
While X data demonstrated how offence was constructed as a collective judgement tied to public 
portrayals of group identity face (Spencer-Oatey 2002 and 2005), the diary report narratives revealed 
how individuals took offence in more personal and relational contexts, often grounded in implicit 
social expectations. Participants registered offence using phrases such as ‘I felt sad’, ‘I was angry’, 
181 
 
‘I was frustrated’, and ‘I felt ashamed and embarrassed’. These emotional responses were triggered 
by perceived violations of a range of expectations, such as the expectation to be treated fairly and 
with consideration, to be recognised for one’s competence, and to have one’s act of ‘favour’ 
appreciated, to be included and allowed to express oneself, to receive appropriate behaviour from 
others based on one’s institutional role and to be treated in ways that align with expectations typically 
associated with close relationships. These expectations shaped how participants judged the 
situations they found offensive, functioning as implicit norms against which behaviour was evaluated 
as offensive. 
9.2.2 Understanding the norms that govern the perceptions of incivility 
The second research question aimed to explore the norms that govern how incivility is perceived and 
evaluated in Omani society. As with the analysis of taking offence, the study of incivility adopts an 
evaluative perspective, focusing on how participants interpret certain behaviours as uncivil in light of 
shared sociocultural norms and expectations. Drawing on insights from a range of disciplines (see 
Chapter 3, Section 2.7)  including criminology, organisational psychology, and political science, and 
especially sociological studies (Phillips & Smith, 2006; Horgan, 2020, 2024) and (im)politeness 
research (Sifianou, 2019; Haugh, 2022), this study defined incivility as a perceived transgression of 
sociocultural norms that govern appropriate conduct in public places, often marked by emotionally 
charged reactions. 
From this perspective, incivility is not understood as a set of behaviours that are inherently 
inappropriate or uncivil but as actions that are interpreted as violating implicit or explicit rules that 
structure everyday life in public settings. What constitutes incivility, therefore, is not the act itself, but 
the meaning assigned to it by the participants within a specific context. This was evident in the 
commenters’ evaluations. To explain, the commenters across the two corpora, those built using the 
hashtags #uncivil and # inappropriate, often referred to the same set of behaviours as uncivil. 
However, the grounds they appealed to in order to legitimise their claims differed, reflecting a variety 
of sociocultural norms individuals drew on. For instance, the act of ‘throwing rubbish’ was one of the 
most mentioned behaviours, but the basis for judging it as uncivil varied across the comments. Some 
participants, for example, drew on religious grounds, referencing Islamic teachings such as 
‘cleanliness is part of faith’ and ‘we are people of religion and cleanliness to argue that such behaviour 
reflected a failure to uphold religious obligations. Others evaluated it based on how the behaviour 
affected the public image of the area, particularly in contexts that involve outsiders such as ‘tourists’ 
or ‘visitors. This was apparent when some X users used phrases like ‘it reflects negatively on the 
182 
 
place’ and ‘why are we conveying an uncivilised image of the region?’ framing it as threatening the 
social identity face of the community. Furthermore, some participants based their evaluations on an 
assumed shared societal obligation for individuals within a group to maintain the cleanliness of public 
spaces. This perspective positioned incivility as a failure to uphold communal responsibility for 
maintaining cleanliness. It was evident in the use of phrases like ‘weak societal reasonability’ and 
“this is not an excuse to litter in the area. Be responsible”. The analysis thus highlights how a single 
behaviour can give rise to multiple interpretations depending on the normative lens through which it is 
assessed. 
Additionally, ‘inappropriate dress’ was also frequently mentioned as uncivil and inappropriate. 
However, as with the ‘littering’, the grounds on which X users based their evaluation varied. To 
explain, in some instances, the judgements were rooted in context-specific conventions. For example, 
some commenters criticised individuals for wearing casual clothing in formal settings such as 
‘government departments’ where wearing formal attire, specifically the ‘dishdasha’ is explicitly 
prescribed. Nevertheless, evaluations based on explicit norms were not the only basis for judging 
attire as uncivil. In other cases, the participants evaluated the attire in relation to implicit sociocultural 
norms that are not codified. For example, some commenters criticised individuals for ‘wearing 
pyjamas’ in mosques and shopping malls. While there are no formal dress codes in these contexts, 
such attire was perceived as inappropriate because it conflicted with widely shared expectations 
about how one should present oneself in public. In both cases, the attire was not inherently 
inappropriate but became so through its perceived misalignment with the explicit and implicit norms 
governing appropriate appearance in particular public settings. 
Beyond the norms tied to specific settings, certain behaviours were evaluated as uncivil because 
they were performed by individuals who were visibly marked as Omani, either through national dress 
or other cultural symbols. To explain, acts such as eating during Ramadan and dancing in a video, 
or producing content perceived as inappropriate content perceived as inappropriate were frequently 
labelled ‘inappropriate’ when performed by individuals identified as ‘Omani’, or visibly wearing attire 
that represented Omanis. In all these cases, the judgement was based on the perceived mismatch 
between the identity signalled by the individual’s appearance and the behaviour they engaged in. X 
users alluded to visible identity markers such as the ‘dishdasha’ or ‘khanjar’ as part of the grounds 
for their evaluations, suggesting that those who display such identity-related symbols are expected 
to uphold the sociocultural norms associated with them. In other words, wearing these symbols, 
‘dishdasha’ or ‘khanjar’, carries an obligation to behave in ways that align with behavioural norms 
tied to Omani identity. Consequently, when the behaviour failed to meet the expectations tied to this 
183 
 
identity, it was seen as a violation that threatens the image of the wider community. Incivility in this 
context was constructed not just through behavioural transgression, but through the social meaning 
assigned to the actor’s identity and the symbolic significance of their appearance. 
In addition to insights drawn from X data, the diary report findings confirmed the central argument of 
this study that incivility is not defined by the act itself, but by how individuals’ evaluated behaviours 
as transgressions of shared sociocultural norms. They also expanded the scope of analysis by 
introducing behaviours that are not present in the X data, such as beating expatriate workers, 
harassing women, taking up more than one parking space, among several others. At the same time, 
some of the concerns raised in the diary report echoed those seen in the X data, such as littering 
and attire-related concerns. However, despite these differences in the types of acts described by 
participants across both datasets, X and diary report consistently anchored their evaluations in 
similar sociocultural norms. Most notably, many judgements were underpinned by concerns for 
social identity face, which refers in this context to the desire for individuals within a group to see their 
collective identity positively represented (Spencer-Oatey, 2000). This concern was evident across a 
wide range of behaviours, such as littering, dressing immodestly, or ‘disrespecting the seller’. The 
evaluations of the participants in all of these behaviours centered around the failure to act in 
accordance with how Omanis view themselves to be, and how they want others to view them. For 
example, littering in tourist areas was said to give a negative image of the area to outsiders. Likewise, 
women’s immodest attire was said not to “reflect a good image of Omani society”, and disrespecting 
expatriate workers was said to give a negative impression of Omanis. 
While the evaluation of the previously mentioned behaviour was often grounded in concerns for 
social identity face, the same behaviours were also interpreted through a different sociocultural norm. 
For instance, the mistreatment of expatriate workers was evaluated by some participants as a 
violation of equity rights, that is, the expectation to treat others with fairness and consideration, 
particularly, in this context, those in vulnerable positions such as ‘being away from their country’ and 
‘not understanding Arabic’. Moreover, dressing immodestly was also framed as a failure to uphold 
religious norms. These examples demonstrate that behaviours judged as uncivil and/or inappropriate 
were not judged solely based on the act itself, but on the underlying sociocultural norms and 
expectations that participants drew on to make sense of and evaluate the behaviour. These norms 
guided what was seen as uncivil or inappropriate and varied depending on what each participant 
prioritised. 
184 
 
To sum up, the findings from both X data and diary reports clearly demonstrate that perceptions of 
incivility in the Omani context are grounded in sociocultural norms and expectations. Across both 
datasets, participants consistently evaluated the behaviour based on whether it aligned with what 
they believed to be acceptable conduct in a given context. These evaluations were informed by 
perceived breaches of shared norms. Based on the findings, acts such as littering, dressing 
immodestly, shouting at service workers, or eating during Ramadan, and more were among the 
behaviours they considered uncivil. Nevertheless, the judgement of the exact behaviour on many 
occasions differed among participants depending on which sociocultural norms they perceived to be 
violated. The norms they alluded in legitimising their claims ranged from religious obligations, 
communal responsibility, and fairness, to most dominantly, appeals to social identity face. This 
underscores that incivility, as perceived, is shaped by the sociocultural norms and expectations it is 
seen to violate. 
Having summarised the key findings for the two research questions, the following section discusses 
the contribution of this research 
9.3 Contribution of the study 
This study has significantly contributed to the field of (im)politeness empirically, theoretically and 
methodologically. Empirically, it broadens our understanding of taking offence and incivility by 
investigating the sociocultural norms and expectations that underlie their evaluations. By focusing 
on Omani society, this research offers valuable insights from a context that has received limited 
scholarly attention within (im)politeness studies. Thus, it contributes to broadening the empirical base 
of the field by extending its focus beyond its traditional emphasis on Western and East Asian 
contexts. In addition to broadening the geographical scope of (im)politeness research, this study 
sheds light on the specific norms and expectations that shape the evaluation of offence and incivility 
within Omani society, particularly the central role of social identity face in informing the judgements 
about inappropriate and/or offensive behaviour. In line with this dominant finding, the study 
demonstrated that responses to perceived offence and incivility function as acts of defending 
collective identity and reaffirming the shared cultural ideals that define what it means to be Omani. 
Furthermore, this study moves beyond the emerging trend in both (im)politeness research and 
sociological studies of incivility, which frames the evaluation of offence and incivility as moral 
transgressions. It demonstrates that in the Omani context, the evaluation of these two phenomena is 
heavily grounded in sociocultural norms and expectations. It has also shown that while a moral lens 
185 
 
was occasionally evoked in the evaluation of both taking offence and incivility, it  was framed through 
culturally embedded expectations, specifically, tied to ideals of how an Omani should act. 
Additionally, this research brings into the foreground incivility, an underexplored concept and 
phenomenon in impoliteness studies. Until now, incivility has not been a central focus within 
(im)politeness research. This research has demonstrated that incivility warrants the same level of 
scholarly attention as any other social phenomenon on which impoliteness research has focused. It 
has been shown that the evaluations of incivility are grounded in perceived transgressions of the 
moral order, that is, in the violation of shared sociocultural norms and expectations that structure 
social life. Moreover, this study also demonstrated that when individuals post online about perceived 
acts of incivility, whether through pictures, video, or descriptions, they are not merely reporting 
incidents but are actively framing these behaviours as violations of the shared moral order. 
This study also makes a theoretical contribution by extending the application of the rapport 
management framework to the analysis of offence and incivility within the Omani context (Spencer- 
Oatey, 2000 & 2008). While this framework has been previously applied to studies of taking offence 
(e.g., Culpeper et al., 2010), earlier applications focused only on interpersonal interactions within 
specific cultural contexts. Moreover, an element of this framework has been applied briefly in the 
Omani context by Spencer-Oately & Kadar (2021), who illustrated an instance of sociality rights and 
obligations through an example involving dress code expectations in a professional governmental 
setting. However, their use of the framework remained illustrative and focused only on one 
interactional incident. Therefore, this is the first study that applies this framework systematically in 
the Omani context. 
Moreover, another novel contribution in this study is the use of this framework to investigate incivility. 
To the best of my knowledge, no study to date has systematically applied rapport management 
rapport model to the investigation of incivility. This application extends the framework beyond its 
traditional usage to the evaluation of breaches of public conduct in public places. 
This study has also contributed methodologically to the field by employing two distinct but 
complementary data collection methods: online data from X and diary report narratives. These two 
methods worked together to give a comprehensive understanding of taking offence and incivility in 
Oman. While recent research on taking offence has relied heavily on online available data such as 
social media posts, as I demonstrated in the literature review, it has often overlooked methods that 
allow for deeper engagement with how individuals make sense of offensive encounters in their 
186 
 
everyday lives. In response to this gap, this study combined discourse drawn from online public 
platforms and personal narratives captured through diary reports, a combination that is rarely used 
in (im)politeness research. The findings revealed a key insight in the expression of taking offence; 
the way offence is articulated and evaluated publicly in X differs significantly from how it is described 
and evaluated in private accounts of diary reports. To explain briefly, offence in X was framed as a 
collective concern shaped by unmet expectations related to group identity face. In contrast, the diary 
report narratives revealed offence as a more personal experience tied to unmet expectations of 
deference based on social role, and reciprocity shaped by either long-term relational history or 
momentary social exchanges. This contrast highlights how the expression and evaluation of offence 
shift depending on the tool used to collect data. This finding has important implications for the study 
of taking offence and broader research on impoliteness. It highlights the limitations of relying only on 
social media data to understand how taking offence is expressed and evaluated, as such platforms 
have been shown to reflect only part of the overall picture. For (im)politeness research more broadly, 
the findings emphasise the importance of using both publicly available sources of data alongside 
more private forms of data collection, such as diary reports or interviews, when studying any social 
phenomenon, since the medium of communication can influence or even shape the kinds of narratives 
individuals share. 
While this study has made several significant empirical, theoretical, and methodological contributions 
to the field of impoliteness, it is important to acknowledge its limitations. Nevertheless, the presence 
of limitations dose not undermine the value of this research but rather highlights areas where future 
research is needed to build upon and extend the insights developed in this research. The following 
section outlines the limitations of the study and suggests directions for future research. 
9.4 Limitations of the study and future development 
This study has tackled two areas that remain underexplored, not only within the Omani context but 
also within the broader field of (Im)politeness research: taking offence and incivility. It investigated 
these two topics not only as concepts but also as lived experiences. Drawing on both X data and 
diary report data, the study offers an in-depth account of how individuals in Omani society interpret 
and judge behaviours that they perceive as offensive or uncivil. While this study offers important 
contributions, several limitations must be acknowledged. 
The first limitation is in terms of the methods used to collect data. The study used both social media 
posts and diary reports to explore the perceptions of offence and incivility; each method presented 
certain limitations. The social media data collected from X offered access to large volumes of 
187 
 
naturally occurring discourse; however, it only reflected the views of specific segments of the 
population, mainly those who use X and are accustomed to engaging in public discourse. X is not 
among the widely used platforms in Oman; therefore, using X might have limited the diversity of 
perspectives represented in the data. This limitation was addressed through the inclusion of diary 
reports, which captured a wider range of voices and experiences, particularly from individuals who 
may not typically participate on the online platform. Nevertheless, future research could still make 
use of social media as a data source. It is recommended, however, to consider more widely adopted 
platforms such as Instagram, which are used across different age groups in Oman and across 
different social backgrounds. 
A further limitation in this research is the demographic composition of the social media dataset. 
Existing research on social media usage in Oman indicates that X is predominantly used by males 
which means that the online data largely reflected male perspectives. Although the diary reports 
broadened the range of voices included in the study, future research using social media should 
consider platforms with more a more gender-balanced user base to obtain a better picture of public 
evaluations.  
Moreover, due to the predominance of male voices in the online data, and the fact that this study 
only focused on Omani nationals, it does not capture how other less represented groups such as 
women, expatriate workers or less advantaged members of the community, experience and evaluate 
offence and incivility. Therefore, future research should explore what these marginalised groups 
evaluate as offensive or uncivil, and how their experiences may differ.  
While the previously mentioned limitations above relate to both the X dataset, and the overall focus 
of the study, the diary reports introduced a different set of methodological constraints.  The diary 
reports provided valuable insights into participants’ personal experiences but relied on participants’ 
memory and willingness to share information that might be considered uncomfortable. This may have 
influenced how much detail participants chose to disclose, especially when describing sensitive or 
emotionally charged situations. In some cases, as I highlighted in the methodology chapter (see 
Chapter 3, Section 3.8.4), the participants skipped the open- ended ‘why’ questions, which were 
central to understanding the reasons behind their evaluation of offence or incivility. As a result, some 
responses lacked sufficient explanation, making it difficult to identify the underlying norms and 
expectations that shaped participants’ judgements. Furthermore, the nature of the diary report, being 
a written form of data collection, does not allow for real-time follow-up, which naturally limits the 
ability to explore participants’ responses in greater depth. Further research could consider using 
188 
 
focus groups or interviews to allow for deeper exploration, especially to better understand the 
reasoning behind their judgements.  
A further limitation of this study was posed by the lack of literature on incivility within the 
(im)politeness field. While the study aimed to investigate incivility as a socially and culturally situated 
judgement, it was challenging to ground the analysis of incivility in a well-established body of work 
within (im)politeness research. This was due to the fact that only a small number of studies in this 
field have addressed incivility directly, which limited the extent to which the findings could be situated 
within existing work. Nevertheless, the study was able to draw on a small number of relevant 
contributions, such as Zhang et al.'s (2021) work on Chinese tourists’ reflections on uncivil behaviour. 
Although this study is not situated within (im)politeness research, it offered useful insights into how 
incivility is evaluated through the lens of social identity and collective image, both of which were 
central to the findings of the present study. Future research could contribute to addressing this gap 
by more fully integrating incivility into the scope of (im)politeness studies. This includes examining 
incivility as a transgression of sociocultural norms or moral norms or a combination of both, 
depending on the reasoning evoked by the participants. Moreover, future research should expand 
the evaluative-based approach that I adopted in this study, which focuses on how individuals 
interpret and judge behaviours as uncivil or inappropriate based on sociocultural norms and 
expectations, rather than treating the behaviours themselves as inherently uncivil.

Appendix B – Participation Information 
Section A: The Research Project 
14. Title of project 
Taking offence and incivility in the Omani society 
15. Brief summary of research. 
This research aims to unveil the norms of impoliteness and incivility in the Omani society. 
16. Name of your Supervisor 
Dr.Vahid Parvaresh 
17. Why have I been asked to participate? 
You have been invited to take part in this study because you are an Omani citizen and because 
you have a good knowledge of the Omani society . 
18. How many people will be asked to participate? 
200 participants 
19. What are the likely benefits of taking part? 
There is no direct benefit to you as a participant, but it has potential to contribute to the study 
field by uncovering the various social, interpersonal and cognitive norms governing perceptions 
of incivility and offensiveness in Oman. 
20. Can I refuse to take part? 
Yes, you can refuse, but this has to be done before the thesis has been submitted 
21. Has the study got ethical approval? 
This study has been looked at and granted ethical approval by the Faculty Research Ethics Panel 
at Anglia Ruskin University to protect your interest. 
22. Has the organisation where you are carrying out the research given permission? 
Not relevant 
23. If your research falls under specific legislation e.g. the Human Tissue Act (2004), you 
Not relevant 
24. Source of funding for the research, if applicable. 
Not relevant 
25. What will happen to the results of the study? 
The anonymised version of the result will be used in my dissertation, and after the submission 
as well as passing the viva, data will be removed. 
26. Contact for further information 
Muhja Saif Al-hadi, 
Email: muhja.al-hadi@pgr.aru.ac.uk 
Dr.Vahid Parvaresh 
Email: vahid.parvaresh@anglia.ac.uk. 
206 
 
Section B: Your Participation in the Research Project (Diary report) 
 
1. What will I be asked to do? 
You will be asked to complete an online diary report which last around 10 minutes and contain 
an open-ended question related to your experience as an Omani citizen with taking offence and 
incivility. 
2. Will my participation in the study be kept confidential? 
Yes, it will be confidential and anonymised. The result will also be written up in anonymised 
format and no names will be mentioned All anonymous data will be stored in a password 
protected computer and will comply with GDPR and Data Protection Act (2018). 
3. Use of quotes. 
Yes, they will be used, but they will be anonymised, and no personal information will be made 
available. 
4. Use of recording equipment 
No, it won’t be used for the diary reports 
5. Will I be reimbursed travel expenses? 
The research does not require you to travel. 
6. Are there any possible disadvantages or risks to taking part? 
Since this research is about is about taking offence, you might find it inconvenient so you may 
withdraw at any time. 
7. Whether I can withdraw at any time, and how. 
If you do not wish to take part in the study, you are under no obligation to do so. You can withdraw 
any time, but you should inform me before the submission of the thesis is made. 
8. What will happen to any information/data/samples (delete as applicable) that are collected from 
you? 
Data will be stored in secure file and will comply with GDPR and Data Protection Act (2018). 
Nobody will have access to the data except me. The data will be deleted after the submission 
of the thesis and passing the viva. 
9. Summary of research findings 
I can send you a summary of the findings upon request. 
10. Contact details for complaints. 
If you have any complaints about the study, in the first instance, you should be encouraged to 
speak to me or my Supervisor Dr.Vahid vahid.parvaresh@anglia.ac.uk. Alternatively, please 
contact Anglia Ruskin University’s complaints at: Email address: complaints@anglia.ac.uk 
Postal address: Office of the Secretary and Clerk, Anglia Ruskin University, Bishop Hall Lane, 
Chelmsford, Essex, CM1 1SQ 
Date 24.10.16, V1.0 
 
 
207

(available on the research ethics website at www.aru.ac.uk/researchethics). 
Ethical approval is given for a period of 3 years for PGR students and staff from the date 
of this letter. 
 
If your research will extend beyond this period, it is your responsibility to apply for an 
extension before your approval expires. 
It is your responsibility to ensure that you comply with ARU’s Research Ethics Policy and 
the Code of Practice for Applying for Ethical Approval available at 
www.aru.ac.uk/researchethics, including the following: 
• Following the procedure for submitting amendments to the panel, should there be any substantial change to 
your research. You cannot implement substantial amendments to your research prior to receiving approval from 
the SREP. 
• Following the procedure for reporting accidents, adverse events and incidents. 
• Complying with the General Data Protection Requirement and Data Protection Act (2018). 
• Complying with any other legislation relevant to your research. You must also ensure that you are aware of any 
emerging legislation related to your research and that you make all relevant changes of legal compliance to your 
study (for which you will need to obtain ethical approval). 
• Obtaining any further ethical approval required from the organisation or country (if not carrying out research in the 
UK) where you will be carrying out the research. This includes other Higher Education Institutions, if you intend to 
carry out any research involving their students, staff or premises. Please ensure that you send copies of this
